title: Contextuality from the Projector Overlap Matrix
abstract: We place several known indicators of Kochen--Specker contextuality -- the KCBS correlator $χ$, the contextual fraction $\CF$, the Shannon-entropic $n$-cycle inequality of Chaves and Fritz, and the operational commutator witness $D$ of Paper~I -- into a single projector-geometric framework organized around the overlap matrix $\Tcal_{ij} = d^{-1}\tr[(\hat P_i \hat Q_j)^2]$, where $\hat P_i$ and $\hat Q_j$ are the joint-eigenspace projectors of the two compatible observable pairs within a measurement context. The state-independent scalar content of $\Tcal$ is carried by two independent contractions: the mutual information energy $E = \sum_{ij}\Tcal_{ij}$ of Paper~I (equivalently, its logarithmic form $S_2 = -\log_2 E$), and the Maassen--Uffink extremal overlap $c_\MU = \max_{i,j}|\langle a_i,b_i | c_j,b_j\rangle|$. We prove that $S_2$ is non-increasing under coarse-graining, that $S_2(\Gcal) > 0$ is a necessary configuration-level condition for observable contextuality, and that the additive composition $S_2(\Gcal) = \sum_αS_2(\Gcal_α)$ is exact for the KCBS pentagon. We further show that in the spin-$1$ realization of the KCBS pentagon, a shared $m_s=0$ eigenstate in each context forces $c_\MU = 1$, rendering every Maassen--Uffink-type bound trivial -- a structural mechanism that makes explicit why outcome-entropic uncertainty relations based on $c_\MU$ are silent on KCBS contextuality, while $S_2 \approx 2.7266$~bits throughout. Applied to KCBS and CHSH, the framework identifies regimes in which every state-dependent witness considered here is silent yet $S_2(\Gcal) > 0$ by an amount set by the projector geometry alone.
one_liner: A projector-overlap framework unifies several contextuality witnesses and isolates geometry-only signatures missed by standard state-dependent tests.
why_it_matters: It gives a compact, configuration-level diagnostic for contextuality that can guide photonic and other platform implementations even when usual witnesses are silent.
detailed_summary: The paper organizes multiple contextuality indicators around the projector overlap matrix T_ij = d^-1 tr[(P_i Q_j)^2], extracting two state-independent contractions: the mutual-information-like quantity S2 = -log2 E and the Maassen-Uffink extremal overlap c_MU. It proves S2 is non-increasing under coarse-graining, that S2(G) > 0 is necessary at the configuration level for observable contextuality, and that S2 composes additively for the KCBS pentagon. In the spin-1 KCBS realization, the authors show shared m_s=0 eigenstates force c_MU = 1, explaining why MU-type entropic bounds become trivial while S2 remains strongly nonzero at about 2.7266 bits. The framework also identifies KCBS and CHSH regimes where all considered state-dependent witnesses vanish but projector geometry still certifies nonclassical structure.
main_result: A single overlap-matrix formalism unifies several contextuality witnesses and reveals nonzero geometry-only contextuality signatures even when standard state-dependent witnesses are silent.
application_angle: The result can help design and benchmark contextuality experiments in integrated photonic and other quantum measurement platforms using state-independent geometric criteria.

title: Molecular-optomechanical phonon laser
abstract: Molecular cavity optomechanics (COM) leverages ultrastrong interactions between confined optical fields and high-frequency molecular vibration, providing a unique platform for exploring high-frequency phonon dynamics. In this work, we theoretically propose the use of a hybrid molecular COM system for realizing an ultra-low-threshold mid-infrared (MIR) phonon laser. Despite an optical quality factor of only $Q_a=100$, an ultra-low threshold power of $\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{th}} = 17.5~\mathrm{nW}$ is achieved, enabled by giant single-photon optomechanical coupling and molecular collective effect. Moreover, the mechanical gain and threshold power can be further tuned by adjusting the distance between mirrors of the Fabry-Pérot cavity. Our findings establish the first direct connection between molecular COM and MIR phonon lasers, with potential applications in MIR acoustics and biomedical imaging.
one_liner: A hybrid molecular optomechanical cavity is proposed as an ultra-low-threshold mid-IR phonon laser with nanowatt-scale pump power.
why_it_matters: If realized, it would provide a practical route to compact MIR coherent acoustic sources for spectroscopy, imaging, and on-chip phononics.
detailed_summary: The authors theoretically analyze a molecular cavity optomechanical system in which confined optical fields strongly couple to high-frequency molecular vibrations. Using giant single-photon optomechanical coupling plus collective molecular enhancement, they predict a mid-infrared phonon laser threshold of only 17.5 nW even with a modest optical cavity quality factor of Qa = 100. The model also shows that mirror spacing in a Fabry-Perot cavity can tune both mechanical gain and threshold power. This directly links molecular cavity optomechanics to MIR phonon lasing and identifies a realistic parameter knob for device optimization.
platform_or_system: hybrid molecular cavity optomechanical Fabry-Perot system
main_result: The work predicts an MIR phonon laser with a threshold pump power of 17.5 nW enabled by ultrastrong molecular optomechanical coupling and collective effects.
application_angle: Such a source could support compact MIR acoustic devices for biomedical imaging, spectroscopy, and chip-scale high-frequency phononic signal generation.

title: Lobe Dynamics, Phase-Space Transport, and Non-Adiabatic Leakage Thresholds in the Nonautonomous Kerr-Cat Qubit
abstract: The Kerr-nonlinear parametric oscillator (KPO) provides a foundational semiclassical model for cat-state quantum hardware. Standard analyses of the KPO typically rely on autonomous, frozen-time approximations to describe the stabilization of macroscopic coherent states. However, state preparation and gate manipulation are driven by explicitly time-dependent microwave pulses, so the operational dynamics are inherently nonautonomous. In this paper, we show that static algebraic equilibrium pictures are incomplete for describing both state formation and gate-induced transport in the Kerr-cat qubit. For nonautonomous state preparation, we analyze the ramped resonant model by combining a linear nonautonomous stability analysis with a local invariant-graph reduction near the vacuum trajectory. This yields a quintic reduced normal form in the critical direction and identifies two symmetric post-threshold moving branches that organize the local state-formation dynamics. The associated diagnostics separate the reduced branch dynamics from the full two-dimensional phase-twist relaxation observed in the hardware coordinates. For gate execution, we model a fast pulse as a weak aperiodic perturbation of the conservative resonant figure-eight separatrix and apply Melnikov's method to derive a leading-order transport criterion. In this framework, transient lobe dynamics emerge as a semiclassical mechanism for non-adiabatic leakage, and the resulting amplitude-width threshold curve provides a leading-order geometric indicator for the onset of gate-pulse-induced transport.
one_liner: This work derives geometric leakage thresholds for driven Kerr-cat qubits by analyzing nonautonomous phase-space transport during ramps and fast gates.
why_it_matters: It translates pulse-induced leakage in cat-qubit hardware into actionable amplitude-width thresholds for control design and error mitigation.
detailed_summary: The paper studies the explicitly time-dependent Kerr-nonlinear parametric oscillator rather than relying on frozen-time approximations. For state preparation, it combines nonautonomous stability analysis with invariant-graph reduction near the vacuum trajectory, yielding a quintic reduced normal form and two post-threshold moving branches that organize cat-state formation. For gate dynamics, it treats a fast pulse as a weak aperiodic perturbation of the resonant figure-eight separatrix and uses Melnikov theory to derive a leading-order transport criterion. The resulting lobe dynamics provide a semiclassical mechanism for nonadiabatic leakage and define amplitude-width threshold curves for when gate pulses start driving unwanted transport.
main_result: The authors derive phase-space transport criteria and pulse amplitude-width leakage thresholds for state preparation and gate execution in Kerr-cat hardware.
application_angle: These thresholds can inform pulse shaping, calibration, and safe operating envelopes in superconducting cat-qubit processors.

title: A Novel Hierarchy of Quantum Kernel Networks on Smoothed Particle Hydrodynamics
abstract: Currently, quantum computing and artificial intelligence are driving revolutionary advancements in computational science. This study pioneers the integration of quantum kernel networks on smoothed particle hydrodynamics (SPH). SPH has matured into a highly versatile meshfree/particle method, exceptionally suited for tracking spatiotemporal trajectories and dynamic modeling phenomena. We developed a hierarchy of Lagrangian quantum network models built upon an improved quantum multilayer perceptron (QMLP). Specifically, a sequential hybrid quantum-classical framework is constructed, utilizing Pauli-Z expectation values over traditional probability outputs to ensure robust gradient-based optimization and mitigate barren plateaus. It combines smoothing kernels with quantum learning, establishing a novel quantum intelligent SPH paradigm. The framework is validated through some continuous benchmarks on eurypalynous quantum neural networks, static multi-level nebula vortex interference reconstructions and transient scalar field advectional tests. Numerical results demonstrate that while pure elementary quantum circuits struggle with parameter-specific generalization in unstructured domains, the proposed hybrid crossed-QMLP seamlessly matches the fitting accuracy of classical SPH in quantum optimized space. Although this approach currently faces limitations in computational efficiency and hardware implementation, it nonetheless paves the way for a novel investigation into quantum SPH, by mapping unstructured Lagrangian particle topologies into integrated quantum circuits.
one_liner: A hybrid quantum-kernel neural hierarchy is built on smoothed particle hydrodynamics to learn unstructured Lagrangian particle dynamics.
why_it_matters: It sketches how quantum models could interface with meshfree scientific simulation pipelines, a step toward application-driven quantum ML workflows.
detailed_summary: The study proposes a hierarchy of Lagrangian quantum network models for SPH, using an improved quantum multilayer perceptron with Pauli-Z expectation outputs rather than raw probability outputs. That choice is intended to support gradient-based training and reduce barren-plateau behavior in a sequential hybrid quantum-classical setup. The framework is tested on continuous benchmarks, vortex-interference reconstructions, and transient scalar advection problems, where pure elementary quantum circuits underperform but the hybrid crossed-QMLP matches classical SPH fitting accuracy in a quantum-optimized representation. The authors note that efficiency and near-term hardware implementation remain major limitations.
main_result: The main result is that a hybrid crossed-QMLP can reproduce classical SPH fitting performance on unstructured particle problems better than simple pure-quantum circuit baselines.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for future quantum-accelerated scientific computing pipelines that must ingest irregular particle-based data rather than grid-structured inputs.

title: Dynamical generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured reservoirs
abstract: Two-mode squeezed states as paradigmatic entangled resources have broad applications in quantum information processing. Here, we study the generation of stable optical-microwave squeezing in structured environments within a hybrid electro-optomechanical system, where a mechanical oscillator is simultaneously coupled to an optical cavity mode and a microwave mode of an LC resonator. Specifically, an effective Hamiltonian that captures the optical-microwave squeezing interaction is constructed by combining strongly modulated driving fields applied to both photonic modes with a mechanical parametric amplifier. Based on this effective model, the dynamical evolution of two-mode squeezing in structured environments is analyzed. It is remarkably shown that the non-Markovian noise can substantially enhance the squeezing level in comparison to the Markovian case, and that two-mode squeezing can persist even in the absence of external driving fields under non-Markovian conditions, thereby mitigating the detrimental effects of anti-squeezing. Furthermore, the persistence of the two-mode squeezed state is enhanced when the environmental spectral densities of the microwave and optical modes are identical. Our work provides a theoretical framework for generating and persisting two-mode squeezing in structured environments.
one_liner: A hybrid electro-optomechanical scheme uses structured reservoirs to generate and preserve optical-microwave two-mode squeezing.
why_it_matters: It suggests non-Markovian environments can be engineered as a resource for persistent microwave-optical entanglement links and transduction networks.
detailed_summary: The paper considers a mechanical oscillator coupled simultaneously to an optical cavity and a microwave LC resonator, with strong modulation on both photonic modes plus a mechanical parametric amplifier to engineer an effective squeezing Hamiltonian. Within this model, the authors analyze the squeezing dynamics in structured, non-Markovian environments and find that non-Markovian noise can enhance squeezing relative to the Markovian case. They further show that squeezing can persist even after external drives are removed, and that persistence improves when the optical and microwave reservoir spectral densities are matched. The result reframes reservoir structure as a control parameter for stable hybrid entanglement resources.
main_result: The work demonstrates theoretically that structured non-Markovian reservoirs can enhance and sustain optical-microwave two-mode squeezing, even without continuous driving.
application_angle: This is useful for quantum networks and microwave-optical interfaces where long-lived entangled resources are needed despite dissipation.

title: Witnessing entanglement between photon and matter due to graviton exchange
abstract: The paper presents a scheme to detect entanglement arising from the quantum nature of gravity between a spin qubit and photons, using Stokes parameters. One of the crucial tests of the general theory of relativity is the bending of light due to the curvature. Recently, a quantum counterpart of this experiment to test the quantum nature of the gravitational interaction has been proposed, in which the spin-2, massless graviton yields entanglement between matter and a photon sector. Hence, it provides one of the most crucial experimental signatures for testing the quantum nature of gravity in a lab, since only spin-2-induced entanglement can yield the correct deflection of light due to matter. Here, we propose a positive partial-transpose (PPT) witness criterion for witnessing such an entanglement. We scan the entangled states in this context by studying the overlap of the final state, which is proportional to the entanglement phase. We exploit the Stokes observables to measure the photon state and the spins in the matter sector, thereby constructing a witness for the quantum nature of gravity in this setup. To quantify this entanglement, we will couple the photon to a local oscillator, whose phase need to be controlled to probe the orthogonal components of the macroscopic interference in the laser beam. We have shown that for a non-maximally entangled state mediated by the quantum nature of gravity, the witness attains a maximal negativity of $-0.052$. Our findings indicate that this witness effectively detects entanglement within the range $0.71 \leq |γ| < 1$, where $γ$ is the overlap between the two coherent states of the photon, providing a clear signature of quantum correlations.
one_liner: A PPT entanglement witness based on Stokes measurements is proposed for photon-matter entanglement generated by graviton exchange.
why_it_matters: It turns speculative quantum-gravity signatures into a concrete measurement protocol with observable witness ranges and detector requirements.
detailed_summary: The authors propose an experimentally motivated witness for matter-photon entanglement arising from the quantum nature of gravity in light-bending-style setups. The protocol combines Stokes-parameter measurements on the photon with spin measurements on the matter sector and uses a positive-partial-transpose criterion to certify entanglement. A local oscillator with controlled phase is included to probe orthogonal interference components of the photonic coherent state. The witness reaches a maximal negativity of -0.052 for the modeled non-maximally entangled state and detects entanglement over the overlap range 0.71 <= |gamma| < 1.
platform_or_system: spin qubit coupled to photonic coherent states
main_result: The paper provides a Stokes-observable PPT witness that can detect graviton-mediated photon-matter entanglement for coherent-state overlaps satisfying 0.71 <= |gamma| < 1.
application_angle: It offers a concrete readout strategy for lab-scale quantum-gravity tests by mapping an abstract entanglement signature onto measurable optical and spin observables.

title: Collective Strong Coupling of Thermal Atoms to Integrated Microring Resonators
abstract: Strong coupling between atomic ensembles and high-quality optical cavities enables collective and nonlinear phenomena that are central to cavity quantum electrodynamics (cQED). Although many experiments have been performed on this topic, most of them have focused on cold atoms. Here, we experimentally demonstrate collective strong coupling between thermal rubidium (Rb) vapor and high-quality silicon nitride microring resonators (MRRs) on an integrated photonic chip. We observe cavity mode splitting, with a measured collective coupling strength of $g_N/2π\approx 1\,\mathrm{GHz}$ and a collective cooperativity of $C_N\approx2$ at $110\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, indicating coherent energy exchange between the atomic ensemble and the cavity mode despite rapid decoherence in the thermal vapor system. We infer an average of $20$ atoms participating in the collective interaction, yielding a single-atom cooperativity of $C_0=0.1$ and approaching the single-atom strong-coupling regime. Our results establish the integrated thermal vapor MRR platform as a robust, compact, and scalable system for studying collective and nonlinear phenomena in cQED.
one_liner: Thermal Rb vapor collectively strongly couples to integrated SiN microrings, showing GHz-scale normal-mode splitting on chip.
why_it_matters: It brings cavity-QED-level light-matter coupling to a compact thermal-vapor photonic platform that is far easier to scale than cold-atom systems.
detailed_summary: The work experimentally demonstrates collective strong coupling between thermal rubidium vapor and high-Q silicon nitride microring resonators integrated on a photonic chip. The authors observe cavity mode splitting with collective coupling gN/2pi of about 1 GHz and collective cooperativity near 2 at 110 C, indicating coherent atom-cavity exchange despite fast thermal-vapor decoherence. From the inferred participation of roughly 20 atoms, they estimate single-atom cooperativity C0 = 0.1, approaching the single-atom strong-coupling regime in an integrated device.
platform_or_system: thermal Rb vapor coupled to SiN microring resonators
main_result: An integrated thermal-vapor microring device achieved collective strong coupling with ~1 GHz splitting and collective cooperativity around 2.
application_angle: This platform could enable compact chip-scale nonlinear quantum optics, cavity QED studies, and scalable atom-photon interfaces without cold-atom overhead.

title: Noise in analog programmable-photonic computation
abstract: Analog Programmable-Photonic Computation (APC) leverages programmable integrated photonics (PIP) to perform high-speed matrix operations using optical waves. However, the continuous nature of optical waves that implement the analog bits or anbits - the fundamental unit of information in APC - makes computational results intrinsically sensitive to physical noise. Here, we establish and experimentally validate a comprehensive noise analysis in APC platforms using the geometric representation of the anbit in the Generalized Bloch Sphere (GBS). By modeling the physical noise sources in PIP circuits as random photocurrent fluctuations at the output of the opto-electrical (O/E) converter, and using error propagation theory, the noise statistics can be projected onto the GBS. This approach leads to specific noise maps in the GBS for each noise source, enabling the identification of the dominant noise sources within the APC system. Analytical predictions are numerically and experimentally validated on a fabricated silicon PIP chip. Beyond statistical characterization of system noise, the proposed model provides quantitative design criteria for noise-adapted analog constellations in the GBS, advancing APC towards scalable and robust optical computing systems, with potential applications in emerging paradigms such as photonic neuromorphic computing.
one_liner: A generalized Bloch-sphere noise framework quantifies and maps noise sources in analog programmable photonic computing chips.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical route to diagnose dominant hardware noise and design more robust optical analog encodings for scalable photonic computing.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a comprehensive noise model for analog programmable-photonic computation by representing optical anbits on a generalized Bloch sphere. Physical noise in programmable integrated photonic circuits is modeled as random photocurrent fluctuations at the optoelectronic readout, then propagated analytically into geometry-dependent noise maps over the state space. The framework is validated numerically and experimentally on a fabricated silicon photonic chip, allowing attribution of performance limits to specific noise sources. It also yields quantitative criteria for selecting noise-adapted analog constellations.
main_result: The authors experimentally validate a geometric noise-projection method that identifies dominant APC noise sources and guides robust analog state design.
application_angle: Better noise-aware encoding and diagnosis directly support more reliable photonic matrix engines for AI, signal processing, and neuromorphic accelerators.

title: GSC-QEMit: A Telemetry-Driven Hierarchical Forecast-and-Bandit Framework for Adaptive Quantum Error Mitigation
abstract: Quantum error mitigation (QEM) is essential for extracting reliable results from near-term quantum devices, yet practical deployments must balance mitigation strength against runtime overhead under time-varying noise. We introduce \emph{GSC-QEMit}, a telemetry-driven, \textbf{context--forecast--bandit} framework for \emph{adaptive} mitigation that switches between lightweight suppression and heavier intervention as drift evolves. GSC-QEMit composes three coupled modules: (G) a Growing Hierarchical Self-Organizing Map (GHSOM) that clusters streaming telemetry into operating contexts; (S) an uncertainty-aware subsampled Gaussian-process forecaster that predicts short-horizon fidelity degradation; and (C) a cost-aware contextual multi-armed bandit (CMAB) that selects mitigation actions via Thompson sampling with explicit intervention cost. We evaluate GSC-QEMit on benchmark circuit families (GHZ, Quantum Fourier Transform, and Grover search) under nonstationary noise regimes simulated in Qiskit Aer, using an instrumented testbed where action labels correspond to graded mitigation intensity. Across Clifford, non-Clifford, and structured workloads, GSC-QEMit improves average logical fidelity by \textbf{+9.0\%} relative to unmitigated execution while reducing unnecessary heavy interventions by reserving them for inferred noise spikes. The resulting policies exhibit a favorable fidelity--cost trade-off and transfer across the evaluated workloads without circuit-specific tuning.
one_liner: A telemetry-driven forecast-and-bandit controller adaptively schedules quantum error mitigation under drifting noise.
why_it_matters: Adaptive mitigation is essential for useful near-term hardware because fixed policies either waste runtime or miss transient noise spikes.
detailed_summary: GSC-QEMit combines three modules: a growing hierarchical self-organizing map for online context clustering, an uncertainty-aware subsampled Gaussian-process model for short-horizon fidelity forecasting, and a cost-aware contextual bandit for mitigation selection. Evaluated in Qiskit Aer on GHZ, QFT, and Grover workloads under nonstationary noise, the controller switches between lighter and heavier mitigation actions based on inferred device state. Across circuit classes, it improves average logical fidelity by 9.0% over unmitigated execution while reducing unnecessary expensive interventions. The policy transfers across workloads without circuit-specific tuning.
platform_or_system: adaptive QEM controller for near-term quantum processors
main_result: The framework improves average logical fidelity by 9.0% versus no mitigation while using heavy mitigation only when predicted noise warrants it.
application_angle: It points to deployable runtime orchestration for cloud quantum processors where latency, cost, and noise drift must be balanced automatically.

title: Balancing Quantum Memories in Asymmetric Repeaters for High-Fidelity Entanglement Distribution
abstract: At the core of the quantum Internet lie quantum repeaters that enable remote end-to-end entanglement generation. Fundamentally, the entanglement generation rate and fidelity of quantum repeaters constitute the bottleneck for end-to-end performance. To achieve high rates, quantum repeaters employ quantum memory multiplexing. In a high-rate standard repeater, each memory sequentially generates an entanglement with its neighboring nodes and then applies entanglement swapping. This, however, results in low fidelity due to decoherence of the first-formed entanglement in the sequential generation process. By allocating different numbers of memories to simultaneously form entanglements with the left and right adjacent nodes, quantum repeaters reduce high waiting times and achieve high fidelity. In such a repeater, a mismatch problem arises due to the difference between the probabilistic number of generated entanglements on both sides. Consequently, some entanglements remain stored until opposite entanglements are available. The mismatch problem reduces the repeater rate and particularly the entanglement fidelity. In this paper, we consider the mismatch problem in an asymmetric repeater with different distances to its adjacent nodes. To mitigate the mismatch problem, we derive a dynamic optimal memory allocation. Under the optimal allocation, we derive statistical lower bounds on the achievable rate and fidelity. We demonstrate that the optimal allocation significantly improves the fidelity while maintaining a comparable rate to the standard repeater. In contrast, our results show that fixed memory allocation may be detrimental to the fidelity.
one_liner: Dynamic memory allocation in asymmetric repeaters boosts entanglement fidelity while preserving useful repeater rates.
why_it_matters: Quantum networks need repeater protocols that handle asymmetric links and memory decoherence without sacrificing throughput.
detailed_summary: The paper studies the mismatch problem in multiplexed quantum repeaters when left and right link distances differ, causing unequal entanglement generation statistics and extra storage time. The authors derive a dynamic optimal memory-allocation strategy that balances simultaneous entanglement attempts across the two sides to reduce waiting-induced decoherence. They also derive statistical lower bounds on achievable rate and fidelity under the optimal policy. Results show substantial fidelity gains compared with standard or fixed-allocation strategies while maintaining comparable entanglement rates.
platform_or_system: asymmetric multiplexed quantum repeater with quantum memories
main_result: An optimal dynamic memory-allocation rule mitigates left-right mismatch, significantly improving distributed-entanglement fidelity at roughly comparable rates.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant to practical repeater control in heterogeneous fiber networks where link asymmetry is unavoidable.

title: Isotopically enriched epitaxial CaWO$_{4}$ thin films for Er$^{3+}$ spin-photon quantum interfaces
abstract: Rare earth ion (REI)-doped oxide thin films are attractive for the application of quantum interconnects due to their stable optical levels and scalability$^{1-3}$. Among them, Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ is promising because it possesses narrow optical linewidth transitions and a long spin coherence time$^{4-6}$. The electron spin coherence is limited at high temperatures by paramagnetic impurities and by the presence of the 14.3% $^{183}$W nuclear spin. To further increase the spin coherence time at millikelvin temperatures, where the paramagnetic impurities are frozen out, our approach is to synthesize chemically and isotopically purified thin films as a host material. We first grow non-isotopically enriched Er$^{3+}$ doped CaWO$_{4}$ thin films, which exhibit a 214(13) MHz photoluminescence (PL) inhomogeneous linewidth, indicating the thin film has high crystalline quality. We then grow isotopically enriched CaWO$_{4}$ thin films using an isotopically purified $^{186}$WO$_{3}$ source. Time of flight secondary ion mass spectrometry (ToF-SIMS) was used to measure the relative concentration of W isotopes. $^{183}$W, the only W isotope that has a net nuclear spin and is the major cause of spin decoherence, was at a relative abundance of 1.2%, a factor of 10 lower than natural abundance. We also observed PL emission from single ions after integrating nano-photonic devices with the thin film. These results establish isotopically engineered CaWO$_{4}$ thin films as a promising platform for future studies of nuclear-spin-limited coherence and for scalable rare-earth-ion-based quantum nanophotonic devices.
one_liner: Isotopically purified Er:CaWO4 thin films suppress nuclear-spin impurities and support single-ion photonic integration.
why_it_matters: Materials engineering at the isotope level can extend spin coherence and make scalable rare-earth quantum interfaces more viable.
detailed_summary: The authors grow Er3+-doped epitaxial CaWO4 thin films and then realize isotopically enriched films using a purified 186WO3 precursor to suppress the spinful 183W isotope. The baseline films show a 214(13) MHz photoluminescence inhomogeneous linewidth, indicating good crystalline quality, while ToF-SIMS confirms the 183W abundance is reduced to 1.2%, about ten times below natural abundance. After integrating nanophotonic devices, they observe photoluminescence from single ions. The results establish a path toward coherence-limited-by-design rare-earth thin-film quantum interfaces.
platform_or_system: Er3+:CaWO4 isotopically enriched epitaxial thin films
main_result: The team fabricated Er:CaWO4 thin films with 183W reduced to 1.2% and demonstrated single-ion photoluminescence in integrated nanophotonic structures.
application_angle: This is a strong materials platform for wafer-scale spin-photon interfaces, quantum interconnects, and integrated rare-earth quantum memories.

title: DiffQEC: A versatile diffusion model for quantum error correction
abstract: Quantum computers could solve problems beyond the reach of classical devices, but this potential depends on quantum error correction (QEC) to protect fragile quantum states from noise. A central challenge in QEC is decoding: inferring likely physical errors from syndrome patterns generated by repeated stabilizer measurements. Existing decoders, including graph-based and neural approaches, typically return a single correction hypothesis and therefore discard the richer posterior structure of the error distribution conditioned on the observed syndrome. Here we recast QEC decoding as posterior inference using discrete denoising diffusion, exploiting the analogy between stochastic error accumulation and the forward diffusion process. We introduce DiffQEC, a generative decoder that combines a syndrome processor for multi-round spatial-temporal syndrome histories with syndrome feature modulation to condition denoising on the observed syndrome throughout inference. On experimental data from Google's superconducting quantum processor, DiffQEC reduces logical error rates by up to 10.2% relative to minimum-weight perfect matching and by about 5% relative to tensor-network decoding. These improvements persist for larger code distances up to 17 under depolarizing noise and for logical circuits of increasing depth. Beyond accuracy, the learned posterior provides confidence estimates for post-selection and reveals physically meaningful error structure, establishing posterior generative decoding as a practical framework for QEC.
one_liner: A diffusion-based generative decoder improves QEC performance on superconducting-processor data and exposes posterior uncertainty.
why_it_matters: Better decoders directly lower logical error rates, and posterior-aware decoding adds confidence estimates useful for practical fault-tolerant control.
detailed_summary: DiffQEC reformulates quantum error-correction decoding as posterior inference with a discrete denoising diffusion model conditioned on multi-round space-time syndrome histories. Its architecture uses a syndrome processor plus syndrome feature modulation so conditioning persists throughout denoising and sampling. On experimental data from Google's superconducting processor, it lowers logical error rates by up to 10.2% versus minimum-weight perfect matching and about 5% versus tensor-network decoding. The advantage remains for code distances up to 17 under depolarizing noise, and the learned posterior provides confidence information for post-selection.
platform_or_system: superconducting-qubit QEC decoder for Google processor data
main_result: DiffQEC achieves up to 10.2% lower logical error rate than MWPM on experimental superconducting-device data while also producing posterior confidence estimates.
application_angle: Posterior generative decoding could improve real-time control stacks for scalable fault-tolerant superconducting quantum computers.

title: Optical depth dictates universal bounds on many-body decay in atomic ensembles
abstract: Cooperative emission is well understood for idealized symmetric systems, but its limits in spatially extended, free-space ensembles remain an open question. Here, we derive a universal law for the scaling of the maximum photon emission rate with system size that unifies both ordered arrays and disordered atomic clouds in arbitrary dimensions at fixed density. We demonstrate that, for a fixed atomic density, the maximum emission rate scales universally as the product of the atom number and the system's optical depth, with the latter encoding the dimensional scaling across all regimes from independent emission to the Dicke limit. Furthermore, we establish a scaling law for directional detection, revealing that the observed rate depends on the detector's numerical aperture: small apertures yield Dicke-like quadratic scaling, whereas large apertures recover our integrated universal bound. Our results establish optical depth as the parameter governing many-body cooperative emission in both ordered and disordered ensembles, and reveal that directional and total-emission scalings must be carefully distinguished in experimental settings.
one_liner: Derives a universal free-space scaling law showing peak cooperative emission is set by atom number times optical depth.
why_it_matters: It gives an experimentally usable figure of merit for collective light emission and clarifies how detector aperture changes observed superradiant scaling.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a universal bound for the maximum photon emission rate in spatially extended free-space atomic ensembles at fixed density. It shows that the peak many-body decay rate scales as atom number times optical depth, unifying ordered arrays and disordered clouds across arbitrary dimensionality. The analysis also separates integrated emission from directional detection, showing that small numerical aperture can recover Dicke-like quadratic scaling while wide collection follows the universal optical-depth bound. This provides a concrete framework for interpreting collective-emission experiments and designing high-brightness atomic-light interfaces.
main_result: The maximum cooperative emission rate is shown to scale universally as N times optical depth for fixed-density ensembles, with aperture-dependent directional deviations.
application_angle: The result helps optimize atomic quantum network nodes, memories, and light-matter interfaces by identifying optical depth as the key design parameter.

title: Singlet-triplet oscillations in multivalley Si double quantum dots
abstract: Charge separation from the $(4,0)$ to the $(3,1)$ state in a Si/SiGe double quantum dot is commonly used for initialization of spin qubits and Pauli-spin-blockade readout. It was used in recent experiments involving creation of the $(3,1)$ singlet, and subsequent shuttling of one of the electrons. We present a theoretical description of the process of charge separation and singlet-triplet mixing, arriving at expressions for the singlet return probability that take into account experimentally observed finite probabilities of the creation of singlets with various patterns of valley occupations. In our analysis we focus on magnetic fields for which the electron spin Zeeman splitting is close to the valley splitting in one of the dots, when the spin-valley coupling causes a strong renormalization of the frequency of oscillations of singlet return probability. The latter effect has been recently used to perform valley splitting mapping by shuttling of one quantum dot to various locations with respect to the other. We give a detailed description of singlet-triplet dynamics near these spin-valley resonances and compare the results of calculations with measurements on double quantum dots in two distinct Si/SiGe heterostructures. Comparison of theory with experiments in which the presence of a few valley occupation patterns is visible, gives insight into the valley dependence of $g$-factors in these structures, providing support for a recently proposed theoretical model of this dependence. We also discuss how dephasing of singlet return probability oscillations near the spin-valley resonances is affected by valley splitting fluctuations caused by electric field noise.
one_liner: Models singlet-triplet oscillations in Si/SiGe double dots near spin-valley resonances and matches measured return-probability dynamics.
why_it_matters: It sharpens how valley physics, g-factor variation, and noise affect spin-qubit initialization, readout, and shuttling-based calibration.
detailed_summary: This work develops a theory of charge separation from (4,0) to (3,1) and the resulting singlet-triplet mixing in multivalley silicon double quantum dots. The model includes finite preparation probabilities for multiple valley-occupation patterns and focuses on the regime where Zeeman and valley splittings become comparable, causing strong spin-valley-induced frequency renormalization. Calculations are compared with experiments in two Si/SiGe heterostructures, giving evidence for valley-dependent g-factors and clarifying dephasing from electric-field-driven valley-splitting fluctuations. The treatment directly connects measurable return-probability oscillations to microscopic valley physics relevant for spin control.
main_result: The theory quantitatively explains singlet return oscillations near spin-valley resonance and links the observed frequencies and dephasing to valley occupations, g-factor shifts, and noise.
application_angle: It improves calibration and error diagnosis for silicon spin-qubit devices, especially for initialization, Pauli-blockade readout, and electron shuttling workflows.

title: The Legacy of Enrico Fermi to Varenna
abstract: The Varenna school is a hub where generations of physicists, including numerous Nobel laureates, have shaped the field, often through collaborative exchanges across political and cultural boundaries. We examine the scientific legacy of Enrico Fermi and its influence on modern atomic, molecular, and optical physics. Beginning with Fermi's 1954 lectures at the Varenna school, key developments are traced from high-energy physics to laser spectroscopy, precision metrology, and the control of ultracold atoms. Milestones such as Doppler-free spectroscopy, optical frequency combs, Bose-Einstein condensation, and degenerate Fermi gases are highlighted as turning points leading to quantum simulation and quantum computation. Fermi's early advocacy for building a computer, rather than buying it, can be viewed as a precursor to today's efforts in quantum science and technologies. This historical trajectory and legacy continues to inform current research in quantum matter and information science.
one_liner: A historical review traces Fermi's influence from early AMO ideas to precision metrology, ultracold atoms, and quantum technologies.
why_it_matters: It maps the conceptual lineage behind modern sensing and quantum platforms, highlighting how foundational AMO advances enabled current applications.
detailed_summary: The article is a perspective on the scientific legacy of Enrico Fermi and the Varenna school, following a path from mid-century physics to modern AMO and quantum science. It highlights key milestones including Doppler-free spectroscopy, optical frequency combs, Bose-Einstein condensation, and degenerate Fermi gases, emphasizing how they reshaped precision measurement and quantum control. Rather than presenting a new experiment, it synthesizes the technical evolution that connects spectroscopy and ultracold-atom advances to quantum simulation and computation. Its value is contextual and field-defining rather than a new hardware or sensing demonstration.
main_result: The paper synthesizes how Fermi-linked developments in spectroscopy, metrology, and ultracold matter seeded major modern quantum-science capabilities.
application_angle: It is useful as a roadmap for researchers entering precision sensing and AMO-based quantum technology, though it is not itself a new experimental advance.

title: Gate-dependent offset charge shifts and anharmonicity in gatemon qubits in the weak tunneling regime
abstract: Gatemon qubits are based on a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor (S-QD-S) junction which enables in situ electrostatic tuning via a gate electrode. For a single-channel QD this structure gives rise to two subgap Andreev bound states (ABSs), and generally leads to a richer quantum phase dynamics as compared to conventional transmons. In a recent work [Phys. Rev. B 111, 214503 (2025)] we derived the quantum phase dynamics from a many-body treatment which leads to an effective gate voltage-dependent Hamiltonian that self-consistently incorporates the phase quantization. It predicts (i) a renormalization of the junction's effective capacitance and (ii) the presence of gate voltage and occupation-dependent charge offsets in junctions with tunneling asymmetry. Here, we quantify the observable impact of these effects on the qubit's energy spectrum and anharmonicity, by studying the interplay of the two Andreev branches as a function of dot-gate voltages and junction transparencies. We show the relation of these predictions to simplified gatemon models and propose a protocol to experimentally detect the predicted charge offsets.
one_liner: Analyzes how gate-tuned Andreev physics shifts offset charge and anharmonicity in weak-tunneling gatemon qubits.
why_it_matters: It identifies measurable nonidealities unique to gatemons and proposes a route to detect them, informing qubit design and operating-point selection.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a superconductor-quantum dot-superconductor gatemon in the weak-tunneling regime using a many-body phase-dynamics framework. The theory predicts gate- and occupation-dependent offset charges in asymmetric junctions together with a renormalized effective capacitance arising from the Andreev subgap branches. It then quantifies how these effects reshape the qubit spectrum and anharmonicity across gate voltages and transparencies, and relates them to simplified gatemon models. An experimental protocol is proposed to directly detect the predicted charge offsets.
main_result: Gate-controlled Andreev-branch physics is shown to produce observable offset-charge shifts and anharmonicity changes in gatemon spectra, with a proposed detection protocol.
application_angle: The results guide fabrication and biasing of gatemon qubits for improved controllability, spectroscopy interpretation, and potentially better coherence-performance tradeoffs.

title: CAbLECAR: efficiently scheduling QLDPC codes on a tileable spin qubit chip with shuttling
abstract: Semiconductor spin qubits are a promising platform for large-scale quantum computing, but have yet to take full advantage of the broad class of quantum low-density parity check (QLDPC) codes, which promise high encoding rates and efficient logic but require nonlocal connectivity between physical qubits. In this work, we investigate the implementation of QLDPC codes on a tileable, shuttling-based spin qubit architecture. By tailoring syndrome extraction circuits to the shuttling noise model, we significantly improve on previous surface code proposals and extend the feasible shuttling range of the architecture by 5-10x, enabling the implementation of more complex codes with long-range interactions. Taking inspiration from the field of robotics, we develop a coordinated shuttle scheduling algorithm that supports arbitrary codes and use it to benchmark the logical performance of a variety of promising code families. We find that the optimized schedules are up to 86% faster than hand-optimized schedules for certain code families. Through detailed circuit-level simulations, we identify specific QLDPC codes that improve upon prior surface code implementations by orders of magnitude, increasing encoding efficiency and reducing logical error rates. This work demonstrates the potential of shuttling-based spin qubit hardware platforms for scalable and efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.
one_liner: Introduces a coordinated shuttling scheduler that makes QLDPC syndrome extraction practical on tileable spin-qubit chips.
why_it_matters: It turns nonlocal code layouts into a hardware-aware control problem and shows substantial speed and logical-performance gains over surface-code baselines.
detailed_summary: This work maps quantum low-density parity-check codes onto a tileable semiconductor spin-qubit architecture with electron shuttling. The authors tailor syndrome-extraction circuits to the shuttling noise model and develop a coordinated shuttle scheduler inspired by robotics that supports arbitrary code graphs. Benchmarks show schedules up to 86% faster than hand-optimized alternatives and extend feasible shuttling distances by roughly 5-10x. Circuit-level simulations identify QLDPC families that can outperform earlier surface-code proposals by orders of magnitude in encoding efficiency and logical error rate.
main_result: Hardware-aware shuttling schedules enable efficient implementation of nonlocal QLDPC codes, with major runtime improvements and favorable logical-error performance.
application_angle: It directly supports scalable fault-tolerant spin-quantum-computer design by reducing the control overhead of long-range stabilizer extraction.

title: Experimental high-dimensional multi-qubit Bell non-locality on a superconducting quantum processor
abstract: Combining recent advances in superconducting quantum hardware, we explore quantum correlations in a previously inaccessible regime by observing \emph{simultaneously} high-dimensional and many-body Bell non-locality. We report a high-confidence Bell violation in the correlations between two $d=64$-dimensional systems encoded in twelve qubits. For system sizes up to $d=32$, the strength of the observed nonlocal correlations exceeds the quantum upper bound for $d=2$ systems, providing direct evidence of high-dimensional nonlocality. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the observed violation is genuinely collective: all qubits contribute to the nonlocal correlations, while most pairwise correlations across the bipartition remain Bell-local. Our work illustrates how present-day quantum processors enable the exploration of fundamental predictions of quantum mechanics in previously inaccessible regimes and, in turn, how fundamental quantum effects can be used to benchmark their performance.
one_liner: Demonstrates collective high-dimensional Bell nonlocality by encoding two 64-dimensional systems across 12 superconducting qubits.
why_it_matters: It uses current superconducting hardware to probe genuinely many-body, high-dimensional correlations while also providing a demanding system-level benchmark.
detailed_summary: The experiment encodes two d=64 subsystems into a 12-qubit superconducting processor and measures a statistically significant Bell violation in that high-dimensional setting. For sizes up to d=32, the observed nonlocal correlations exceed the quantum limit attainable by two-dimensional systems, giving direct evidence of genuinely high-dimensional nonlocality. The study also shows the violation is collective, with all qubits contributing even though most pairwise cross-partition correlations remain Bell-local. This positions Bell tests as both a foundational probe and a hardware benchmark for multiqubit processors.
main_result: A 12-qubit superconducting device experimentally realizes simultaneous many-body and high-dimensional Bell nonlocality, including a violation for two encoded d=64 systems.
application_angle: The protocol offers a stringent benchmark for coherent multiqubit control and supports future quantum-networking and qudit-processing schemes that exploit high-dimensional entanglement.

title: Emergent prethermal Bethe integrability in a periodically driven Rydberg chain
abstract: We study a chain of periodically driven Rydberg atoms and identify a class of drive protocols for which the system exhibits emergent prethermal Bethe integrability at special drive frequencies. We provide a perturbative analytic expression of its Floquet Hamiltonian in the large drive amplitude regime. We demonstrate integrability of the leading term of this Floquet Hamiltonian at special drive frequencies, which we identify, by mapping it to the Hamiltonian of the paradigmatic spin-$1/2$ ${\rm XXZ}$ chain. We support our analytical results by exact diagonalization studies on finite chains. Our numerical results on level statistics, half-chain entanglement entropy, and longitudinal magnetization of the driven chain brings out its emergent integrable nature at the special drive frequencies which persists up to a large prethermal timescale.
one_liner: A driven Rydberg chain is shown to enter a long-lived prethermal regime with emergent XXZ-type Bethe integrability at special drive frequencies.
why_it_matters: It identifies a controllable route to engineer integrable many-body dynamics in neutral-atom hardware, useful for benchmarking Floquet control and nonequilibrium quantum simulation.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a periodically driven Rydberg atom chain in the large-drive-amplitude regime and derives a perturbative Floquet Hamiltonian. At special drive frequencies, the leading Floquet term maps onto the spin-1/2 XXZ chain, implying emergent Bethe integrability over a long prethermal window. Exact diagonalization on finite systems supports this via level statistics, half-chain entanglement entropy, and longitudinal magnetization, all consistent with integrable dynamics before eventual heating.
main_result: The authors identify special drive frequencies where a driven Rydberg chain realizes an XXZ-like integrable Floquet Hamiltonian with long-lived prethermal behavior.
application_angle: This gives neutral-atom experiments a concrete operating regime for controlled Floquet simulation, calibration against integrable dynamics, and studies of heating suppression.

title: Randomised measurements of a disorder-induced entanglement transition in a neutral atom quantum processor
abstract: The development and spread of entanglement in complex quantum systems is central to exploring many-body phenomena out of equilibrium. Measuring entanglement dynamics can shed light on information scrambling and thermalisation, namely on transitions from many-body quantum chaos to localisation in disordered, interacting systems. In quantum computing systems, entanglement entropy and other nonlinear functions of the density matrix have been recently measured, in particular by using the randomised measurement toolbox. However, it is difficult to implement the required arbitrary unitary rotations on specific subsystems without universal local control. Here we devise and demonstrate the measurement of entanglement entropy in a programmable analogue quantum simulator using a randomised measurement protocol that leverages local energy tuning together with a global field to bypass the need for local gate control. We implement this on a commercially available neutral-atom quantum simulator, QuEra's Aquila, and use it to show how programmable disorder in the local Hamiltonian parameters leads to a transition from chaotic to localised entanglement dynamics. Given current decoherence times, we clearly resolve disorder-specific, time-dependent entanglement spreading in small systems. Our work extends the utility of programmable analogue quantum simulators, and opens further opportunities for wider randomised measurement toolboxes in a range of other analogue systems.
one_liner: Randomized measurements on QuEra Aquila resolve a disorder-driven transition in entanglement dynamics without requiring arbitrary local gates.
why_it_matters: It expands entanglement-metrology tools for analog neutral-atom processors, enabling practical probes of scrambling and localization on existing hardware.
detailed_summary: The work develops a randomized-measurement protocol for analog neutral-atom devices that combines local energy tuning with a global field, avoiding the need for universal local unitary control on subsystems. Implemented on QuEra's Aquila processor, the method measures entanglement entropy during out-of-equilibrium dynamics in the presence of programmable disorder. The experiments resolve disorder-dependent, time-resolved entanglement spreading in small systems and reveal a transition from chaotic to localized behavior within current coherence limits.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate entanglement-entropy measurements on an analog neutral-atom processor and observe a disorder-induced crossover from chaotic to localized entanglement dynamics.
application_angle: The protocol turns current analog quantum simulators into more capable diagnostic platforms for benchmarking scrambling, localization, and control quality without full local gate sets.

title: Demonstration of quantum random number generation using nitrogen vacancy centres
abstract: Quantum random number generation (QRNG) relies on the inherent unpredictability of quantum mechanical phenomena to efficiently generate high-quality random numbers that can be used in a wide range of cryptography and simulation applications. Here we report the experimental demonstration of QRNG from the arrival times of photons emitted by nitrogen vacancy (NV) centres in fluorescent nanodiamonds. The generation rates achieved range from 0.173 Mbits/s for a region with a single NV centre to 4.77 Mbits/s for a region with just under 50 NV centres, where the latter demonstrates an order of magnitude improvement compared to the highest generation rate previously achieved with NV centres. For all the regions investigated, the generated bits passed the ENT and NIST Statistical Test Suites without post-processing. The results are consistent with our theoretical analysis, where we show that the min-entropy is very close to the ideal value of one per bit for all the regions investigated. This work opens up new possibilities for robust QRNG in highly compact on-chip settings.
one_liner: Photon arrival times from NV centers in nanodiamonds are used to generate high-quality quantum random numbers at up to 4.77 Mbits/s.
why_it_matters: It shows NV-center emitters can support compact, robust QRNG hardware with strong statistical performance and improved rates relevant to deployed photonic security devices.
detailed_summary: The experiment extracts random bits from photon arrival times emitted by nitrogen-vacancy centers hosted in fluorescent nanodiamonds. Reported generation rates range from 0.173 Mbits/s for a single-NV region to 4.77 Mbits/s for regions containing just under 50 NV centers, exceeding prior NV-based rates by about an order of magnitude at the high end. Across all tested regions, the raw output passes ENT and NIST statistical suites without post-processing, and the inferred min-entropy remains close to the ideal value of one bit per sample.
main_result: The paper demonstrates post-processing-free QRNG from NV-center photon timing with up to 4.77 Mbits/s throughput and near-ideal min-entropy.
application_angle: The approach is attractive for integrated quantum-secure hardware, especially compact on-chip random-number sources for cryptography and simulation systems.

title: Phase diagram of a dual-species Rydberg atom ladder
abstract: Dual-species Rydberg atom arrays extend single-species platforms by introducing competing interaction scales and enhanced quantum fluctuations, enabling phenomena beyond homogeneous settings. In this work, we study the ground-state phase diagram of a one-dimensional dual-species Rydberg atom ladder using large-scale density-matrix renormalization group calculations. We identify disordered phases, multiple ordered phases with $\mathbb{Z}_2$, $\mathbb{Z}_3$, and $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry, as well as floating phases characterized by incommensurate wave vectors and algebraically decaying correlations. Importantly, we observe a smooth crossover between distinct $\mathbb{Z}_2$-ordered regimes, reflecting a reorganization of low-energy degrees of freedom rather than a true phase transition, which is absent in single-species Rydberg arrays. We further uncover a multi-critical point at the boundary between the $\mathbb{Z}_2 \otimes \mathbb{Z}_2$ and $\mathbb{Z}_3 \otimes \mathbb{Z}_3$ ordered phases, where Ising, chiral, and first-order transition lines intersect. Our results demonstrate that dual-species Rydberg atom arrays provide a unique platform for realizing crossover physics and multi-critical behavior inaccessible in single-species architectures.
one_liner: Large-scale DMRG maps a rich phase diagram of a dual-species Rydberg ladder, including multiple ordered states, floating phases, and multicriticality.
why_it_matters: It provides concrete many-body targets unique to heterogeneous Rydberg arrays, informing how dual-species hardware can access physics unavailable in single-species platforms.
detailed_summary: Using density-matrix renormalization group calculations, the study determines the ground-state phase diagram of a one-dimensional dual-species Rydberg atom ladder. It finds disordered regimes, ordered phases with Z2, Z3, and Z4 symmetry, and floating phases with incommensurate wave vectors and algebraically decaying correlations. The authors also identify a smooth crossover between distinct Z2-ordered regimes and a multicritical point where Ising, chiral, and first-order transition lines meet between Z2⊗Z2 and Z3⊗Z3 phases.
main_result: The main result is a detailed many-body phase map showing heterogeneous Rydberg ladders support crossover physics and multicritical behavior absent in single-species arrays.
application_angle: These results define experimentally relevant operating regimes for future dual-species neutral-atom simulators aimed at realizing exotic ordering and critical phenomena.

title: Data-Driven Hamiltonian Reduction for Superconducting Qubits via Meta-Learning
abstract: We introduce HAML (Hamiltonian Adaptation via Meta-Learning), a framework for fast online adaptation of effective Hamiltonian models of superconducting quantum processors. HAML proceeds in two phases. A supervised training phase uses an ensemble of simulated devices to learn an offline map from control inputs and device parameters to effective Hamiltonian coefficients. An online adaptation phase then uses a small number of hardware-accessible measurements to identify the unknown parameters of a new device. By training directly against effective two-qubit coefficients extracted from full multi-mode simulations, HAML implicitly learns the reduction from full multi-mode Hamiltonians to effective qubit descriptions without invoking perturbation theory. We further show that a variance-maximizing greedy selection of measurement configurations boosts online adaptation efficiency. We demonstrate HAML on a transmon-coupler-transmon system, recovering effective two-qubit coefficients across a wide range of operating regimes, including parameter regions where Schrieffer-Wolff perturbation theory (SWPT) breaks down. This establishes a scalable, sample-efficient approach to Hamiltonian reduction and characterization for near-term quantum processors, with direct implications for calibration, control, and error mitigation.
one_liner: A meta-learning framework learns reduced effective Hamiltonians for superconducting qubits and adapts to new devices from few measurements.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical route to faster calibration and model identification in superconducting processors, especially where perturbative reductions fail.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces HAML, which first trains on simulated device ensembles to map controls and device parameters to effective Hamiltonian coefficients, then adapts online to a new device using a small set of hardware-accessible measurements. By training directly against effective two-qubit coefficients from full multimode simulations, the method learns the reduction from complex circuit models to qubit-level descriptions without relying on perturbation theory. Demonstrations on a transmon-coupler-transmon system show accurate recovery of effective couplings even in operating regions where Schrieffer-Wolff perturbation theory breaks down, with further gains from variance-maximizing greedy measurement selection.
main_result: HAML enables sample-efficient identification of effective two-qubit Hamiltonian parameters across broad regimes, including nonperturbative regions inaccessible to standard SWPT reductions.
application_angle: The framework is directly relevant to automated calibration, control optimization, and error-mitigation workflows in near-term superconducting quantum processors.

title: Networked Realization of Quantum LDPC Codes
abstract: Quantum low-density parity-check (QLDPC) codes with good parameters are promising candidates for low-overhead fault-tolerant quantum computing, but their non-local stabilizers require long-range connectivity and frequent qubit movement, introducing practical challenges. Prior work has studied the networked implementation of topological codes, where each node only holds one or a few qubits of the entire code, and demonstrated competitive performance under practical constraints such as the quality of network-provided entanglement. However, since these codes are already geometrically local, such a networked setting might not be essential. In this work, we propose and study the networked implementation of better QLDPC codes, specifically bivariate bicycle codes due to their similarity to surface codes and the controlled amount of long-range connections in their stabilizers. We begin by recreating networked surface codes in Stim, with one code qubit per node, and provide additional insights into their circuit-level noise performance. We then extend this approach to bipartitions of bivariate bicycle codes, using balanced min-cut partitioning on their combined X-Z Tanner graph to identify optimal qubit splits. For stabilizers spanning nodes, we implement teleported CNOTs and vary the Bell pair fidelity enabling these gates. Through circuit-level noise simulations with BP-OSD decoding, we provide the first insights into networked realizations of these codes and compare their performance with monolithic implementations. We conclude by outlining advantages, limitations, and future directions.
one_liner: This work studies how bivariate bicycle quantum LDPC codes can be implemented over entanglement networks using teleported gates and realistic noisy links.
why_it_matters: It tackles a key hardware bottleneck for practical QLDPC deployment by quantifying how nonlocal stabilizers behave in networked architectures.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a networked realization of bivariate bicycle QLDPC codes, motivated by the challenge of implementing their nonlocal stabilizers on monolithic hardware. They first reconstruct networked surface-code baselines in Stim, then partition bivariate bicycle codes via balanced min-cut on the combined X-Z Tanner graph to define cross-node qubit layouts. Inter-node stabilizers are executed with teleported CNOTs under varying Bell-pair fidelity, and circuit-level noise simulations with BP-OSD decoding provide initial performance comparisons against monolithic implementations.
platform_or_system: Networked quantum processor implementing bivariate bicycle LDPC codes
main_result: The paper delivers the first circuit-level study of networked bivariate bicycle code implementations using teleported entangling gates and realistic entanglement-link noise.
application_angle: It informs the co-design of distributed fault-tolerant quantum computers where communication quality and code partitioning jointly determine viable architectures.

title: Bell Test of Photons from Electron-Positron Annihilation via POVM-based Compton Polarimetry
abstract: Quantum entanglement between gamma-ray photons emitted following electron-positron annihilation is expected to be maximal and may be characterized via non-classical polarization correlations. However, this is difficult to verify experimentally because there are no established schemes that approach ideal projective-polarization measurements for high-energy photons. Hence, polarization entanglement between MeV-scale annihilation photons has not yet been conclusively demonstrated. We develop here a framework that models polarization measurements of high-energy photons via Compton polarimetry, employing the formalism of positive operator-valued measures (POVMs). We extend the POVM description to sequences of repeated interactions and show that the measurement converges toward an ideal projective measurement of linear polarization as the number of interactions increases. We demonstrate that this progressive improvement in measurement sharpness can enable the experimental violation of CHSH inequalities.
one_liner: A POVM-based Compton polarimetry framework shows how repeated MeV-photon interactions can enable a Bell test on annihilation gamma pairs.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete measurement route toward conclusively demonstrating high-energy photon polarization entanglement, long blocked by non-ideal polarimetry.
detailed_summary: The paper models polarization measurements of electron-positron annihilation photons using a POVM formalism tailored to Compton scattering, where single interactions are intrinsically non-projective. It extends the analysis to repeated interaction sequences and shows that the effective measurement sharpness approaches ideal linear-polarization projection as interaction count increases. Within this framework, the authors derive conditions under which the nonclassical correlations of MeV-scale photon pairs become strong enough to violate CHSH inequalities experimentally.
main_result: The authors show that repeated-interaction Compton polarimetry can asymptotically realize near-projective polarization measurements sufficient for CHSH violation with annihilation photons.
application_angle: This gives an experimentally actionable path to high-energy quantum photonics tests and could inform gamma-ray quantum measurement platforms.

title: Determination of the Fermi Energy of Diamond using Photoluminescence Spectral Analysis
abstract: Electronic band structures and the Fermi energy provide essential information for understanding the electronic properties of solids. In semiconductors, the Fermi energy level is determined by the donor and acceptor concentrations. For diamond, the relationship between the Fermi energy level and the donor-acceptor concentrations is highly nonlinear; therefore, experimental determination of the Fermi energy level is important. Here, we report a method to determine the Fermi energy of diamond based on photoluminescence (PL) measurement. The density-functional-theory (DFT) study by Deák et al.~\cite{deak2014formation} showed the relationship between the Fermi energy and the formation energies of nitrogen-vacancy centers in the negatively charged (NV-) and neutrally charged (NV0) charge states. In the present method, we measure the relative populations of the NV- and NV0 centers from PL spectral analysis and, using these populations and the DFT result, determine the Fermi energy of the diamond samples. Moreover, we show the application of the method to study the spin coherence and the stability against the charge state conversion of the NV centers on several diamond samples. We also extend the method for the Fermi energy determination using the silicon-vacancy (SiV) center in diamond. The PL-based method is advantageous for determining the Fermi energy with high spatial and fast time resolutions, even in extreme environments, and can be extended to determine various wide band gap semiconductors.
one_liner: Photoluminescence charge-state analysis of NV and SiV centers is used to extract diamond Fermi energy with spatially resolved optical readout.
why_it_matters: A fast, noncontact optical method for Fermi-level mapping in diamond directly supports engineering of defect charge stability and spin coherence for quantum devices.
detailed_summary: The method infers diamond Fermi energy from the relative populations of NV- and NV0 centers measured by photoluminescence spectroscopy, using prior DFT relations between charge-state formation energy and Fermi level. The authors apply the approach across several samples and connect the extracted Fermi energy to NV spin coherence and resistance to charge conversion. They further extend the framework to SiV-based analysis, emphasizing high spatial resolution, rapid temporal response, and operability in extreme environments.
main_result: The work demonstrates an optical spectroscopy method to determine diamond Fermi energy from defect charge-state populations and relates it to qubit-relevant material performance.
application_angle: It can serve as an in situ diagnostics tool for tuning diamond growth, processing, and operating conditions in quantum sensing and spin-photon hardware.

title: USEQIP: Outcomes and experiences from 17 years of undergraduate summer schools in experimental quantum information science
abstract: To grow the quantum information science and technology workforce, opportunities for students to gain experiential learning and build a sense of belonging in the broader community are essential. The Undergraduate School on Experimental Quantum Information Processing (USEQIP) is a two-week summer school for undergraduate students that has been held since 2009 with the goal of introducing undergraduate students from around the world to the tools of quantum information research, paired with a summer internship program. Here we report on the structure, impact, and outlook of the program, including hands-on laboratory activities refined over many iterations of the program. We highlight the career trajectories of program alumni, many of whom have made significant contributions to the quantum field.
one_liner: A 17-year review of USEQIP details a sustained hands-on training pipeline for experimental quantum information science.
why_it_matters: Workforce formation is a practical bottleneck for scaling quantum hardware and photonics, and this study documents a replicable experimental training model.
detailed_summary: The paper reports outcomes from 17 years of the Undergraduate School on Experimental Quantum Information Processing, a two-week program combining lectures, laboratory modules, and research internships. It describes how the curriculum and hands-on experimental activities were iteratively refined to introduce undergraduates to quantum information tools and lab practice. The authors also track alumni trajectories to assess long-term impact on entry into quantum research and industry.
main_result: The study shows that a long-running, lab-centered summer school can measurably contribute to developing the experimental quantum workforce.
application_angle: Its documented structure and outcomes provide a deployable template for institutions building talent pipelines for photonics and quantum hardware labs.

title: Parallel distributed quantum gates for dual-species quantum emitters
abstract: We propose a parallel protocol for implementing distributed nonlocal quantum gates between spatially separated stationary qubits encoded in dual-species quantum emitters (i.e., color-center and superconducting qubits). By utilizing entangled photon pairs with distinct frequencies as a quantum data bus, our approach connects spatially separated devices without requiring quantum frequency conversion or preshared entanglement, while maintaining an always-ready and resource-efficient property for distributed quantum computing and networks. Furthermore, we demonstrate the feasibility of implementing parallel distributed nonlocal quantum gates on multiple pairs of spatially separated qubits using a single high-dimensional entangled photon pair, which directly benefits from the enhanced quantum capacity provided by optical qudit encoding. Our protocol establishes a scalable and practically implementable framework for distributed quantum networks, potentially enabling the development of future large-scale quantum computing architectures.
one_liner: A distributed-gate protocol uses frequency-distinct entangled photons to connect dual-species stationary qubits without frequency conversion.
why_it_matters: It addresses a core interoperability problem in heterogeneous quantum networks by linking unlike emitters with an always-ready photonic bus.
detailed_summary: The authors propose nonlocal gate implementations between spatially separated stationary qubits encoded in heterogeneous emitters such as color centers and superconducting systems. Their scheme uses entangled photon pairs with distinct frequencies as the communication resource, avoiding both quantum frequency conversion and preshared entanglement while retaining an always-ready operating mode. They also generalize the protocol to parallel gates across multiple remote qubit pairs using a single high-dimensional entangled photon pair, leveraging qudit photonic encoding to raise channel capacity.
platform_or_system: Dual-species stationary qubits linked by entangled photons
main_result: The work presents a scalable protocol for parallel distributed quantum gates across heterogeneous qubits using frequency-multiplexed entangled photonic resources.
application_angle: This could reduce integration overhead in modular quantum computing and enable more practical heterogeneous quantum network architectures.

title: Experimental Workflows for Combinatorial Optimization: Towards Quantum Advantage
abstract: Demonstrating quantum advantage for combinatorial optimization requires more than standalone algorithmic results; it calls for end-to-end case studies that integrate problem modelling, quantum execution, and classical refinement into practical workflows. This paper presents a sandbox platform for experimenting with hybrid quantum-classical workflows in graph optimization, enabling the systematic study of end-to-end optimization pipelines. Using our platform, we investigate three classically intractable and mutually reducible graph problems -- Minimum Vertex Cover, Maximum Independent Set, and Maximum Clique -- by transforming them into an unconstrained problem and solving the resulting instances with QAOA on IBM platforms. Our workflow combines classical pre-processing to reduce instance size, quantum optimization on the reduced problem, and classical postprocessing to map quantum outputs to high-quality feasible solutions, thereby avoiding direct constraint encoding in the quantum circuit. We evaluate the approach on synthetic graphs, benchmark instances, and real-world networks, and report hardware experiments on IBM Quantum System One at PINQ2 in Bromont, Quebec, powered by IBM's 156-qubit Heron r2 processor on graphs up to 128 vertices, with circuits involving up to 128 qubits and 13,555 two-qubit gates. The results illustrate how sandbox-style end-to-end experimentation can expose bottlenecks, clarify the role of classical-quantum workload partitioning, and provide domain experts and practitioners with a practical guide for interpreting quantum optimization outputs and assessing quantum utility on the road to quantum advantage in combinatorial optimization.
one_liner: An end-to-end hybrid workflow runs graph optimization with QAOA on IBM Heron hardware for problem sizes up to 128 vertices.
why_it_matters: It moves beyond toy algorithm benchmarks by exposing the real workflow bottlenecks that matter for practical quantum optimization claims.
detailed_summary: The paper builds a sandbox platform for full-stack hybrid optimization workflows covering classical preprocessing, quantum execution, and classical postprocessing. Minimum Vertex Cover, Maximum Independent Set, and Maximum Clique instances are reduced to an unconstrained form and solved with QAOA on IBM systems, including experiments on the 156-qubit Heron r2 processor at PINQ2. Reported demonstrations reach graphs up to 128 vertices with circuits using up to 128 qubits and 13,555 two-qubit gates, allowing the authors to assess where classical reduction and quantum subroutines meaningfully contribute.
platform_or_system: IBM Quantum Heron r2 superconducting processor
