main_result: The authors demonstrate large-scale hybrid QAOA workflows on superconducting hardware for graph optimization instances up to 128 vertices.
application_angle: The workflow offers a practical template for evaluating quantum optimization utility in industrial-style pipelines rather than isolated algorithm tests.

title: Spectral tuning of single T centres by the Stark effect
abstract: Among the many solid-state emitters being explored for scalable quantum technologies, the silicon T centre is a leading candidate offering long-lived spin qubits, a telecommunications-band spin-photon interface, and integration with on-chip photonic circuits. However, nanophotonic integration broadens both the inhomogeneous spectral distribution and individual emitter linewidths. Here, we integrate single T centres into silicon nanophotonic cavities with p-i-n diodes for local electronic control. These devices enable Stark tuning up to 30 GHz, sufficient to bring 55(2)% of on-chip T centres into mutual resonance, and demonstrate tunable lifetime reduction across the cavity resonance. A model of the joint excitation probability shows an orders-of-magnitude increase in entanglement rate by tuning distinct emitters into mutual resonance. Luminescence modulation at high reverse biases reveals a transition to a dark charge state. Finally, bias-induced modulation of the optical transition splitting uncovers a potential mechanism for electrically driven excited-state spin mixing via spin-orbit coupling. Localized and individual spectral tuning increases the yield of performant silicon spin-photon interfaces and the number of devices per chip available for large-scale entanglement and quantum information technologies.
one_liner: Single silicon T centers in nanophotonic cavities are Stark tuned by up to 30 GHz, bringing many emitters into mutual resonance.
why_it_matters: Spectral alignment is a key yield limiter for spin-photon hardware, and this shows local electrical tuning on a telecom-band silicon emitter platform.
detailed_summary: The work integrates single T centers in silicon nanophotonic cavities with p-i-n diode control for local Stark tuning. The devices achieve up to 30 GHz tuning, enough to bring 55(2)% of on-chip emitters into mutual resonance, and also show tunable lifetime reduction across the cavity mode. Additional measurements reveal bias-dependent luminescence quenching into a dark charge state and modulation of optical splitting consistent with a possible electrically driven excited-state spin-mixing mechanism.
platform_or_system: Silicon T centers in nanophotonic cavity p-i-n devices
main_result: The authors demonstrate electrically controlled spectral tuning of single T centers sufficient to substantially improve resonance matching and expected remote-entanglement rates.
application_angle: This improves usable emitter yield for scalable silicon spin-photon networks and telecom-compatible quantum interconnect hardware.

title: Quantum-enhanced Network Tomography
abstract: Network tomography refers to the use of inference techniques for inferring internal network states from end-to-end probes. Quantum probes, implemented by sending blocks of $n$ coherent-state pulses augmented with continuous-variable (CV) squeezing ($n=1$) or weak temporal-mode entanglement ($n>1$) over a lossy channel to a receiver with homodyne detection capabilities, are known to carry information about the channel transmissivity. Assuming a subset of nodes in an optical network is capable of sending and receiving such probes through intermediate nodes with all-optical switching capabilities, we leverage these quantum probes to estimate link transmissivities. To determine how to route the probes in a network, we propose a probe construction algorithm that guarantees link identifiability, while maximizing the number of information orthogonal sets of transmissivities. A set of probes induces a Fisher information matrix (FIM). We then derive two metrics, the determinant of the FIM and the trace of its inverse, to evaluate the performance of the probes. In particular, our results can be used to characterize the quantum improvement in estimating link transmissivities in a general optical network.
one_liner: Uses CV-squeezed and temporally entangled coherent probes to improve optical network link-transmissivity tomography.
why_it_matters: It frames quantum probes as a practical way to diagnose internal optical-network losses with better estimation efficiency than classical probing.
detailed_summary: The paper studies network tomography in optical networks where selected nodes send coherent-state pulse blocks enhanced by CV squeezing or weak temporal-mode entanglement through switched lossy paths. It proposes a probe-routing and construction algorithm that guarantees identifiability of individual link transmissivities while maximizing orthogonal information content across probes. Performance is quantified through the Fisher information matrix, using both its determinant and the trace of its inverse to compare probe sets. The framework generalizes quantum-enhanced channel estimation from single links to complex optical network topologies.
platform_or_system: optical network with CV quantum probes
main_result: The authors derive an identifiable probe-design framework and Fisher-information metrics showing how quantum probes can improve estimation of link transmissivities in general optical networks.
application_angle: This could enable more accurate loss mapping, fault localization, and performance monitoring in reconfigurable photonic networks and quantum communication infrastructure.

title: Semi-transmitter-device-independent quantum key distribution
abstract: Transmitter-device-dependence is a longstanding but often implicit problem in quantum key distribution (QKD), as compared to measurement-device-dependence. One-sided device-independent (1sDI) scenario relaxes the security conditions of DI framework and offers an intuitive solution to transmitter-sided dependence. Here we show that, by constructing a composable transmitter in a 1sDI configuration, the transmitter-device-dependence can be largely eliminated. Our scheme integrates the entanglement source into the transmitter as an individual part, and the detection module is another individual part treated as black box. In a proof-of-principle experiment we obtain a secure key-rate of 1~kbps at an equivalent transmission fiber distance of 20~km, demonstrating the first discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD. By implementing semi-transmitter-device-independent security while maintaining strong loss tolerance, our approach bridges security and practicality for real-world 1sDI-QKD deployments.
one_liner: Demonstrates semi-transmitter-device-independent QKD with composable security and 1 kbps secure key at 20 km equivalent fiber.
why_it_matters: It addresses transmitter-side trust assumptions in QKD without sacrificing practical loss tolerance, narrowing the gap between DI security and deployable systems.
detailed_summary: This work introduces a semi-transmitter-device-independent QKD protocol based on a one-sided device-independent configuration, targeting the usually overlooked problem of transmitter-device dependence. The entanglement source is incorporated into the transmitter as a trusted composable component, while the detection module is treated as a black box. In a proof-of-principle discrete-variable experiment, the system achieves a secure key rate of about 1 kbps over an equivalent 20 km fiber distance. The protocol is positioned as the first experimental discrete-variable 1sDI-QKD demonstration with practical loss tolerance.
main_result: The experiment realizes semi-transmitter-device-independent QKD and produces a composably secure 1 kbps key over an equivalent 20 km fiber channel.
application_angle: The scheme offers a more realistic security model for deployed QKD by reducing transmitter trust requirements while retaining experimentally practical performance.

title: Nonlinearity-enhanced Quantum Sensing in Discrete Time Crystal Probes
abstract: Discrete time crystals are non-equilibrium phases of matter in periodically driven systems, characterized by robust subharmonic oscillations and broken discrete time-translation symmetry. Their long-lived coherent dynamics and resilience to imperfections make them promising resources for quantum sensing. A disorder-free discrete-time crystal probe can provide the quantum-enhanced estimation of the coupling parameter. Here, we extend this sensing mechanism to nonlinear interactions and show that this nonlinear profile strongly enhances the sensing precision by increasing the system-size scaling exponent of the quantum Fisher information. Our analytical discussion separates a rigorous seminorm upper bound from the physically relevant scaling realized by product-state probes in the time crystal regime. Numerically, we find that the quantum Fisher information retains its quadratic long-time growth with the number of Floquet cycles, while its system-size exponent increases approximately linearly with the nonlinearity exponent, identifying nonlinearity as a resource for quantum-enhanced sensitivity. We further show that stronger nonlinearities shrink the time crystal stability window, making the probe more sensitive to small deviations from the resonant condition. We also analyze the effect of imperfect pulses and show that such imperfections can enhance, rather than suppress, the information encoded in the evolved state. Finally, we discuss a digital implementation of the nonlinear DTC sensing protocol using superconducting qubits.
one_liner: Shows that nonlinear interactions can boost time-crystal quantum sensing by increasing the system-size scaling of quantum Fisher information.
why_it_matters: It identifies nonlinearity as a sensing resource and links an exotic many-body phase to implementable superconducting-qubit metrology.
detailed_summary: The paper extends discrete-time-crystal sensing protocols to nonlinear interactions and analyzes how the nonlinearity exponent modifies metrological scaling. Analytically and numerically, it finds that the quantum Fisher information keeps its quadratic growth with Floquet cycle number while the system-size scaling exponent rises approximately linearly with nonlinearity. The study also maps tradeoffs: stronger nonlinearities narrow the time-crystal stability window and heighten sensitivity to detuning, while pulse imperfections can unexpectedly enhance encoded information. A digital realization using superconducting qubits is discussed as a concrete hardware pathway.
main_result: Nonlinear DTC probes retain long-time quadratic QFI growth and achieve stronger system-size scaling, yielding enhanced parameter-estimation sensitivity.
application_angle: The results suggest a route to higher-sensitivity quantum sensors implemented on programmable superconducting-qubit platforms using driven many-body dynamics.

title: Millikelvin digital-to-analog converter for superconducting quantum processors
abstract: Scaling superconducting quantum processors is increasingly constrained by the wiring, heat load, and calibration overhead associated with delivering high-resolution analog signals from room temperature to qubits at millikelvin temperature. Here we demonstrate a superconducting digital-to-analog converter (DAC) integrated with high-coherence fluxonium qubits in a multi-chip module architecture. The DACs generate persistent analog flux signals for tuning qubit parameters and are programmed deterministically using single-flux-quantum (SFQ) pulses, providing a digital interface compatible with established SFQ routing and demultiplexing technologies. Operating at millikelvin temperature, the DACs enable in-situ tuning of fluxonium qubits without measurable degradation of qubit coherence. The presented device provides a static control primitive for flux-tunable qubits, enabling parameter homogenization and eliminating the need for individual room-temperature DC bias lines. These results establish SFQ-programmable millikelvin DACs as a building block for digitally controlled superconducting quantum processors.
one_liner: Implements an SFQ-programmable millikelvin DAC that statically tunes fluxonium qubits without measurable coherence loss.
why_it_matters: It removes a major scaling bottleneck in superconducting processors by replacing many room-temperature bias lines with integrated cryogenic digital control.
detailed_summary: This work demonstrates a superconducting digital-to-analog converter co-integrated with high-coherence fluxonium qubits in a multi-chip module. The DAC generates persistent analog flux biases and is deterministically programmed using single-flux-quantum pulses, making it compatible with scalable SFQ routing and demultiplexing. Operating at millikelvin temperatures, it enables in-situ qubit parameter tuning and homogenization without measurable qubit-coherence degradation. The device is presented as a static control primitive for flux-tunable superconducting processors.
main_result: The authors demonstrate cryogenic DAC-based persistent flux control of fluxonium qubits with no measurable loss of coherence.
application_angle: Integrated millikelvin DACs can cut wiring, heat load, and calibration overhead in large superconducting quantum processors, improving scalability of control hardware.

title: Entanglement Dynamics in a Two Transmon Qubit System under Continuous Measurement and Postselection
abstract: We investigate the role of continuous measurement and postselection in the dynamics and entanglement of a transmon-cavity-transmon coupled system. In the dispersive regime, characterized by a large detuning between the transmons and the cavity, the two transmons interact via virtual excitation of the cavity, giving rise to an effective transmon-transmon coupling. In addition to this coherent interaction, each transmon undergoes spontaneous emission, which is continuously monitored through independent detection channels. By incorporating realistic detector inefficiencies, we analyze both efficient and imperfect monitoring scenarios and demonstrate that postselection significantly slows down the decay of entanglement compared to the unmonitored case. We formulate the stochastic master equation for the coupled system, derive the corresponding postselected master equation, and investigate the dynamics through the Liouvillian superoperator spectrum. In the interaction frame, we identify the emergence of an exceptional point and characterize the associated broken and unbroken PT-symmetric phases. We show how these phases influence the system dynamics and the corresponding entanglement behavior. Our results provide insight into how continuous measurement and postselection affect entanglement in dissipative quantum systems, with potential applications in quantum information processing.
one_liner: Analyzes how continuous monitoring and postselection preserve entanglement in a dissipative two-transmon system and reveal PT-symmetric dynamics.
why_it_matters: It connects realistic measurement backaction to entanglement stabilization mechanisms relevant for monitored superconducting quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two transmons coupled through a cavity in the dispersive regime, where virtual cavity excitations generate effective qubit-qubit interactions while each qubit undergoes monitored spontaneous emission. Using stochastic and postselected master-equation treatments with finite detector efficiency, it shows that postselection substantially slows entanglement decay relative to unmonitored evolution. Spectral analysis of the Liouvillian identifies an exceptional point and broken/unbroken PT-symmetric phases in the interaction frame. These phases are linked to distinct dynamical and entanglement behaviors under continuous measurement.
main_result: Postselection under continuous monitoring significantly prolongs entanglement in a dissipative two-transmon platform and exposes exceptional-point-driven dynamical regimes.
application_angle: The results inform measurement-based protocols for entanglement preservation and state conditioning in superconducting quantum information hardware.

title: Second Harmonic Generation Through Backward Raman Scattering in Magnetized Plasmas Driven by Circularly Polarized Intense Lasers
abstract: A fluid-based theoretical framework is developed to describe the nonlinear cascade linking primary BRS-driven plasma wave amplification, oscillating two-stream instability (OTSI), nonlinear current generation within a self-formed ponderomotive channel, and radiation of the secondary electromagnetic mode. Systematic parameter studies reveal a strong sensitivity of the entire cascade to the relative handedness of laser polarization and axial magnetic field direction, as well as to cyclotron resonance strength. Resonant right-handed circular polarization significantly enhances ponderomotive expulsion, channel depth, BRS and OTSI growth rates, nonlinear current density, and the amplitude of the secondary harmonic, whereas non-resonant left-handed polarization effectively suppresses these processes. Fully kinetic particle in cell simulations using EPOCH, together with macroscopic finite-element modelling in COMSOL Multiphysics, corroborate the polarization- and magnetization dependent wake modulation and channelling efficiency across a wide range of laser wavelengths, pulse durations, and plasma densities. The temporal evolution and saturation of OTSI, resilience to density inhomogeneities, and wavenumber-resolved resonance tuning illustrate axial magnetization as a flexible control mechanism for adjusting the intensity, spectral position, bandwidth, and stability of multi peak Raman spectra. These findings demonstrate that cyclotron resonance and polarization control are effective methods for manipulating nonlinear Raman emission in high-intensity laser magnetized plasma interactions, offering predictive insights for forthcoming kinetic simulations and experimental implementations.
one_liner: Shows polarization- and magnetization-controlled second-harmonic generation via backward Raman scattering cascades in magnetized plasma.
why_it_matters: It provides a tunable mechanism for nonlinear frequency conversion and Raman spectral shaping in intense laser-plasma photonic systems.
detailed_summary: A fluid-theory model is developed for a nonlinear cascade in magnetized plasma linking backward Raman scattering, oscillating two-stream instability, ponderomotive channel formation, nonlinear current generation, and secondary electromagnetic emission. The analysis predicts strong dependence on the handedness of circular laser polarization relative to the axial magnetic field, with resonant right-handed polarization strongly enhancing channeling, instability growth, nonlinear current, and second-harmonic output. Particle-in-cell simulations with EPOCH and finite-element modeling in COMSOL support the predicted polarization- and magnetization-dependent behavior across laser and plasma parameters. The work also examines saturation dynamics, robustness to density inhomogeneity, and tunability of Raman spectra via axial magnetization.
platform_or_system: intense circularly polarized lasers in magnetized plasma
main_result: The study shows that cyclotron resonance and polarization handedness can strongly control and enhance Raman-driven second-harmonic emission in magnetized plasma.
application_angle: This offers a controllable route for high-intensity nonlinear light generation and spectral engineering in plasma-based photonic and laser-conversion setups.

title: A graph-aware bounded distance decoder for all stabilizer codes
abstract: We formulate a bounded distance decoding strategy applicable to all stabilizer codes including both CSS and non-CSS code-families. The framework emerges out of the local Clifford equivalence between arbitrary stabilizer states and graph states. Using the graphical representation of the stabilizers and the syndromes, we constitute the bounded distance decoding as an adaptable generalization of maximum likelihood decoding, ensuring correction of all errors with weights upper bounded by a target weight. We show that strategic pruning associated with a feed-forward network structure of the graph can reduce the search space and subsequently the runtime of the designed decoder. We demonstrate satisfactory performance of the bounded distance decoder in the case of the optimal non-CSS codes up to distance $d=11$ subjected to the depolarizing error on all qubits, and near-optimal decoding for the color and the surface codes, both belonging to the CSS family, under the bit-flip errors on the qubits. We also develop an open-source library, QGDecoder, enabling the graph-aware bounded distance decoding of arbitrary stabilizer codes.
one_liner: A graph-state-based bounded-distance decoder extends efficient stabilizer decoding beyond CSS codes to arbitrary stabilizer families.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical decoding route for non-CSS and CSS codes alike, with pruning strategies and open-source tooling relevant to real fault-tolerant architectures.
detailed_summary: The work maps arbitrary stabilizer codes to locally Clifford-equivalent graph states and formulates decoding directly in terms of graphical stabilizers and syndromes. This yields a bounded-distance decoder that guarantees correction of all errors up to a chosen target weight, effectively generalizing maximum-likelihood style reasoning to a broader code class. The authors introduce graph-structured pruning using a feed-forward network view to reduce the search space and runtime. They report strong performance on optimal non-CSS codes up to distance 11 under depolarizing noise and near-optimal results for surface and color codes under bit-flip noise.
main_result: The decoder successfully handles arbitrary stabilizer codes, with demonstrated correction performance on non-CSS codes up to distance 11 and competitive results on surface and color codes.
application_angle: A unified decoder for diverse code families can simplify software stacks for fault-tolerant quantum processors and accelerate exploration of hardware-efficient non-CSS codes.

title: Elucidating mechanism of optical cavities in superconducting strip single photon detectors using transmission line and impedance models
abstract: We clarified the physical mechanism of superconducting strip single photon detectors (SSPDs) with optical cavities by using transmission line and impedance models. By introducing the transmission line model, we derived the analytical formulae for the absorptance of SSPDs with optical cavities. We compared the absorptance obtained from the analytical formulae for SSPDs with single-side, double-side, and dielectric multi-layer optical cavities against the results of numerical simulations. The comparison showed that the results were nearly identical. By introducing the impedance model, it was clearly shown that the SSPDs with optical cavities achieved the maximum absorptance when their input impedance of the SSPDs with optical cavities matched the impedance of the input medium. The design concepts proposed in this study are applicable to other superconducting detectors, such as microwave kinetic inductance detectors and transition-edge sensors.
one_liner: Transmission-line and impedance models explain and predict cavity-enhanced absorption in superconducting strip single-photon detectors.
why_it_matters: The paper turns cavity design in superconducting photon detectors into an impedance-matching problem, enabling faster and more systematic optimization across detector platforms.
detailed_summary: The authors derive analytical absorptance formulas for superconducting strip single-photon detectors with single-side, double-side, and dielectric multilayer optical cavities using a transmission-line model. They show close agreement between these formulas and numerical simulations, validating the simplified physical description. An impedance model then makes the operating principle explicit: absorptance is maximized when the detector-cavity input impedance matches that of the incident medium. The framework is positioned as generalizable to MKIDs and TES devices as well.
platform_or_system: superconducting strip single-photon detectors with optical cavities
main_result: They show that cavity-enhanced SSPD absorption can be predicted analytically and optimized by input-impedance matching, with results matching numerical simulations across cavity types.
application_angle: This offers a compact design rule for building higher-efficiency superconducting photodetectors used in quantum optics, imaging, and cryogenic sensing systems.

title: Quantum Compressed Sensing Enables Image Classification with a Single Photon
abstract: Image classification is a core task of intelligent sensing, conventionally follows a sequential imaging then processing pipeline. However, redundant high-dimensional image reconstruction is inherently inefficient, especially in photon limited scenarios. Here we report a photon level image classification method using quantum compressed sensing, which reformulates the classification task as a sparse signal measurement problem directly oriented toward class labels. By exploiting the parallelism of photonic quantum superposition states, a single photon can be encoded the complete spatial information of a high-dimensional image. Through a diffractive deep neural network, we physically construct a dedicated measurement basis aligned with the class space, enabling signal-dependent adaptive compressive measurement. Ideally, our method can extract class information via a single quantum projective measurement, reducing the required number of measurements from the logarithmic scaling O(Klog(N/K)) of classical compressed sensing to the constant-order information-theoretic limit M = K = 1. Experimental results show that a classification accuracy of 69.0% can be achieved by using a single-photon detection event as the decision criterion, while it increases to 95.0% with four-photon detection events. This work demonstrates image classification at the energy efficiency limit and introduces a measurement as decision framework. It provides a foundation for intelligent sensing systems that operate under extreme photon budgets and harsh environments.
one_liner: Single-photon quantum compressed sensing is used to classify images directly, bypassing full image reconstruction.
why_it_matters: It reframes machine vision as a measurement problem and pushes classification toward the extreme photon-efficiency limit needed in low-light and hostile environments.
detailed_summary: The method encodes the full spatial content of a high-dimensional image into a photonic quantum superposition and uses a diffractive deep neural network to realize a class-aligned measurement basis. Rather than reconstructing the image, the system performs task-specific projective measurements aimed directly at label extraction. The authors argue this can reduce measurement requirements from classical compressed-sensing scaling to an information-theoretic constant-order limit for the target task. Experimentally, they report 69.0% classification accuracy from a single detection event and 95.0% with four detected photons.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates direct image classification with 69.0% accuracy from one photon event and 95.0% accuracy using four photon detections.
application_angle: The approach is relevant for ultra-low-power machine vision in remote sensing, covert imaging, and photon-starved autonomous sensing platforms.

title: Quantum-Accelerated Gowers $U_2$ Norm for Bent Boolean Functions
abstract: Bent Boolean functions extremal objects that maximally resist affine approximation are notoriously hard to construct for large numbers of variables. We propose a hybrid quantum-classical genetic algorithm (GA) that uses a \emph{quantum circuit} to evaluate the Gowers $U_2$ norm as the evolutionary fitness function. Our central contribution is a complexity-theoretic separation: the quantum evaluation circuit requires only $3n$ qubits and $\bigO(n^2)$ two-qubit gates per function query, whereas the classical computation of the exact Gowers $U_2$ norm demands $\bigO(2^{2n})$ arithmetic operations an exponential overhead that renders it infeasible for $n \gtrsim 25$. We validate the framework on $n=6$ and $n=8$ variable systems. For $n=8$, our classical GA run extended to 1000 generations achieves best fitness $\Utwof = 0.250000$ \emph{exactly} the theoretical bent threshold $2^{-n/4}$ with average fitness $0.257267$, confirming that the Gowers $U_2$ norm is a superior fitness criterion over Walsh-Hadamard spectral flatness. Quantum-assisted evaluation faithfully reproduces the classical trajectory up to finite-sampling noise, and our complexity analysis demonstrates that for $n > 25$ the quantum evaluator provides a decisive computational advantage on fault-tolerant hardware.
one_liner: A hybrid quantum-classical search uses quantum evaluation of the Gowers U2 norm to find bent Boolean functions more efficiently.
why_it_matters: It identifies a concrete quantum subroutine with exponential classical cost avoidance in a hardware-relevant hybrid optimization workflow.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes using a quantum circuit to evaluate the Gowers U2 norm as the fitness function inside a genetic algorithm searching for bent Boolean functions. The key resource claim is that the quantum evaluator uses 3n qubits and O(n^2) two-qubit gates per function query, while exact classical evaluation scales as O(2^(2n)). Validation on 6- and 8-variable instances shows that the quantum-assisted fitness reproduces classical optimization trajectories up to sampling noise. For n=8, the classical GA reaches the theoretical bent threshold exactly, supporting the choice of U2 as a strong fitness metric.
platform_or_system: fault-tolerant quantum circuit for hybrid optimization
main_result: They show a quantum U2-norm evaluator with polynomial circuit resources that can replace an exponentially costly classical fitness computation in bent-function search.
application_angle: This is a plausible near-term blueprint for embedding quantum accelerators into specialized combinatorial search and cryptographic function design pipelines.

title: Ultrafast electron vortex produced by a grating made of light
abstract: The generation of vortex matter waves carrying quantized orbital angular momentum is challenging and relies heavily on the material nanofabrication methods due to their extremely small de-Broglie wavelengths. Here, we introduce an all-optical method for generating an electron vortex by diffraction through a grating made of light. We realize the orbital angular momentum transfer between free electrons and photons by stimulated Compton scattering. The transferred angular momentum quantum number can be freely tuned. The method can be generalized to a broad range of charged particles, neutral atoms, and molecules of diverse masses. Our results open up novel opportunities for applications in free electron lasers and ultrafast electron microscopy by utilizing the orbital angular momentum degree of freedom of free electrons.
one_liner: An all-optical light grating transfers tunable orbital angular momentum to free electrons to create ultrafast electron vortices.
why_it_matters: It removes the need for nanofabricated material gratings and opens a flexible route to vortex matter waves for ultrafast probes and beam engineering.
detailed_summary: The authors propose generating vortex matter waves by diffracting electrons from a grating made purely of light rather than a fabricated material structure. Orbital angular momentum is transferred through stimulated Compton scattering, and the OAM quantum number is described as freely tunable. Because the mechanism is optical, it avoids de Broglie wavelength fabrication constraints that complicate conventional electron-vortex generation. The concept is also presented as extendable to other charged particles, neutral atoms, and molecules.
platform_or_system: free electrons interacting with an optical light grating
main_result: They introduce an all-optical scheme for producing tunable ultrafast electron vortices via stimulated Compton scattering from a light grating.
application_angle: The method could enable dynamically programmable vortex beams for free-electron lasers, ultrafast electron microscopy, and structured-particle-beam instrumentation.

title: Near-identical photons from distant quantum dot-cavity devices
abstract: Scalable optical quantum technologies require interference between large numbers of indistinguishable single-photons emitted by independent sources. Semiconductor quantum dots are known to be excellent on-demand sources of single-photons. They show record efficiency when inserted into optical cavities to control their spontaneous emission and generate trains of near identical photons over microsecond timescales. However, generating perfectly identical photons from distant cavity-based sources has remained a long-standing challenge. It requires precise matching of the emission wavelengths and emission dynamics, while simultaneously minimizing spectral noise across all time scales for distant emitters in uncorrelated environments. Here, we report on the nanofabrication of a large number of quantum dot-cavity sources with ultra-low spectral noise and wavelength dispersion. The high source efficiency and the use of two tuning mechanisms enable precise optimization of the spectral overlap between distant sources. With this approach, we demonstrate a two-photon indistinguishability of $88\pm1$ % between photons emitted from two distant sources. Remarkably, this value reaches the upper bound set by the intrinsic indistinguishability of photons emitted successively by each source. These results represent a key milestone for scaling photon-based quantum technologies.
one_liner: Distant quantum dot-cavity devices emit near-identical photons with 88% two-source indistinguishability.
why_it_matters: High-visibility interference from independent solid-state emitters is a central scaling requirement for photonic quantum computing and networking.
detailed_summary: The work addresses the long-standing challenge of making photons from remote cavity-coupled quantum dots identical despite wavelength spread, emission-dynamics mismatch, and uncorrelated spectral noise. The authors combine nanofabrication yielding low spectral noise and low wavelength dispersion with two independent tuning mechanisms to optimize spectral overlap between sources. They measure 88±1% two-photon indistinguishability between photons from distant devices. That value reaches the apparent ceiling set by each source's intrinsic successive-photon indistinguishability, indicating the remote-source matching is no longer the dominant limitation.
main_result: They demonstrate 88±1% two-photon indistinguishability between photons emitted by two distant quantum dot-cavity devices, near the intrinsic source limit.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to scaling multiplexed photonic quantum processors and distributed quantum networks that require interference between independent on-demand emitters.

title: No Tile Left Behind: Multiprogramming for Surface-Code Architectures
abstract: Fault-tolerant quantum computing (FTQC) is emerging as the architectural regime in which practical large-scale quantum workloads will execute. In this setting, however, multiprogramming is no longer a matter of partitioning a flat pool of qubits. Quantum error correction exposes a structured floorplan of data tiles, ancilla tiles, and magic-state service resources, so concurrent execution must account for compact placement, connectivity, routing headroom, and shared support infrastructure. This makes FTQC multiprogramming fundamentally harder than its NISQ counterpart: admission decisions can fragment the remaining floorplan, conservative reservations can waste ancilla, and dynamic contention across data, ancilla, and magic-state resources can degrade both throughput and quality of service. In this work, we develop a formal framework for FTQC multiprogramming that captures these structural constraints and their runtime implications. We formulate the baseline static allocation problem, extend it to limited-resource and online settings through hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and further generalize it to cultivation-enabled architectures with dynamic magic-state generation. Through simulation on synthetic Clifford+T workloads, the proposed scheduler achieves a normalized system speedup of 3.1x, improving over prior FTQC multiprogramming baselines by ~29% while maintaining low mean slowdown.
one_liner: A surface-code multiprogramming framework boosts concurrent FTQC throughput by coordinating data, ancilla, routing, and magic-state resources.
why_it_matters: It tackles a core systems bottleneck for useful fault-tolerant quantum computers, showing sizable throughput gains without large slowdown penalties.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates multiprogramming for fault-tolerant surface-code machines as a structured allocation problem over data tiles, ancilla tiles, routing space, and shared magic-state services. It extends static placement to limited-resource and online settings with hierarchy-aware scheduling policies, and further includes cultivation-enabled architectures with dynamic magic-state generation. In simulation on synthetic Clifford+T workloads, the scheduler reaches 3.1x normalized system speedup and improves on prior FTQC multiprogramming baselines by about 29% while keeping mean slowdown low.
main_result: A hierarchy-aware scheduler for surface-code architectures achieves 3.1x normalized system speedup on synthetic Clifford+T workloads.
application_angle: The work is directly relevant to quantum data-center OS/runtime design, where packing and scheduling multiple fault-tolerant jobs will determine practical hardware utilization.

title: Robustness of fiber-optic attenuators to 1061-nm sub-nanosecond pulsed laser radiation in quantum key distribution systems
abstract: The security of quantum key distribution (QKD) systems relies on the physical integrity of their components. While laser-damage attacks (LDAs) using high-power continuous-wave (cw) lasers have been well studied, the threat posed by pulsed lasers at alternative wavelengths remains underestimated. Here, we experimentally investigated the stability of four types of fiber-optic attenuators under exposure to sub-picosecond pulses at 1061 nm with average power reaching 1 W. Mechanical variable attenuators with blocking elements and fixed air-gap attenuators show resistance to this attack. MEMS-based variable attenuators exhibit increased attenuation or irreversible damage that causes a permanent reduction in attenuation of approximately 3.8 dB. For fixed attenuators with an absorption element, we demonstrate that initial pulsed irradiation significantly lowers the optical damage threshold of the components compared to direct cw attacks. The attenuation reduction achieved is up to 7 dB at a 1 W cw laser at 1550 nm. These results highlight the possibility of establishing a hidden side-channel for eavesdropping attacks and underscore the insufficiency of existing countermeasures against sophisticated LDA scenarios.
one_liner: Sub-nanosecond 1061 nm laser tests reveal that some fiber attenuators in QKD can be permanently altered, opening hidden side channels.
why_it_matters: It identifies a realistic hardware attack path in deployed QKD components and shows current laser-damage countermeasures are incomplete.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally expose four classes of fiber-optic attenuators to sub-picosecond 1061 nm pulses with average power up to 1 W. Mechanical variable attenuators with blocking elements and fixed air-gap attenuators remain resistant, while MEMS-based variable attenuators show either increased attenuation or irreversible damage that lowers attenuation by about 3.8 dB. Fixed attenuators with absorption elements become more vulnerable after initial pulsed irradiation, enabling up to 7 dB attenuation reduction under a 1 W continuous-wave 1550 nm follow-up attack.
main_result: The study shows that 1061 nm pulsed irradiation can permanently modify certain QKD attenuators and create exploitable attenuation changes.
application_angle: These results matter for QKD certification and hardening because component-level optical robustness directly affects resistance to eavesdropping attacks.

title: Quantum memory and scrambling from the perspective of a classical neural network
abstract: Entropic uncertainty relations are universal quantifiers of fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements and are widely discussed in the quantum metrology literature. Quantum memory is a phenomenon related to the specific type of quantum correlations that allows for reducing fundamental uncertainties of quantum measurements. In the present work, the modified concept of quantum memory for time-dependent problems is proposed. We compare the time-dependent formulation of quantum memory with the out-of-time-ordered correlator (OTOC). Quantum memory is a rigorous mathematical concept that requires demanding calculations. Thus, until now, quantum memory has been discussed mainly for simple model systems and stationary problems. In the present work, we demonstrate that quantum memory can also be studied for realistic and physically relevant systems, e.g., the atomic helical spin chain, as well as the emergence and propagation of quantum correlations in time. We found that quantum memory manifests faster oscillations in time than OTOC and does not equilibrate. Furthermore, an artificial neural network is trained and asked to predict results for OTOC and quantum memory. These results show that quantum memory is more sensitive than OTOC in terms of broken inversion symmetry and the nonreciprocal effect.
one_liner: A neural-network-assisted study finds time-dependent quantum memory is a more sensitive probe of scrambling asymmetry than OTOCs in helical spin chains.
why_it_matters: It points to a potentially sharper diagnostic for correlation flow and nonreciprocity in realistic quantum many-body systems.
detailed_summary: The work reformulates quantum memory for time-dependent dynamics and compares it directly with out-of-time-ordered correlators in realistic systems such as an atomic helical spin chain. The authors find that quantum memory exhibits faster temporal oscillations than OTOCs and does not equilibrate, while also being more sensitive to broken inversion symmetry and nonreciprocal effects. A classical artificial neural network is trained to predict both OTOC and quantum-memory behavior, enabling analysis of correlation propagation in settings where exact calculations are demanding.
main_result: Time-dependent quantum memory outperforms OTOCs as a sensitive indicator of inversion-symmetry breaking and nonreciprocal dynamics.
application_angle: The approach could help characterize correlation transport and directional effects in quantum simulators and spin-based devices used for metrology or information transfer.

title: Intensity-guided pose-free multiview fusion for single photon sensing
abstract: Single-photon light detection and ranging (LiDAR) extends active three-dimensional sensing at the fundamental level and has found applications in extreme environments involving long-range operation, low-reflectance targets, and adverse visibility. However, the acquired measurements often give rise to single-photon point clouds that are sparse, spatially non-uniform, and corrupted by outliers and depth distortions, making multi-view registration challenging especially when sensor poses are not accurately known. In this work, we present a geometry-intensity coupled registration framework (GIC-Reg) of pose-free multi-view fusion for single-photon sensing. It is established by combining physical-aware preprocessing, joint geometry-intensity grid feature aggregation, global matching, and local ambiguity disambiguation to estimate inter-view rigid transformations and hence to construct a globally consistent reconstruction. On the synthetic benchmark, it admits the lowest relative rotation error (RRE), relative translation error, and root mean square error across all background-noise and dropout rates, in comparison to baselines. Notably, under the most degraded dropout, it reduces the RRE from $13.167^\circ$ to $8.459^\circ$ compared with the learning-based baseline. Furthermore, experimental results on real multi-view data acquired at about 80~m show that it achieves more reliable global orientation and local alignment. Our results show that photon intensity provides an effective physical cue for stabilizing multiview registration in single-photon point cloud, and thus our work aids significant progress in exploring practical utility of single-photon sensing.
one_liner: A pose-free multiview fusion method uses photon intensity to stabilize registration of sparse, noisy single-photon LiDAR point clouds.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical failure mode in long-range single-photon 3D sensing where geometry alone is too weak for reliable alignment.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces GIC-Reg, a geometry-intensity coupled registration framework for pose-free fusion of single-photon point clouds. Its pipeline combines physics-aware preprocessing, joint geometry-intensity grid feature aggregation, global matching, and local ambiguity disambiguation to estimate rigid inter-view transforms and build globally consistent reconstructions. On synthetic benchmarks it achieves the best relative rotation, translation, and RMSE metrics across noise and dropout settings, cutting relative rotation error from 13.167 degrees to 8.459 degrees in the hardest dropout case, and it also improves alignment on real data collected at about 80 m.
main_result: GIC-Reg uses intensity-informed registration to outperform baseline multiview fusion methods on sparse, noisy single-photon sensing data.
application_angle: This can improve deployable long-range 3D imaging in low-light, low-reflectance, or degraded-visibility environments where accurate pose information is unavailable.

title: Universal Characterization of Classical Qubit Noise
abstract: We propose a general method to fully characterize a classical stochastic noise process causing qubit dephasing through repetitive Ramsey interferometry measurements (RIMs) on the qubit. Compared to filter-function-based spectroscopy, our method does not require complicated dynamical decoupling pulses and can directly detect arbitrary-order correlation functions of such noise processes. We show that each RIM with a short evolution time and suitably chosen control pulses can perform a direct sampling of the noise field and the $n$-point correlations of the RIM outcomes are proportional to the $n$-point correlation functions of the noise processes. Then we numerically demonstrate this method for characterizing two typical examples of classical noises, including the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck processes producing Gaussian noises and an ensemble of TLFs producing non-Gaussian noises. Our method is independent of qubit lifetime and robust against qubit decoherence and measurement errors, thus offering a universal and efficient protocol for qubit noise spectroscopy across diverse platforms.
one_liner: Repetitive Ramsey interferometry is used to directly sample and reconstruct arbitrary-order classical dephasing noise seen by a qubit.
why_it_matters: It offers a platform-agnostic, pulse-light alternative to filter-function spectroscopy for diagnosing both Gaussian and non-Gaussian qubit noise.
detailed_summary: The authors propose a universal protocol in which short-time repetitive Ramsey interferometry measurements directly sample a classical stochastic field acting on a qubit. With suitable control pulses, n-point correlations of Ramsey outcomes become proportional to the n-point correlation functions of the underlying noise, allowing reconstruction beyond second-order spectra. Numerical demonstrations cover Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Gaussian noise and non-Gaussian noise from ensembles of two-level fluctuators, and the method is argued to be insensitive to qubit lifetime limitations and robust to decoherence and measurement error.
main_result: The method fully characterizes classical qubit dephasing noise, including arbitrary-order correlations, using repetitive Ramsey measurements rather than complex decoupling sequences.
application_angle: It is practically useful for calibrating and improving qubit performance across hardware platforms by exposing detailed noise statistics with minimal control overhead.

title: Ground-state energies of Ising models calculated using the samples from a quantum computer that simulates short-time evolution
abstract: We find the ground-state energy of the Ising model using the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver (CVQE) algorithm with the Guided-Sampling Ansatz (GSA) using up to 63 qubits on a quantum computer. We study a heavy-hex lattice to match the qubit architecture, allowing us to perform calculations in the quantum utility regime. We study both a homogeneous and random-coupling model. We locate the boundary of acceptable quantum errors as a function of the number of qubits and coupling strength. An entropic analysis is performed giving insights into the quantum computing performance. A subspace analysis is performed that suggests that the Ising model is especially suited for near-term quantum computing.
one_liner: A guided-sampling CVQE computes Ising ground-state energies on up to 63 heavy-hex qubits using short-time evolution on real quantum hardware.
why_it_matters: It probes the quantum-utility regime for a nontrivial optimization problem while identifying hardware error boundaries for useful performance.
detailed_summary: The paper applies the Cascaded Variational Quantum Eigensolver with a Guided-Sampling Ansatz to estimate Ising-model ground-state energies on hardware with up to 63 qubits. The study uses heavy-hex instances matched to device connectivity and examines both homogeneous and random-coupling cases while mapping the boundary of acceptable quantum error as a function of system size and coupling strength. Entropic and subspace analyses suggest that the Ising model is particularly compatible with near-term quantum computation in this architecture.
main_result: The authors demonstrate Ising ground-state estimation with CVQE and guided sampling on real devices up to 63 qubits in a regime relevant to quantum utility.
application_angle: This informs practical near-term quantum optimization workflows by showing where hardware connectivity and error levels still permit useful many-qubit energy estimation.

title: Coherence Revivals and Lifetime Extension of Polariton Condensates by Mirror-Mediated Self-Feedback
abstract: Temporal coherence of driven-dissipative condensates is limited by phase noise. We show that mirror-mediated time-delayed self-feedback enables control of coherence in a trapped exciton-polariton condensate. Reinjecting a small fraction of the emitted light with a tunable delay reveals two regimes set by the ratio of delay time to intrinsic coherence time. Long delays result in pronounced coherence revivals at integer multiples of the feedback delay, while short delays suppress phase diffusion and nearly double the coherence time. A minimal stochastic delayed model reproduces both regimes and supports an interpretation in terms of phase stabilization and delay-induced spectral filtering.
one_liner: Time-delayed optical self-feedback revives and extends coherence in trapped exciton-polariton condensates.
why_it_matters: It shows a simple feedback knob for stabilizing nonequilibrium polariton hardware, potentially improving coherence without redesigning the device.
detailed_summary: The paper studies a driven-dissipative exciton-polariton condensate with mirror-mediated reinjection of a small fraction of emitted light after a tunable delay. Two regimes emerge: for delays longer than the intrinsic coherence time, the first-order coherence exhibits revivals at integer multiples of the delay, while for short delays the feedback suppresses phase diffusion and nearly doubles the coherence time. A stochastic delayed model reproduces both behaviors and attributes them to phase stabilization plus delay-induced spectral filtering.
main_result: Tunable mirror-mediated self-feedback produces coherence revivals for long delays and nearly twofold coherence-time extension for short delays.
application_angle: The method offers a lightweight photonic control strategy for boosting coherence in polariton-based quantum and neuromorphic hardware.

title: Quantum Error Correction Exploiting Quantum Spatial Distribution and Gauge Symmetry
abstract: We explore what the integrated use of quantum spatial distribution (QSD), or more specifically, superposition of both spin and position states of particles, and gauge symmetry (GS) within stabilizer formalism provides for quantum error correction. The exploration employs $3+2$ particles on nested squares proposed in the companion letter (arXiv:2504.07941), where three of them encode Shor's nine-qubit code and the remaining two detect errors in this code through their spin state measurements (unlike the letter's quantum walk model, each particle evolves by gate operations acting exclusively on either its spin or position state). The first result is that the GS offers resilience against three types of noise acting on a particle: arbitrary decoherence of its spin or position state, and dephasing of both states, which partly or completely destroys its QSD. To show that, we formulate a noise model unifying the above noise and prove the correctability of this unified model under our error-correcting scheme. The second result is that QSD provides architectural flexibility allowing us to stack the error-correcting systems both vertically and horizontally. Indeed, we show implementations of the error detection (stabilizer measurement), logical Hadamard and Toffoli gates, and a quantum adder with the required interactions only between nearest-neighbor and next-nearest-neighbor particles.
one_liner: A mixed spin-position encoding with gauge symmetry enables fault-tolerant primitives using only short-range interactions.
why_it_matters: It proposes a hardware-aware error-correction architecture that trades standard qubit overhead for spatially distributed encoding and local connectivity.
detailed_summary: The work integrates quantum spatial distribution, where particles occupy superposed spin and position states, with gauge-symmetric stabilizer constructions. Using a 3+2 particle nested-square layout, the authors prove correction of a unified noise model covering arbitrary spin decoherence, position decoherence, and dephasing that degrades the spin-position superposition. They further show implementations of stabilizer measurements, logical Hadamard and Toffoli gates, and a quantum adder using only nearest- and next-nearest-neighbor couplings, with vertical and horizontal stackability.
platform_or_system: spin-position particle encoding on nested squares
main_result: The scheme corrects a unified spin/position noise model and supports logical gates and arithmetic with strictly local interactions.
application_angle: If physically realizable, the architecture could ease routing and connectivity constraints in scalable fault-tolerant quantum processors.

title: Quantum sensing-enabled deuterium NMR spectroscopy with nanoscale sensitivity at low magnetic fields
abstract: Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy provides unparalleled access to molecular structure and dynamics but is traditionally limited by weak signal strength, requiring large sample volumes and high magnetic fields. Here, we demonstrate nanoscale deuterium (2H) NMR spectroscopy using nitrogen vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, reproducing the characteristic quadrupolar powder line shapes that are present in the conventional bulk NMR spectra. By detecting statistical spin fluctuations from nanometer scale detection volumes, our approach delivers a sensitivity enhancement of six to eight orders of magnitude over inductive detection while operating at magnetic fields two orders of magnitude lower than those used in conventional NMR. Temperature dependent measurements of a deuterated polymer and molecular solid reveal distinct motional averaging and phase transitions with nanoscale sensitivity. Powder-like NV detected 2H NMR establishes a powerful tool for probing molecular dynamics on the nanoscale and, in the ultimate limit, at the single molecule level - capabilities beyond those of most existing spectroscopic techniques.
one_liner: NV centers detect nanoscale deuterium NMR at low field while preserving quadrupolar powder-line spectral signatures.
why_it_matters: It extends quantum-diamond NMR to deuterium and molecular dynamics, enabling chemically specific nanoscale spectroscopy beyond inductive limits.
detailed_summary: The authors use nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond to perform nanoscale 2H NMR spectroscopy by sensing statistical spin fluctuations from nanometric detection volumes. The measurements recover the characteristic quadrupolar powder line shapes familiar from bulk deuterium NMR, despite operating at fields roughly two orders of magnitude lower than conventional systems. Temperature-dependent studies on a deuterated polymer and a molecular solid resolve motional averaging and phase transitions, with reported sensitivity gains of six to eight orders of magnitude over inductive detection.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates low-field, nanoscale deuterium NMR with bulk-like quadrupolar spectra and sensitivity far beyond inductive methods.
application_angle: This opens a route to local structural and dynamical spectroscopy of thin films, soft matter, and potentially single molecules.

title: Numerically-Exact Quantum-Simulation Approach for Two-Dimensional Spectroscopy of Open Quantum Systems
abstract: Two-dimensional spectroscopy (2DS) is a powerful ultrafast technique for probing electronic and vibrational dynamics in complex microscopic systems. Extracting detailed information on system dynamics and system-bath interactions from 2DS experiments requires precise theoretical simulations for comparison, which motivates the development of numerically-exact and computationally-efficient simulation approaches. Here, we propose a quantum-simulation approach for 2DS based on the bath-engineering technique (BET), which has been successfully employed in quantum simulations of open quantum dynamics. To demonstrate our approach, we first simulate the 2DS of a driven four-level system in chiral enantiodetection, where we also assess the applicability of the center-line slope (CLS) method for extracting time correlation functions (TCFs) from the 2DS. We further apply our approach to the 2DS of ${\rm Rh(CO)_2C_5H_7O_2}$ (RDC) dissolved in chloroform, where the results reproduce the main spectral patterns observed in experiments. Our work provides a numerically-exact and efficient framework for simulating 2DS, and can offer additional insight into the dynamics of open quantum systems.
one_liner: Bath-engineered quantum simulation gives numerically exact two-dimensional spectroscopy for open quantum systems.
why_it_matters: It provides a practical exact-modeling route for interpreting ultrafast spectra where environment-driven dynamics matter strongly.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a quantum-simulation framework for two-dimensional spectroscopy based on bath-engineering techniques previously used for open-system dynamics. It is benchmarked on a driven four-level chiral enantiodetection model, including an assessment of how reliably center-line slope analysis extracts time-correlation information. The method is then applied to Rh(CO)2C5H7O2 in chloroform, reproducing the main experimental 2D spectral features while retaining numerical exactness.
platform_or_system: bath-engineered quantum simulation of open systems
main_result: The approach reproduces key 2D spectral patterns in model and molecular systems with numerically exact treatment of system-bath dynamics.
application_angle: Better exact forward models can improve extraction of microscopic dynamics from ultrafast spectroscopy in chemistry, materials, and photonic quantum simulators.

title: Lottery BP: Unlocking Quantum Error Decoding at Scale
abstract: To enable fault tolerance on millions of qubits in real time, scalable decoding is necessary, which motivates this paper. Existing decoding algorithms (decoders), such as clustering, matching, belief propagation (BP), and neural networks, suffer from one or more of inaccuracy, costliness, and incompatibility, upon a broad set of quantum error correction codes, such as surface code, toric code, and bivariate bicycle code. Therefore, there exists a gap between existing decoders and an ideal decoder that is accurate, fast, general, and scalable simultaneously. This paper contributes in three aspects, including decoder, decoder architecture, and decoding simulator. First, we propose Lottery BP, a decoder that introduces randomness during decoding. Lottery BP improves the decoding accuracy over BP by 2~8 orders of magnitude for topological codes. To efficiently decode multi-round measurement errors, we propose syndrome vote as a pre-processing step before Lottery BP, which compresses multiple rounds of syndromes into one. Syndrome voting increases the latency margin of decoding and mitigates the backlog problem. Second, we design a PolyQec architecture that implements Lottery BP as a local decoder and ordered statistics decoding (OSD) as a global decoder, and it is configurable for surface/toric code and X/Z check. Since Lottery BP boosts the local decoding accuracy, PolyQec invokes the costly global OSD decoder less frequently over BP+OSD to enhance the scalability, e.g., 3~5 orders of magnitude less for topological codes. Third, to evaluate decoders fairly, we develop a PyTorch-based decoding simulator, Syndrilla, that modularizes the simulation pipeline and allows to extend new decoders flexibly. We formulate multiple metrics to quantify the performance of decoders and integrate them in Syndrilla. Running on GPUs, Syndrilla is 1~2 orders of magnitude faster than CPUs.
one_liner: Lottery BP adds randomness to belief propagation and sharply improves scalable quantum error decoding.
why_it_matters: Real-time decoding is a core bottleneck for large fault-tolerant machines, and this work targets both accuracy and throughput at scale.
detailed_summary: The authors propose Lottery BP, a randomized belief-propagation decoder that improves logical error performance over standard BP by 2 to 8 orders of magnitude on topological codes. For repeated syndrome extraction, they add syndrome vote, which compresses multiple measurement rounds into one and increases latency margin by reducing decoder backlog. They also present the PolyQec local-plus-global decoding architecture and the GPU-based Syndrilla simulator, reporting 3 to 5 orders of magnitude fewer costly OSD invocations and 1 to 2 orders of magnitude simulation speedups over CPU baselines.
main_result: Lottery BP substantially boosts decoding accuracy and reduces expensive global-decoder usage in scalable QEC workflows.
application_angle: The decoder and simulator are directly relevant to classical-control stacks needed for million-qubit fault-tolerant quantum hardware.

title: The mixed-dimensional quantum MacWilliams identity: bounds for codes and absolutely maximally entangled states in heterogeneous systems
abstract: As emerging quantum architectures evolve into heterogeneous networks combining different physical substrates, such as qubits for logic and higher-dimensional qudits for robust communication, the traditional scalar metrics of quantum error correction become insufficient. To address this, we introduce a mathematical framework based on dimension multisets to characterize quantum error-correcting codes (QECC) and absolutely maximally entangled (AME) states in mixed-dimensional Hilbert spaces. By replacing scalar weights with multisets, we accurately capture the exact physical composition of error supports across these diverse systems. Our central result is the mixed-dimensional quantum MacWilliams identity, which establishes the formal algebraic relationship between Shor-Laflamme enumerators and unitary weight enumerators. From this foundation, we deduce the mixed-dimensional shadow identity and derive rigorous, generalized constraints on code parameters, explicitly formulating the mixed-dimensional quantum Hamming, Singleton and Scott bounds, and developing a linear program to systematically evaluate code viability. For the Singleton bound, a tighter bound that has no homogeneous analogue is derived for pure mixed-dimensional codes. Finally, we deploy this enumerator machinery to thoroughly analyze AME states, utilizing shadow inequalities to constrain their existence and introducing a combinatorial grid method for the explicit construction of mixed-dimensional tripartite AME states.
one_liner: A mixed-dimensional MacWilliams identity yields coding and entanglement bounds for heterogeneous quantum systems.
why_it_matters: As quantum platforms mix qubits and qudits, this gives the algebraic tools needed to assess viable code and AME constructions.
detailed_summary: This paper generalizes quantum enumerator theory from homogeneous Hilbert spaces to mixed-dimensional systems by replacing scalar weights with dimension multisets that track the exact substrate composition of error supports. From the mixed-dimensional quantum MacWilliams identity, the authors derive shadow relations and generalized Hamming, Singleton, and Scott bounds, including a tighter pure-code Singleton bound with no homogeneous counterpart. They also formulate a linear-program feasibility test and develop a combinatorial grid construction for tripartite mixed-dimensional absolutely maximally entangled states.
main_result: The work establishes the core enumerator identities and parameter bounds needed to analyze QECCs and AME states in mixed-dimensional architectures.
application_angle: These tools are useful for designing error correction and entanglement resources in hybrid photonic, atomic, and networked quantum platforms.

title: Hidden Hermiticity of the Jaynes-Cummings Projected Liouvillian
abstract: The Jaynes-Cummings model has been studied for six decades, yet its Nakajima-Zwanzig projected Liouvillian QLQ hides an unexpected structure: although manifestly non-Hermitian, its spectrum is purely real across all numerically accessible parameters. We resolve this anomaly by constructing a positive-definite metric eta > 0 such that (QLQ)^dag eta = eta (QLQ), proving that the generator of quantum memory is pseudo-Hermitian. The effect is genuine, not a block-diagonal artefact: the Delta N = 0 and Delta N = +/- 2 sectors remain strongly non-Hermitian, yet the global spectrum is protected by eta. Building on Ng and Rabani's identification of exceptional-point physics in QLQ, we show that the Jaynes-Cummings point sits inside a symmetry-protected pseudo-Hermitian phase. Under continuous deformation toward the full Rabi model this protection breaks and re-enters, producing a U(1) -> complex -> Z_2 phase structure bracketed by exceptional-point boundaries. The result supplies a structural reason for Hardy-space analyticity of the memory kernel in the canonical quantum-optical model and identifies a phase transition between oscillatory and genuinely dissipative quantum memory.
one_liner: This work proves the projected Jaynes-Cummings memory generator is pseudo-Hermitian, explaining its unexpectedly real spectrum.
why_it_matters: It identifies a symmetry-protected phase structure behind quantum-memory dynamics, sharpening theory for open-system behavior relevant to light-matter interfaces.
detailed_summary: The paper studies the Nakajima-Zwanzig projected Liouvillian QLQ for the Jaynes-Cummings model and shows that, despite being manifestly non-Hermitian, it has a purely real spectrum across accessible parameters. The authors construct a positive-definite metric eta satisfying (QLQ)^dag eta = eta (QLQ), proving pseudo-Hermiticity of the memory generator rather than an accidental block effect. They further track how this protection evolves under deformation toward the Rabi model, finding a U(1) to complex to Z_2 phase structure bounded by exceptional points. This gives a structural explanation for analyticity of the memory kernel and distinguishes oscillatory from genuinely dissipative memory regimes.
main_result: A positive-definite metric is constructed that renders the projected Jaynes-Cummings Liouvillian pseudo-Hermitian and explains its real spectrum and phase boundaries.
application_angle: The result offers a principled framework for analyzing memory effects in cavity-QED-style quantum communication and network node models.

title: Proof of the Error Scaling for Universally Robust Dynamical Decoupling Sequences
abstract: Universally robust dynamical decoupling (UR$n$) sequences were proposed to compensate pulse imperfections arising from arbitrary experimental parameters while achieving high-order error suppression with only a linear increase in the number of pulses. Although their performance was supported by analytical arguments, numerical simulations, and experiments, a complete mathematical proof of the claimed order of error compensation has been absent. In this work, we present a rigorous proof for UR$n$ DD sequences with even $n$. Using a series expansion of a quantity whose modulus is the fidelity $F$, we derive necessary and sufficient conditions for the cancellation of its coefficients up to, but not including, order $n$. The UR$n$ phase prescription satisfies these conditions, and therefore $1-F=O(ε^n)$. Our results establish the UR$n$ construction on firm analytical grounds and clarify the structure responsible for its high-order robustness.
one_liner: A rigorous proof establishes that even-order URn dynamical-decoupling sequences suppress infidelity as O(epsilon^n).
why_it_matters: It puts a widely used robust control method on firm mathematical footing for precision quantum sensing and coherence protection.
detailed_summary: The authors provide a complete proof for universally robust URn dynamical decoupling sequences with even n. By expanding a fidelity-related quantity in powers of the control error epsilon, they derive necessary and sufficient conditions for cancellation of terms through order n-1. They then show the URn phase prescription satisfies those conditions, yielding 1-F = O(epsilon^n). The analysis clarifies why high-order robustness can be achieved with only linear pulse-count growth.
main_result: The paper proves that even-order URn sequences cancel lower-order pulse-error contributions and achieve infidelity scaling of order epsilon to the n.
application_angle: This strengthens the case for deploying UR sequences in experimental sensors and qubit platforms that need robust coherence preservation under imperfect control.

title: A unified quantum random walk model for internal crystal effects in dynamical diffraction
abstract: The theory of dynamical diffraction (DD) in perfect crystals is the backbone of high-precision neutron and X-ray diffraction experiments, enabling accurate determination of crystal structure factors and the realization of perfect crystal interferometers. In practice, however, real crystals exhibit deformations and imperfections, including surface roughness, defects, temperature gradients, angled crystal faces, and curvature, that degrade interferometer performance and are difficult to model using conventional DD theory, particularly in complex geometries. To address these challenges, a quantum information (QI) model for DD has been under development, with demonstrated experimental agreement for both ideal crystals and in the presence of some imperfections such as surface roughness and defects. Here, we present a unified quantum random walk model that is now suitable for reproducing all established DD effects. We demonstrate this by incorporating a broad range of internal crystal effects influencing DD intensity distributions, including linear temperature gradients, the DD Talbot effect, and angled or miscut crystals. These results establish the QI model as a comprehensive and flexible framework for experimental analysis, as well as for the design of next-generation perfect crystal neutron interferometers and neutron optical components, such as condensing monochromators.
one_liner: A unified quantum random-walk framework reproduces internal-crystal effects in neutron and X-ray dynamical diffraction.
why_it_matters: It provides a flexible modeling tool for designing and diagnosing high-precision crystal interferometers and neutron optical components.
detailed_summary: This work extends a quantum-information-based model of dynamical diffraction into a unified quantum random walk description that captures a broad set of real-crystal effects. The framework incorporates temperature gradients, surface and internal imperfections, crystal face angles, miscuts, curvature, and the dynamical-diffraction Talbot effect while remaining consistent with established DD behavior. The authors position the model as a practical alternative where conventional DD becomes cumbersome in complex geometries. Its scope is aimed directly at experimental analysis and component design for perfect-crystal interferometry.
main_result: The model reproduces established dynamical-diffraction phenomena while incorporating diverse internal crystal effects within one quantum random-walk framework.
application_angle: It can guide optimization of next-generation neutron interferometers and condensing monochromators used in precision metrology and materials analysis.

title: Minimum Toffoli depth for the multi-controlled Toffoli gate via teleportation
abstract: The decomposition of complex quantum operations into experimentally feasible gate sets has been a central challenge since the early development of quantum computing. The multi-controlled Toffoli (MCT) gate is a key example, with applications across a wide range of quantum algorithms, whose decomposition into smaller gates, however, typically leads to deep circuits. In this work, we introduce a teleportation-based decomposition that implements an arbitrary MCT gate with unit Toffoli depth, independent of the number of controls, while maintaining a relatively low Toffoli count compared to existing approaches. This is achieved at the cost of a linear overhead in ancilla qubits and the ability to distribute entangled pairs across distant qubits, a capability already available in several quantum computing platforms. We further demonstrate the advantages of this implementation in circuits that rely on MCT gates, such as the adder operator, quantum read-only memory, quantum neurons, and quantum decision trees.
one_liner: A teleportation-based construction implements arbitrary multi-controlled Toffoli gates with unit Toffoli depth.
why_it_matters: Constant-depth nonlocal logic can reduce bottlenecks in distributed and communication-enabled quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a decomposition of the multi-controlled Toffoli gate that keeps Toffoli depth fixed at one regardless of the number of controls. The construction relies on teleportation resources, linear ancilla overhead, and the ability to distribute entangled pairs between distant qubits. Compared with standard decompositions, it preserves a relatively low Toffoli count while eliminating depth growth in the non-Clifford layer. The authors also show advantages for larger routines including adders, QROM, quantum neurons, and decision-tree circuits.
main_result: Arbitrary multi-controlled Toffoli gates are realized with unit Toffoli depth using teleportation and linearly many ancillas.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant to networked and modular quantum hardware where remote entanglement can trade qubits for much lower logical-circuit latency.

title: MCMit: Mid-Circuit Measurement Error Mitigation
abstract: Distributed Quantum Computing (DQC) and Quantum Error Correction (QEC) rely on dynamic circuits that include Mid-Circuit Measurements (MCMs) and classical feedback. These operations present a major bottleneck: MCMs suffer from high error rates that lead to real-time branching errors, while MCM and classical feedback latencies amplify decoherence errors. Current hardware controllers, qubit-state discriminators, and software error mitigation techniques fail to address these challenges holistically. We propose MCMit, a hardware-software co-design to mitigate branching and latency-induced errors. MCMit introduces a scalable, constant-latency multi-control branch instruction for faster classical feedback and two qubit-state discriminators, a transformer, and a CNN, with high accuracy even under short measurement durations. On the software side, static MCM elimination and stochastic branching complement the hardware by mitigating residual branching errors that persist despite hardware improvements. We implement MCMit on Qubic and evaluate it using experimentally extracted QPU readout traces. Our branch instruction reduces feedback latency by up to 70\%, improving circuit depths by up to $7\times$ over Qubic. Our CNN discriminator achieves 37-73\% higher accuracy for short measurement durations than the baselines, leading to up to 80\% lower logical error rates in QEC. Last, our software mitigation improves fidelity by 18--30\% over baseline methods.
one_liner: MCMit co-designs controller instructions, ML readout, and software mitigation to cut mid-circuit measurement errors and latency.
why_it_matters: Mid-circuit measurement is a core bottleneck for error correction and distributed quantum computing, so practical mitigation directly impacts usable hardware depth.
detailed_summary: MCMit targets both branching errors and latency in dynamic quantum circuits by combining hardware and software techniques. On the hardware side, it adds a constant-latency multi-control branch instruction and introduces transformer- and CNN-based state discriminators trained on experimentally extracted QPU readout traces. On the software side, it uses static mid-circuit-measurement elimination and stochastic branching to suppress residual errors. Evaluated on Qubic, the approach reports up to 70% lower feedback latency, up to 7x deeper effective circuits, up to 80% lower logical error rates in QEC, and 18-30% higher fidelity than baselines.
main_result: The co-designed stack substantially improves mid-circuit measurement fidelity and feedback latency, yielding major gains in logical error rate and executable depth.
application_angle: This is directly useful for near-term QEC and distributed workloads that depend on fast, reliable measurement-conditioned control.

title: Optimized thermal control of a dual-wavelength-resonant nonlinear cavity
