abstract: Optical resonator-enhanced nonlinear interactions are of great importance for the efficient generation of continuous-wave second harmonic generation, optical parametric oscillation, frequency mixing, and the generation of squeezed light. In order to maximize these interactions within the intra-cavity nonlinear material, high intensities, optimal phase matching, and simultaneous resonance of all interacting fields are required. However, the dispersion of the optical resonator often prevents the co-resonance of multiple wavelengths. Here, we present a novel implementation using a monolithic bimetallic heat sink for controlling the resonator dispersion based on a shallow temperature gradient directly applied to a section of the nonlinear crystal. This method enables precise dispersion control and is designed to minimize mechanical and thermal stresses in the nonlinear crystal, thus providing an additional method for designing highly efficient and reliable resonator-enhanced nonlinear devices for demanding applications such as gravitational wave detection, quantum optics, and frequency conversion.
one_liner: A bimetallic heat-sink scheme tunes cavity dispersion to enable dual-wavelength resonance in nonlinear optical cavities.
why_it_matters: Reliable multiwavelength co-resonance is critical for efficient squeezing, frequency conversion, and other cavity-enhanced nonlinear photonic functions.
detailed_summary: The authors present a monolithic bimetallic heat sink that applies a controlled shallow temperature gradient to part of a nonlinear crystal inside a resonator. This locally adjusts dispersion so that interacting wavelengths can be simultaneously resonant while maintaining favorable phase matching and high intracavity intensity. The design is engineered to reduce thermal and mechanical stress in the crystal compared with more intrusive tuning methods. The approach is positioned as a practical route to more efficient and reliable resonator-enhanced nonlinear devices.
main_result: Localized thermal-gradient control enables precise dispersion tuning for dual-wavelength resonance in a nonlinear cavity.
application_angle: The method can improve deployable sources for squeezed-light generation, frequency conversion, and other photonic systems used in sensing and quantum optics.

title: QCalEval: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for Quantum Calibration Plot Understanding
abstract: Quantum computing calibration depends on interpreting experimental data, and calibration plots provide the most universal human-readable representation for this task, yet no systematic evaluation exists of how well vision-language models (VLMs) interpret them. We introduce QCalEval, the first VLM benchmark for quantum calibration plots: 243 samples across 87 scenario types from 22 experiment families, spanning superconducting qubits and neutral atoms, evaluated on six question types in both zero-shot and in-context learning settings. The best general-purpose zero-shot model reaches a mean score of 72.3, and many open-weight models degrade under multi-image in-context learning, whereas frontier closed models improve substantially. A supervised fine-tuning ablation at the 9-billion-parameter scale shows that SFT improves zero-shot performance but cannot close the multimodal in-context learning gap. As a reference case study, we release NVIDIA Ising Calibration 1, an open-weight model based on Qwen3.5-35B-A3B that reaches 74.7 zero-shot average score.
one_liner: QCalEval benchmarks how well vision-language models read and reason about quantum calibration plots across major hardware modalities.
why_it_matters: Calibration is a core experimental bottleneck, and this benchmark quantifies whether multimodal AI can reliably assist hardware tuning workflows.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces QCalEval, a benchmark of 243 calibration-plot samples spanning 87 scenario types and 22 experiment families drawn from superconducting-qubit and neutral-atom systems. It evaluates six question types under zero-shot and in-context settings, showing that strong general-purpose closed models improve substantially with multimodal context while many open-weight models degrade. A supervised fine-tuning study at the 9B scale improves zero-shot accuracy but does not recover the multimodal in-context gap. The authors also release an open reference model, NVIDIA Ising Calibration 1, reaching a 74.7 average zero-shot score.
platform_or_system: Quantum calibration plots for superconducting and neutral-atom hardware
main_result: It establishes the first systematic benchmark for quantum calibration-plot understanding and shows frontier closed VLMs outperform open models, especially in multimodal in-context use.
application_angle: The benchmark directly targets AI-assisted calibration, diagnosis, and operator support for experimental quantum processors.

title: Testing a continuous-variable Bell-like inequality with a hybrid-encoded system
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum systems are promising candidates for quantum computing and quantum information processing. It is widely known that quadrature measurements on Gaussian continuous-variable systems can be described by a noncontextual hidden-variable model and cannot violate a Bell inequality. Here, we demonstrate that the observation fails when sequential measurements are involved. Our experiment is realized by mapping the spatial modes of a single photon, deterministically generated from an InAs/GaAs quantum emitter, to the logical operations in the Gottesman--Kitaev--Preskill code space. Employing a black-box-style approach, we observe a violation of the Bell-like noncontextual hidden-variable inequality by 380 standard deviations. Our results address the conceptual loopholes in previous works and open up new possibilities for studying fundamental quantum physics using photonic-encoded continuous-variable systems.
one_liner: A hybrid photonic continuous-variable system violates a Bell-like noncontextuality inequality using sequential measurements.
why_it_matters: It shows a route to nonclassical certification in CV photonics beyond standard Gaussian no-go limits, using an experimentally realistic encoded platform.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a deterministically generated single photon from an InAs/GaAs quantum emitter, with spatial modes mapped onto logical operations in a Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill code space. By using sequential measurements in a black-box-style protocol, the authors demonstrate that the usual noncontextual hidden-variable description for Gaussian CV quadrature measurements fails in this setting. They report a Bell-like inequality violation by 380 standard deviations, addressing conceptual loopholes in prior work. The result turns hybrid-encoded photonic CV states into a concrete testbed for foundational and information-processing experiments.
platform_or_system: Single-photon spatial-mode CV photonic encoding from an InAs/GaAs emitter
main_result: The work experimentally demonstrates a 380-sigma violation of a continuous-variable Bell-like noncontextual hidden-variable inequality using sequential measurements.
application_angle: Hybrid photonic encodings with sequential readout could support robust nonclassical state verification for optical quantum information platforms.

title: Heralding probability optimization for nonclassical light generated by photon counting measurements on multimode Gaussian states
abstract: Generation of highly non-classical quantum states of light is essential for optical quantum information processing and quantum metrology. Given the lack of sufficiently strong nonlinear interactions between optical fields, the commonly employed optical quantum-state preparation schemes are conditional, based on nonlinearity induced by heralding photon number measurement on a part of a multimode squeezed Gaussian state. Development and optimization of such probabilistic quantum-state engineering schemes represents one of the central challenges in current quantum optics. As technology advances and experiments progress to detection of higher numbers of photons, the maximization of the heralding probability becomes essential to ensure sufficiently high state-preparation rates. Here, we show that for the conditional quantum state preparation schemes based on Gaussian states and photon number measurements the maximization of the heralding probability can be formulated as finding solution to a system of polynomial equations, which offers an efficient way to find the optimal configuration and allows us to apply techniques dedicated specifically to solving such systems of equations. Our approach can seamlessly incorporate bounds on the available single-mode quadrature squeezing, which is highly experimentally relevant. We mainly consider generation of finite superpositions of Fock states but show that the approach can be straightforwardly extended to generation of squeezed superpositions of Fock states. We focus on Gaussian states with vanishing coherent displacements, hence the conditionally generated states have well defined photon number parity. We illustrate our general methodology on examples of generation of single-mode and two-mode states with two heralding modes.
one_liner: This work turns heralded nonclassical-state design in multimode Gaussian optics into a polynomial optimization problem for higher-rate generation.
why_it_matters: Better heralding probabilities directly improve usable state-preparation rates for photonic computing and metrology experiments constrained by loss and squeezing limits.
detailed_summary: The authors show that maximizing heralding probability for photon-number-conditioned state preparation from multimode squeezed Gaussian states can be reformulated as solving a system of polynomial equations. This provides an efficient route to optimize conditional generation schemes while incorporating experimentally important bounds such as limited single-mode quadrature squeezing. The framework is developed mainly for finite Fock-state superpositions, with a straightforward extension to squeezed Fock superpositions, and examples include single-mode and two-mode targets with two heralding modes. The treatment focuses on zero-displacement Gaussian resources, so the prepared states have definite photon-number parity.
platform_or_system: Multimode squeezed Gaussian optical states with photon-number heralding
main_result: It provides an efficient polynomial-equation framework to optimize heralding probability for conditional generation of nonclassical optical states under realistic squeezing constraints.
application_angle: Higher-probability heralded sources are directly useful for scalable photonic quantum processors and quantum-enhanced sensing schemes needing non-Gaussian light.

title: High-fidelity entangling gates and nonlocal circuits with neutral atoms
abstract: Creation and manipulation of entanglement with low error is essential in quantum information systems. In practice, two-qubit entangling gates constitute a dominant error source, limiting circuit depths and performance in fault-tolerant architectures. Using a neutral-atom quantum processor, we realize entangling CZ gates with a high Rabi frequency smooth-amplitude pulse, employing state-selective readout and qubit reuse for fast calibration, and achieve state-of-the-art fidelities of 99.854(4)% which improve to 99.941(3)% upon loss postselection, with stable performance for 10 hours. We then use these low-error gates in quantum circuits with coherent atom rearrangement. We first benchmark performance by creating and disentangling cluster states, and subsequently implement scrambling circuits featuring longer-range connectivity to study non-locally entangled states generated through chaotic dynamics. These results pave the way towards deep-circuit, efficient fault-tolerant quantum computation.
one_liner: A neutral-atom processor achieves state-of-the-art CZ fidelities and uses them in nonlocal circuits with atom rearrangement.
why_it_matters: Two-qubit error rates below 0.2% with stability over hours are a concrete step toward deeper neutral-atom circuits and fault-tolerant operation.
detailed_summary: Using a smooth-amplitude high-Rabi-frequency pulse plus state-selective readout and qubit reuse for rapid calibration, the authors realize neutral-atom CZ gates with 99.854(4)% fidelity, improving to 99.941(3)% under loss postselection. Performance remains stable for 10 hours, indicating a robust operating point rather than a transient best case. They then combine these gates with coherent atom rearrangement to build and invert cluster states and to implement scrambling circuits with longer-range connectivity. This links high-fidelity local entangling operations to larger nonlocal circuit primitives on the same platform.
main_result: The processor demonstrates CZ gates up to 99.941(3)% fidelity with loss postselection and deploys them in cluster-state and scrambling circuits with coherent atom movement.
application_angle: The combination of low-error entangling gates and dynamic connectivity is directly relevant to deep-circuit quantum computing and fault-tolerant neutral-atom architectures.

title: Towards a microscopic model for an electronic quantum charge liquid
abstract: We provide a route to constructing an electronic quantum charge liquid (QCL), a state made up of fermions at fractional filling of a lattice that does not break translation. Starting with spinless fermions at filling $ν=3/2$ we pair them to get bosons at filling $ν=3/4$ per unit cell. The tetramer model, a generalization of the dimer model, on the square lattice is evaluated as a candidate bosonic QCL at filling $ν= 3/4$. It is shown that these models exhibit a local $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry. Upon numerical study of a family of tetramer wavefunctions it is found that while one is gapless due to $\mathrm{U}(1)^3$ symmetry at least one other can be definitively shown to be gapped. The gapped nature of this state, along with its $\mathbb{Z}_4$ symmetry, leads us to propose that it is an example of the elusive bosonic QCL displaying the minimal $\mathbb{Z}_4$ topological order. We conclude by discussing possible extensions to other lattice geometries, electronic QCLs, and to Rydberg atoms.
one_liner: A tetramer-lattice construction is proposed as a microscopic route to a gapped quantum charge liquid with minimal Z4 topological order.
why_it_matters: It offers a concrete many-body model that could guide engineered platforms seeking translation-invariant fractionalized charge-liquid phases.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs a route to an electronic quantum charge liquid by starting from spinless fermions at filling nu=3/2, pairing them into bosons at nu=3/4 per unit cell, and analyzing a square-lattice tetramer model. The model exhibits a local Z4 symmetry, and numerical study of a family of tetramer wavefunctions identifies at least one gapped state, contrasting with another gapless state protected by U(1)^3 symmetry. The gapped phase is proposed as a bosonic quantum charge liquid with minimal Z4 topological order. The authors discuss extensions to other lattices, electronic realizations, and possible implementation links to Rydberg-atom systems.
platform_or_system: Square-lattice tetramer model for paired fermions
main_result: It identifies a gapped tetramer-state candidate consistent with a bosonic quantum charge liquid exhibiting minimal Z4 topological order.
application_angle: Although theoretical, the model provides a target phase and design language for programmable quantum simulators, including prospective Rydberg-atom implementations.

title: Arbitrary parallel entangling gates with independent calibration on a trapped ion quantum computer
abstract: Parallel processing of information plays a critical role in accelerating computation. This includes quantum computers, where parallel processing of quantum information will play a critical role in practical quantum advantage. Here, we demonstrate a new type of parallel entangling gates in a trapped-ion quantum computer, that simultaneously provides efficient gate-pulse synthesis and calibration, as well as graph-pattern-agnostic implementation. We demonstrate the resulting reduced execution time in three well-known algorithms, exhibiting disjoint gates, a star graph and a ring graph respectively. For disjoint qubit pairs the execution time of our parallel gates is comparable to that of a single-pair entangling gate resulting in an approximately linear speed up. For all graph patterns our parallel gate fidelities are comparable to the fidelity of a single-pair entangling gate. These advantages motivate architectures featuring multiple medium length ion chains in future quantum computing devices.
one_liner: Trapped ions realize graph-agnostic parallel entangling gates with independent calibration and near-single-gate fidelity.
why_it_matters: Parallel high-fidelity entangling operations reduce algorithm runtime and address a practical scaling bottleneck in trapped-ion processors.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a parallel entangling-gate scheme for trapped-ion quantum computers that supports efficient pulse synthesis, independent calibration, and implementation across arbitrary interaction graphs. For disjoint ion pairs, the total execution time is comparable to that of a single-pair entangling gate, yielding an approximately linear speedup. The authors also demonstrate star-graph and ring-graph cases in representative algorithms while maintaining fidelities comparable to single-pair gates. This combination of calibration practicality and graph flexibility is especially relevant for medium-length ion-chain architectures.
main_result: The system demonstrates independently calibrated parallel entangling gates across multiple graph patterns with runtimes near a single two-qubit gate and comparable fidelity.
application_angle: This capability can shorten circuit depth and wall-clock execution in practical trapped-ion algorithms, improving throughput on scalable ion-chain hardware.

title: Continuous Noise Model for Quantum Circuits
abstract: Quantum noise is a central challenge in quantum computing across many applications. Extensive work has examined how qubits couple to their environment, leading to decoherence and relaxation, which is irreversible. Current studies focus on coherent gate errors caused by control misalignment, which accumulate with circuit depth but can, in principle, be corrected. This work studies a continuous coherent noise model for quantum circuits and compares it with a discrete Pauli model. The focus is on small coherent gate errors that build up across circuit depth. These errors are modeled as random rotations on the Bloch sphere using a von Mises-Fisher distribution. In the small-angle limit, the model reduces to an isotropic Gaussian distribution. We test the model on quantum error-correction circuits based on the [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] codes. A variant of Grover's search circuit with different qubit counts is also examined. To enable fair comparison, we introduce a model-independent matching scheme. Pauli and continuous noise channels are aligned using the binary entropy at readout. This isolates the effect of noise structure at fixed uncertainty. An approximate analytical method for coherent error propagation is also developed. The method tracks error distributions through Clifford circuits without full Monte Carlo sampling. It reduces simulation cost while preserving accuracy for circuit-level error estimates. The approximation is validated against brute-force simulations, identifying its regime of validity with Clifford circuits and limits under error correction. Our results show that continuous coherent noise can degrade logical performance more strongly than Pauli noise. They also clarify when simplified propagation models succeed and when they break down.
one_liner: Introduces a continuous coherent-noise model for quantum circuits and benchmarks its impact against matched Pauli noise.
why_it_matters: It shows that structure in small coherent gate errors can hurt logical performance more than Pauli approximations, affecting hardware benchmarking and QEC studies.
detailed_summary: The paper models coherent control errors as random Bloch-sphere rotations drawn from a von Mises-Fisher distribution, with an isotropic Gaussian limit for small angles. It compares this continuous channel against discrete Pauli noise under a model-independent entropy-matching condition at readout, so differences come from noise structure rather than overall uncertainty. Benchmarks on [[5,1,3]] and [[7,1,3]] error-correction circuits and Grover variants show stronger logical degradation under continuous coherent noise. The authors also derive an approximate propagation method for Clifford circuits that reduces Monte Carlo cost while identifying where the approximation breaks down under error correction.
main_result: Matched at equal readout entropy, continuous coherent noise produces worse circuit and logical performance than comparable Pauli noise in the tested regimes.
application_angle: The model is directly useful for more realistic error budgeting, decoder assessment, and calibration targets in near-term superconducting or trapped-ion processors.

title: DFT-assisted natural abundance 13C zero-field NMR via optical magnetometry
abstract: Zero-field (ZF) nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy probes scalar J-couplings between nuclei while dispensing with large homogeneous magnetic fields, enabling low-cost and geometrically flexible detection, including through conductive enclosures. Despite these advantages, its broader use for chemical analysis has been limited by sensitivity and by the difficulty of predicting the dense spectral multiplets that arise at zero field. Here we demonstrate natural-abundance (1.1%) 13C ZF spectroscopy on off-the-shelf liquids using a compact commercial 87Rb magnetometer for the first time, without hyperpolarization or special sample preparation. Instrumental advances yield improved sensitivity, <250-mHz linewidths and >week-long stability, enabling isotopomer-resolved fingerprint spectra across a 13-molecule library, including the ability to discern rare (0.0121%) doubly 13C-labelled species. In parallel, we demonstrate vibrationally corrected density-functional theory (DFT) based prediction of ZF NMR spectra for chemically diverse molecules with few-hertz accuracy. Comparing experiment with these calculations renders residual deviations as chemically informative, reporting on hydrogen bonding, hydration and ion pairing at high ionic strength. Together, these results contribute towards DFT-assisted ZF NMR as a general platform for field-constraint-free molecular identification and for extracting transient solution-state structure from responsive J-coupling observables.
one_liner: Demonstrates natural-abundance 13C zero-field NMR on standard liquids using an optical 87Rb magnetometer plus DFT spectral prediction.
why_it_matters: It pushes zero-field NMR toward practical chemical analysis without big magnets, hyperpolarization, or isotope enrichment while improving interpretability.
detailed_summary: Using a compact commercial 87Rb optical magnetometer, the authors measure natural-abundance 13C zero-field NMR spectra from off-the-shelf liquid samples without hyperpolarization or special preparation. Instrument upgrades deliver sub-250 mHz linewidths and week-scale stability, enabling isotopomer-resolved fingerprints across 13 molecules and even detection of rare doubly 13C-labeled species at 0.0121% abundance. In parallel, vibrationally corrected DFT predicts zero-field spectra with few-hertz accuracy across chemically diverse molecules. Residual experiment-theory differences are shown to encode effects such as hydrogen bonding, hydration, and ion pairing.
main_result: The work achieves natural-abundance 13C zero-field NMR with high spectral resolution and pairs it with few-hertz-accurate DFT-assisted spectral assignment.
application_angle: This enables portable, field-free molecular identification and solution-state structure probing in settings where conventional high-field NMR is impractical.

title: Hardware-Efficient Quantum Optimization for Transportation Networks via Compressed Adiabatic Evolution
abstract: Transportation systems such as urban logistics, vehicle routing, and infrastructure planning require solving large-scale combinatorial optimization problems under complex constraints. Problems such as the vehicle routing problem (VRP), traveling salesman problem (TSP), and facility location problem (FLP) involve large discrete search spaces and the need to generate multiple feasible solutions in real time. In this work, we develop a hardware-grounded hybrid quantum optimization framework that uses Approximate Quantum Compilation (AQC) to compress early segments of digitized adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits. The compressed prefix is combined with variational layers, enabling a systematic study of how initialization, circuit depth, and expressivity interact on near-term quantum hardware. All experiments are performed on an IBM gate-based quantum computer, and circuits are evaluated as stochastic generators of candidate transportation plans. Results show that moderate prefix compression reduces two-qubit gate depth while maintaining or improving feasible solution discovery, particularly for routing problems. These benefits depend on compatibility between the compressed prefix and the variational ansatz: while standard QAOA effectively leverages AQC initialization, linear-chain QAOA shows limited improvement. Overall, this work demonstrates that hybrid AQC-QAOA methods provide a practical pathway for hardware-efficient quantum optimization, positioning quantum algorithms as candidate generators within transportation decision-making workflows.
one_liner: Uses approximate quantum compilation to compress adiabatic evolution into shallow circuits for transportation optimization on IBM hardware.
why_it_matters: It offers a hardware-aware route to useful quantum candidate generation for routing problems by trading circuit depth against feasible-solution quality.
detailed_summary: The authors build a hybrid optimization method that compresses early segments of digitized adiabatic evolution using Approximate Quantum Compilation, then appends variational layers. They test the resulting AQC-QAOA approach on transportation-inspired combinatorial problems, treating measured bitstrings as stochastic generators of feasible plans on an IBM gate-based processor. Moderate prefix compression lowers two-qubit depth while maintaining or improving feasible-solution discovery, especially for routing instances. Performance depends strongly on ansatz compatibility, with standard QAOA benefiting more than linear-chain QAOA from the compiled initialization.
main_result: Compressed adiabatic prefixes combined with suitable variational layers can reduce entangling-gate depth while preserving or improving feasible candidate generation for transportation tasks.
application_angle: The method is relevant for near-term quantum optimization workflows where shallow circuits must generate usable routing or logistics candidates under hardware constraints.

title: Algebraic quantum kinematics and SR-selection
abstract: We develop, as the first of a six-paper series, an operator-algebraic framework relating non-relativistic quantum mechanics and special relativity. Three structural facts organize the framework. (i)~The photon sector of free QED is a transparent realization: classical Fourier--Maxwell theory supplies a complex Hilbert-space scaffold (inner product, symplectic form, Schrödinger-form mode equation, polarization $\mathbb{C}^2$) with no quantum input, and a single canonical commutator with scale $\hbar$ on the mode amplitudes promotes it to single-photon QED, with photon indivisibility, the Planck relation $E=\hbarω$, and the spin spectrum $\pm\hbar$ as theorems. (ii)~The constants $c$ and $\hbar$ play non-interchangeable roles: $c$ is intrinsic to each Fourier-conjugate space, while $\hbar$ acts \emph{between} them, converting kinematic phase rates into dynamical observables. (iii)~Lifting the framework to a Haag--Kastler net with sharper-than-equal-time microcausality and positive-energy spectrum is structurally obstructed in the Galilean case; we state this as the SR-selection conjecture and identify three strands of evidence (Hegerfeldt spreading; absence of a known Galilean multi-particle resolution; Reeh--Schlieder failure on Galilean Haag--Kastler nets), the third established as a precise no-go theorem in the second paper of the series. The framework's modular content (Tomita--Takesaki, Bisognano--Wichmann, Unruh as KMS, type-$\mathrm{III}_1$ universality) is collected as the algebraic substrate on which the curved-background, dynamical-metric, and crossed-product extensions of the third through sixth papers operate.
one_liner: Develops an operator-algebraic framework linking photon kinematics, quantization, and a conjectured special-relativity selection principle.
why_it_matters: It reframes the photon sector as a mathematically transparent route from classical field structure to quantum behavior, with implications for relativistic quantum theory.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs an algebraic framework in which the free-photon sector of QED arises from classical Fourier-Maxwell theory equipped with Hilbert-space, symplectic, and mode-evolution structure before quantization. Imposing a single canonical commutator on mode amplitudes then yields single-photon quantization, including indivisibility, E = hbar omega, and spin ±hbar as derived consequences. It distinguishes the roles of c and hbar and argues that extending the construction to a Haag-Kastler net with sharp microcausality is structurally obstructed in the Galilean setting. This motivates the SR-selection conjecture and sets up later extensions to modular and curved-spacetime settings.
main_result: The main result is a formal operator-algebraic construction showing how single-photon quantum structure emerges from classical Fourier-Maxwell kinematics plus canonical commutation.
application_angle: While theoretical, the framework can sharpen how photonic quantum models are built and interpreted in relativistic quantum optics and field-based quantum technologies.

title: System-Level Design of Scalable Fluxonium Quantum Processors with Double-Transmon Couplers
abstract: Fluxonium qubits combine long coherence times with strong anharmonicity, making them a promising platform for scalable superconducting quantum processors. Recent experiments have demonstrated high-fidelity operations in multi-qubit processors while suppressing stray qubit interactions using fluxonium-transmon-fluxonium (FTF) architectures. However, scaling such systems to larger arrays is constrained by a trade-off between achievable coupling strength, crosstalk suppression and qubit-qubit spacing required for wiring in a two-dimensional architecture. Multimode couplers, such as the double-transmon coupler (DTC), provide a promising pathway to overcome this limitation by enabling stronger interactions without compromising qubit spacing and isolation. Here, we develop a quantitative design framework for fluxonium-based quantum processors employing DTCs. Central to this work is a frequency-partitioned architecture that places qubit transitions, tunable-coupler excitations, and resonator modes in well-separated spectral regions. This structured allocation reduces parameter interdependence and enables the concurrent optimization of gate operations, readout, and qubit reset. By formulating device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under realistic experimental constraints and fabrication-induced disorder, we develop a tractable sequential workflow and determine a feasible parameter regime that simultaneously supports high-fidelity single- and two-qubit gates, fast qubit reset, and robust dispersive readout. These results establish a system-level architectural methodology that links circuit parameters to processor-level performance, and provide an experimentally actionable pathway toward scalable fluxonium quantum processors.
one_liner: Presents a system-level design workflow for scalable fluxonium processors using double-transmon couplers and spectral partitioning.
why_it_matters: It addresses a key scaling bottleneck in superconducting hardware by co-optimizing coupling, crosstalk, readout, reset, and fabrication tolerance.
detailed_summary: The work studies fluxonium processors coupled through double-transmon couplers, targeting stronger interactions without sacrificing qubit spacing or isolation in 2D layouts. A frequency-partitioned architecture places qubit transitions, tunable-coupler modes, and resonator modes in separated spectral bands to reduce parameter entanglement during design. The authors cast device design as a multi-objective optimization problem under realistic constraints and fabrication disorder, then propose a sequential workflow to find viable parameter regimes. The resulting designs support simultaneous high-fidelity 1Q and 2Q gates, fast reset, and robust dispersive readout.
main_result: The paper identifies an experimentally feasible parameter regime and design methodology for scaling fluxonium processors while maintaining gate, reset, and readout performance.
application_angle: It provides an actionable architecture recipe for larger superconducting quantum chips where wiring, spacing, and crosstalk otherwise limit processor growth.

title: Quantum Complexity and New Directions in Nuclear Physics and High-Energy Physics Phenomenology
abstract: Advances in quantum information science (QIS) are providing transformative insights into the complexity of quantum many-body systems, potentially defining new frontiers in nuclear and high-energy physics. This review explores how QIS-derived techniques are fostering new analytic frameworks and algorithms - both classical and quantum - to tackle (some of the) present barriers to discovery in fundamental physics, with applicability to other science domains. We highlight how these techniques are shedding new light on the structure and dynamics of hadrons, nuclei, matter in extreme conditions, and beyond. Importantly, they are expected to play an essential role in the development of large-scale quantum simulations of such systems, particularly in setting the balance among quantum and classical computational resources.
one_liner: Reviews how quantum-information complexity tools are reshaping nuclear and high-energy physics phenomenology and simulation strategies.
why_it_matters: It maps where QIS methods can change computational practice in strongly correlated fundamental-physics problems that challenge classical approaches.
detailed_summary: This review surveys how quantum information concepts and complexity measures are being applied to hadrons, nuclei, and matter in extreme conditions. It emphasizes new analytic viewpoints and algorithmic tools, both classical and quantum, for addressing many-body structure and dynamics that remain difficult with standard methods. A central theme is resource balancing between classical and quantum computation in future large-scale simulations. The paper positions QIS as a framework for setting priorities and architectures in next-generation phenomenology workflows.
platform_or_system: quantum-information methods for HEP and nuclear systems
main_result: The main result is a synthesized roadmap showing how QIS-derived complexity tools and algorithms can guide simulation and phenomenology across nuclear and high-energy physics.
application_angle: Practically, it helps identify where quantum-enabled computation may become useful for modeling complex many-body systems beyond current classical limits.

title: Genuine tripartite entanglement in Bhabha scattering with an entangled spectator particle
abstract: From the perspective of quantum information science, we investigate tree-level Bhabha scattering between an incident electron $A$ and a positron B, where $B$ is initially entangled with a spectator electron $C$, which does not participate in the scattering interaction.We find that the quantum electrodynamics (QED) scattering between $A$ and $B$ can drive the global $ABC$ system into a genuine tripartite entangled (GTE) state. Using four canonical tripartite entanglement metrics, we systematically characterize and quantify the GTE of the composite system, and demonstrate that the scattering momentum of the $A$-$B$ pair and the initial $B$-$C$ entanglement are the key resources governing GTE generation.We further analyze the monogamy of quantum correlations, which imposes fundamental constraints on the shareability of quantum resources in multipartite systems. Specifically, we systematically study the monogamy relations for the squared entanglement of formation and squared quantum discord in our scattering model, and find that monogamy constraints are markedly relaxed in the non-relativistic regime, enabling enhanced shareability of quantum correlations across the three particles. This work uncovers novel quantum correlation properties of fundamental QED scattering processes, and provides direct theoretical guidance for the development of QED-based quantum information processing protocols.
one_liner: Tree-level Bhabha scattering with a spectator electron can generate genuine tripartite entanglement across three particles.
why_it_matters: It links a fundamental QED process to controllable multipartite entanglement generation, suggesting scattering-based quantum information primitives.
detailed_summary: The paper studies electron-positron Bhabha scattering where the incoming positron is initially entangled with a spectator electron that does not interact directly. Using four standard tripartite entanglement measures, the authors show that the scattering interaction can drive the full three-particle state into a genuinely tripartite-entangled regime. They identify scattering momentum and initial spectator-pair entanglement as the main control parameters, and analyze monogamy relations for squared entanglement of formation and squared quantum discord. In the non-relativistic regime, monogamy constraints are found to relax, allowing greater shareability of correlations across the three particles.
platform_or_system: electron-positron QED scattering with spectator electron
main_result: The authors demonstrate theoretically that Bhabha scattering can convert bipartite pre-entanglement into genuine tripartite entanglement in the full ABC system.
application_angle: The work provides a blueprint for using relativistic scattering processes as quantum-resource generators in future particle-based quantum sensing or information schemes.

title: Precision Spectroscopy of 2S-nS Transitions in Atomic Hydrogen: A Determination of the Proton Charge Radius
abstract: We present absolute frequency measurements of 2S_{1/2}-nS_{1/2} two-photon transitions with n = 8, 9, and 10 in a cryogenic beam of atomic hydrogen. Each transition has been measured with a fractional uncertainty of approximately 2.6*10^(-12). Combining the results from this work and the 1S_{1/2}-2S_{1/2} transition frequency, we extract a root-mean-square proton radius of r_p = 0.8433(31) fm and a Rydberg frequency of cR_{\infty} = 3,289,841,960,252.9(9.7) kHz. These are in good agreement with the CODATA 2022 recommended values.
one_liner: Cryogenic hydrogen spectroscopy of 2S-nS transitions yields a proton charge radius consistent with CODATA 2022.
why_it_matters: It pushes precision metrology in atomic hydrogen, strengthening quantum-control and frequency-reference tools relevant to quantum hardware and sensing.
detailed_summary: The experiment reports absolute frequency measurements of the 2S1/2-nS1/2 two-photon transitions in atomic hydrogen for n = 8, 9, and 10 using a cryogenic hydrogen beam. Each line is measured at roughly 2.6e-12 fractional uncertainty, enabling a joint fit with the 1S-2S transition. From that fit, the authors extract a proton root-mean-square charge radius of 0.8433(31) fm and a Rydberg frequency of 3,289,841,960,252.9(9.7) kHz. The values agree with CODATA 2022 and reinforce hydrogen spectroscopy as a benchmark platform for ultra-precise quantum measurement.
main_result: Absolute 2S-nS transition measurements in hydrogen determine the proton charge radius and Rydberg constant at state-of-the-art precision.
application_angle: The techniques improve optical frequency metrology and laser-stabilized spectroscopy infrastructure that underpins precision quantum devices and standards.

title: Large-Scale Quantum Circuit Simulation on an Exascale System for QPU Benchmarking
abstract: Recent advances in quantum computing have enabled the development of quantum processors with hundreds of qubits. However, noise continues to limit the amount of useful information that can be extracted from these systems, making it essential to identify the regime in which experimental outputs remain reliable. In this work, we benchmark Quantinuum Helios-1, a 98-qubit trapped-ion quantum processing unit, using the linear ramp quantum approximate optimization algorithm (LR-QAOA). To this end, we perform large-scale noiseless simulations on JUPITER, Europe's first exascale supercomputer, for circuits of up to 48 qubits and 3,384 two-qubit gates. These simulations, executed on 4,096 nodes equipped with 16,384 GH200 superchips and high-bandwidth CPU-GPU interconnects, provide a reference for validating experimental results at the edge of classical tractability. We find that, up to 48 qubits, Helios-1 remains in a noise-tolerant region, i.e., its samples cannot be clearly distinguished from those coming from a noiseless simulation. We then extend the analysis to larger system sizes using experimental data only, and apply a mean-of-means resampling procedure with a 3$σ$ threshold to determine whether the QPU output is statistically distinguishable from random sampling. This analysis identifies a regime of coherent performance up to 93 qubits (12,834 two-qubit gates), beyond which, at 95 qubits, the outputs become statistically indistinguishable from random sampling. These results demonstrate how exascale classical simulation can be used to validate quantum processors, and provide a quantitative boundary between noise-tolerant and random regimes in quantum processors.
one_liner: Exascale simulation benchmarks a 98-qubit trapped-ion QPU and maps where its outputs remain meaningfully non-random.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical validation workflow for large QPUs by combining classical simulation with statistical tests near the edge of tractability.
detailed_summary: The study benchmarks Quantinuum's 98-qubit Helios-1 trapped-ion processor using LR-QAOA circuits and large-scale noiseless simulation on the JUPITER exascale system. The simulations reach 48 qubits with 3,384 two-qubit gates, providing a classical reference against which experimental samples can be compared. Up to 48 qubits, the device output remains within a noise-tolerant regime where it is not clearly distinguishable from noiseless simulation, while experimental-only analysis shows coherent performance up to 93 qubits and 12,834 two-qubit gates. At 95 qubits, the output becomes statistically indistinguishable from random sampling under the mean-of-means 3-sigma test.
main_result: The work establishes a quantitative boundary between coherent and effectively random operating regimes for a 98-qubit trapped-ion processor.
application_angle: This directly supports hardware qualification, performance validation, and procurement decisions for near-term quantum computing systems.

title: Geometric-Configuration Modulation: A Novel Free-Space Optical Communication Paradigm for $D/r_{0}\sim 5$ Turbulence Resistance
abstract: We propose Geometric-Configuration Modulation (GM), a novel AO-free FSO paradigm utilizing multi-source geometric configuration encoding and active correlative decoding. GM demonstrates exceptional resistance to strong atmospheric turbulence ($D/r_{0}\sim 5$) over a 1.2 m link in preliminary experiments.
one_liner: A multi-source geometric-configuration modulation scheme resists strong turbulence in free-space optical links without adaptive optics.
why_it_matters: It proposes a simpler turbulence-robust FSO architecture that could reduce complexity and cost for practical optical and quantum links.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces Geometric-Configuration Modulation, an AO-free free-space optical communication approach based on encoding information in the geometric arrangement of multiple sources. Decoding is performed through active correlation, rather than relying on conventional adaptive wavefront correction. In preliminary experiments over a 1.2 m link, the method shows strong resistance to atmospheric turbulence at about D/r0 ~ 5. The concept targets operation in conditions where standard beam-quality preservation becomes difficult.
main_result: Preliminary experiments show that geometric-configuration modulation maintains communication robustness under strong turbulence without adaptive optics.
application_angle: The approach could simplify deployment of resilient terrestrial or short-range airborne optical links, including turbulence-limited quantum communication channels.

title: Geometric-Phase (Pancharatnam-Berry) Correction for Time-Bin Photonic Qudits: A Calibration and Feed-Forward Algorithm
abstract: We develop a geometric-phase framework for time-bin photonic qudits and propose a practical calibration and feed-forward algorithm for separating and compensating geometric (Pancharatnam-Berry), dynamical, and technical phase contributions. Working directly in the time-bin basis, we use a parallel-transport gauge so that geometric phases appear as experimentally identifiable interferometric offsets, while all phase contributions enter a bin-resolved diagonal transformation. We model state preparation by cascaded unbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometers and give closed-form amplitudes for arbitrary splitting ratios and phases, noting that single-port monitoring requires post-selection and renormalization. We then give an interferometric tomography recipe based on adjacent-bin scans, with a Fourier-basis cross-check, and a multi-mode numerical case study that separates total, dynamical, and geometric phases and demonstrates feed-forward compensation. The protocol uses standard components, including tunable UMZIs, phase shifters or EOMs, and single-photon detectors, together with routine phase sweeps. It is intended for small to moderate dimensions, approximately d up to 10, and provides a scalable route toward phase-stable high-dimensional temporal encoding for quantum communication and photonic processing.
one_liner: A calibration and feed-forward method isolates and corrects geometric phase errors in time-bin photonic qudits.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical stability bottleneck for high-dimensional photonic encodings used in quantum communication and photonic information processing.
detailed_summary: The authors formulate a geometric-phase description for time-bin photonic qudits directly in the time-bin basis using a parallel-transport gauge. They derive a bin-resolved diagonal phase model that separates geometric, dynamical, and technical contributions, and analyze state preparation with cascaded unbalanced Mach-Zehnder interferometers for arbitrary splitting ratios and phases. The paper also provides an adjacent-bin interferometric tomography protocol with a Fourier-basis cross-check, plus a numerical multi-mode case study showing phase separation and compensation. The proposed workflow uses standard tunable UMZIs, phase shifters or EOMs, and single-photon detectors, targeting dimensions up to roughly d <= 10.
platform_or_system: time-bin photonic qudits in cascaded UMZIs
main_result: The work demonstrates a practical algorithmic route to identify and feed-forward-correct geometric phase offsets in high-dimensional time-bin photonic states.
application_angle: This improves phase stability for deployable high-dimensional photonic links and processors, enabling more reliable temporal-mode quantum communication.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization by Quantum-Annealing-Inspired Algorithms
abstract: Combinatorial optimization is widely regarded as a primary application for near-term quantum processors, although a definitive demonstration of the practical quantum advantage remains elusive. Recent studies have reported that both gate-based quantum circuits and quantum annealers can outperform state-of-the-art classical heuristics on multi-objective optimization (MO-MaxCut) problems. However, these studies did not fully account for the substantial pre- and post-processing overheads intrinsic to quantum solvers, leading to incomplete comparisons between quantum and classical approaches. In this work, we re-examine the same benchmark suite using GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms (QAIAs), which, analogously to quantum processors, generate probabilistic samples and thus serve as formidable classical contenders. Our results show that QAIAs can sample candidate solutions approximately two orders of magnitude faster than previously studied quantum processors. In terms of end-to-end runtime, QAIAs also surpass industry-leading classical solvers, thereby establishing themselves as the superior performers among the quantum and classical solvers evaluated thus far for the MO-MaxCut instances.
one_liner: GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms outperform prior quantum and classical solvers on MO-MaxCut runtime.
why_it_matters: It sharpens the hardware benchmark for optimization by showing how strong classical sampling baselines can erase apparent near-term quantum advantage claims.
detailed_summary: The paper revisits multi-objective MaxCut benchmarks previously used to motivate quantum advantage claims for gate-based and annealing platforms. Instead of idealized comparisons, it includes end-to-end overheads and evaluates GPU-based quantum-annealing-inspired algorithms that, like quantum solvers, produce probabilistic samples. These QAIAs generate candidate solutions about two orders of magnitude faster than the quantum processors considered in prior work. On full runtime, they also beat leading classical industrial solvers on the tested instances.
main_result: Quantum-annealing-inspired GPU algorithms deliver the best end-to-end performance among the tested quantum and classical MO-MaxCut solvers.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to quantum hardware evaluation because it raises the classical baseline that practical optimization QPUs must surpass to show real value.

title: Fundamental Physics, Existential Risks and Human Futures
abstract: Over the past 25 years, I have been involved in some intriguing developments in the foundations of physics, exploring the quantum reality problem, the relationship between quantum theory and gravity and the interplay between consciousness and physical laws. These investigations make it plausible that we will find physics beyond quantum theory, potentially including both new evolution laws and new types of measurement. There is also a significant chance they could have potentially transformative impact on information processing and on the development of and our future with AI.
one_liner: A perspective piece argues that physics beyond standard quantum theory could reshape measurement, information processing, and AI.
why_it_matters: It frames nonstandard quantum dynamics and measurement as possible future levers for radically new sensing and information technologies.
detailed_summary: This article is a forward-looking foundations essay rather than an experimental report. It links the quantum reality problem, quantum-gravity questions, and possible new measurement laws, arguing that post-quantum physics may alter what can be inferred and processed physically. The application relevance is indirect: if new measurement dynamics exist, they could open qualitatively new sensing and information-processing regimes.
main_result: The paper synthesizes arguments that physics beyond standard quantum theory is plausible and could materially affect measurement and computation.
application_angle: Its practical relevance is in motivating future sensing and information architectures that exploit nonstandard quantum measurement or dynamics if such physics is verified.

title: Random Number Generators in Advanced Optical Experiments: A Comparative Analysis of Semiclassical, Quantum, and Hybrid Architectures
abstract: Random numbers sequences (RNSs) play a vital role in various scientific and engineering applications. They are critical to the integrity of classical and quantum cryptography, the accuracy of mathematical modeling and Monte Carlo simulations, and the core mechanics of applications in fields as diverse as gambling and statistical sampling. While the primary criteria for RNSs sources are their quality and generation rate, their integration into experimental designs is equally significant for many fundamental physical tests and applications. This work presents a comparative analysis of optical random number generation architectures, which can be seamlessly included into various advanced classical and quantum optical experimental schemes. In particular, we evaluate the trade-off between the high generation rate of an attenuated laser (a quasi-single-photon source) and the superior statistical quality of a heralded single-photon source operating at a much lower frequency. To overcome the limitations of each individual source, we propose and examine a novel hybrid architecture that utilizes their mixed radiation, enabling the generation of highquality RNSs at an enhanced rate. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the raw sequences generated by such a source can not only exhibit but, in some cases, even surpass the degree of randomness achieved by sequences processed through powerful randomness extractors.
one_liner: This work compares optical RNGs based on attenuated lasers, heralded single photons, and a hybrid source for speed-versus-randomness tradeoffs.
why_it_matters: High-rate, high-quality optical randomness is core infrastructure for quantum communication, cryptography, and advanced photonic experiments.
detailed_summary: The authors benchmark semiclassical, quantum, and mixed optical random-number-generation architectures with an emphasis on deployability inside real optical experiments. They contrast the high throughput of an attenuated laser source with the stronger intrinsic statistics of a heralded single-photon source, then propose a hybrid architecture that combines both radiations. Their analysis indicates that the hybrid source can deliver better rate-quality balance, and that raw output can in some cases match or exceed extractor-processed randomness metrics.
main_result: A hybrid optical RNG mixing attenuated-laser and heralded-photon radiation is shown to improve the tradeoff between generation rate and statistical randomness quality.
application_angle: The design is directly relevant to embedded QRNG modules for QKD, photonic testbeds, and other experiments that need fast trustworthy randomness without heavy post-processing.

title: Addressable Rydberg excitation in arrays of single neutral atoms with a strongly focused flat-top beam
abstract: We present a method for generating a laser beam with flat intensity and phase profiles in the focal region where the beam interacts with neutral $^{87}$Rb atoms in an array of optical dipole traps. We synthesize the beam as a superposition of Hermite--Gaussian or Laguerre--Gaussian modes. Then we give analytical expressions for the coefficients of such a superposition, an analysis of beam propagation along the $z$ axis in the vicinity of the waist, and several other related theoretical issues. Rydberg two-qubit dynamics driven by this flat-top profile are analyzed through numerical solutions of the Lindblad master equation using our in-house Julia package. Beam preparation is demonstrated on a neutral-atom experimental platform. Measurements reveal a difference in the visibility of Rabi oscillations for addressed atoms compared with neighboring ones, confirming the effective spatial selectivity provided by the flat-top beam profile.
one_liner: A flat-top tightly focused beam enables spatially selective Rydberg excitation in neutral-atom arrays with improved addressing contrast.
why_it_matters: Precise local control is a central bottleneck for scalable neutral-atom quantum processors and high-fidelity two-qubit gates.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a beam-shaping method that synthesizes a flat intensity and phase profile near focus using Hermite-Gaussian or Laguerre-Gaussian mode superpositions. The authors derive analytical coefficients, study near-waist propagation, and model addressed Rydberg two-qubit dynamics with a Lindblad-master-equation solver. On a trapped-atom platform with single 87Rb atoms, they experimentally prepare the beam and observe stronger Rabi-oscillation visibility on the addressed site than on neighboring sites, confirming spatial selectivity.
main_result: The work demonstrates experimentally viable flat-top beam addressing that selectively drives target atoms in a neutral-atom array while suppressing excitation of neighbors.
application_angle: This directly supports denser neutral-atom layouts, lower crosstalk, and more uniform gate operations in scalable quantum computing hardware.

title: Development of a compact cryogenic Penning trap with permanent magnets: An intermediate step toward the Shanghai Penning Trap
abstract: Penning traps, renowned for their unparalleled precision in determining fundamental properties such as mass and magnetic moments, are cornerstone instruments in modern physics. Their applications span from nuclear structure studies to stringent tests of quantum electrodynamics and CPT invariance. Although Penning traps have been demonstrated for fundamental studies, often employing superconducting magnets, their high cost and operational complexity remain challenges. In this work, we report the development of a compact cryogenic Penning trap that utilizes a permanent magnet to provide a confining magnetic field, offering a more economical and flexible alternative. We have successfully demonstrated all core functionalities of this system, including ion generation, transport, confinement, manipulation, and signal detection. This compact trap not only serves as a vital technical testbed for the development of the Shanghai Penning Trap, but also establishes a cryogenic Penning-trap experiment platform for ion trapping and cooling applications as well as envisaged spectroscopic studies applications.
one_liner: A compact cryogenic Penning trap using permanent magnets reproduces key ion trapping functions without superconducting-magnet complexity.
why_it_matters: Lower-cost cryogenic Penning traps could broaden precision sensing, spectroscopy, and ion-control hardware beyond specialized large facilities.
detailed_summary: The authors build a cryogenic Penning-trap platform that replaces the usual superconducting magnet with a permanent-magnet field source. They demonstrate the core experimental stack: ion generation, transport, confinement, manipulation, and signal detection in a compact apparatus. The system serves both as an intermediate engineering platform for the Shanghai Penning Trap and as a standalone base for ion cooling and precision spectroscopic studies.
main_result: The team demonstrates a compact cryogenic Penning trap with permanent magnets that performs the essential functions required for trapped-ion precision experiments.
application_angle: The approach reduces cost and operational burden for precision sensing and trapped-ion hardware, making deployable spectroscopy and metrology systems more feasible.

title: Parameterized Quantum Circuits as Feature Maps: Representation Quality and Readout Effects in Multispectral Land-Cover Classification
abstract: We investigate variational quantum classifiers (VQCs) for land-cover classification from multispectral satellite imagery, adopting a feature-map perspective in which the quantum circuit defines a nonlinear data embedding while the readout determines how this representation is exploited. Using the EuroSAT-MS dataset, we perform a systematic one-vs-one evaluation across all class pairs under a controlled experimental protocol, comparing classical baselines (logistic regression, SVMs, neural networks) with VQCs employing both linear readout and quantum-kernel SVM strategies. Our results show that, while VQCs with linear readout do not outperform strong classical baselines such as RBF-SVM, the same trained quantum feature map can significantly improve performance when reused within a kernel-based decision framework. A qubit-count sweep further reveals saturation effects consistent with the mismatch between exponential Hilbert space dimension and linear parameter scaling. Overall, our findings highlight that the effectiveness of quantum models depends critically on the interplay between representation and readout, and that meaningful gains may arise from combining learned quantum feature maps with classical decision mechanisms rather than seeking direct replacement of classical models.
one_liner: Quantum feature maps are tested on multispectral land-cover classification, showing that readout choice strongly controls achievable performance.
why_it_matters: It clarifies where learned quantum embeddings may be useful in practice: as reusable representations paired with strong classical decision layers.
detailed_summary: Using EuroSAT-MS one-vs-one land-cover tasks, the study compares classical baselines with variational quantum classifiers interpreted as learned feature maps. It finds that linear readout on top of the quantum embedding does not beat strong classical models such as RBF-SVM, but that the same trained feature map can perform better when reused in a quantum-kernel SVM pipeline. A qubit-count sweep also shows saturation behavior consistent with limited parameter scaling relative to Hilbert-space growth.
platform_or_system: Parameterized quantum circuits for quantum ML
main_result: The main result is that representation quality from PQC feature maps can be useful, but practical gains depend critically on using an appropriate, often kernel-based, readout.
application_angle: For applied quantum workflows, the paper supports hybrid deployment strategies where quantum embeddings augment classical inference rather than replace it end to end.

title: Fast, powerful, low-noise optical pumping of an atomic vapor with semiconductor optical amplifiers
abstract: We use a $^{87}\text{Rb}$ atomic vapor, suitable for an optically-pumped magnetometer (OPM) in Earth-field conditions, to study the noise properties of three strategies for generating pulsed optical pumping. We compare a frequency-modulated (FM) laser, amplitude modulation (AM) via an acousto-optic modulator (AOM), and amplitude modulation via a semiconductor optical amplifier (SOA). Pumping the ensemble to operate as a Bell-Bloom OPM, and with an equal degree of spin polarization, the three methods give nearly identical sensitivity, showing that the SOA, despite being an active device, can introduce negligible additional noise. Pumping the ensemble to operate as a free-induction-decay OPM, we observe longer unpumped coherence times with the SOA-AM method than with the FM method. Finally, using the higher power available from the SOA, we demonstrate an environment-limited sensitivity of $80\text{fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ at $600\text{Hz}$ and 200fT$200\text{fT}/\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ at $4\text{kHz}$, one to two orders of magnitude beyond what was achievable with the other pumping methods.
one_liner: Semiconductor optical amplifiers provide fast, low-noise pulsed optical pumping for rubidium vapor magnetometers with much higher usable power.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical path to more sensitive optically pumped magnetometers using compact photonic hardware that does not add significant noise.
detailed_summary: The paper compares three pulsed optical-pumping strategies in a 87Rb vapor suitable for Earth-field OPM operation: laser frequency modulation, AOM-based amplitude modulation, and SOA-based amplitude modulation. At equal spin polarization, all three methods give similar sensitivity in Bell-Bloom mode, indicating that the SOA adds negligible excess noise despite being an active component. In free-induction-decay mode, SOA pumping yields longer coherence than FM pumping, and its higher available power enables environment-limited sensitivities of 80 fT/sqrt(Hz) at 600 Hz and 200 fT/sqrt(Hz) at 4 kHz, far beyond the other methods.
main_result: SOA-based amplitude modulation is shown to deliver low-noise, high-power optical pumping and substantially improved magnetometer sensitivity over FM and AOM alternatives.
application_angle: The result is directly useful for compact high-performance OPMs in biomagnetism, navigation, geophysics, and field-deployable quantum sensing.

title: Efficient mapping of multi-constraint satisfaction problems to Rydberg platforms
abstract: We present a hardware-native gadget framework for solving constraint satisfaction problems on Rydberg quantum computing architectures. Our approach introduces a compact $xor_1$ gadget that enforces exactly-one constraints, ubiquitous in combinatorial optimization, directly through geometric embedding and blockade interactions. A key advantage of the $xor_1$ gadget is its fixed, problem-size-independent detuning requirements: enforcing constraints through blockade interactions eliminates the need for large penalty terms, thereby substantially reducing the detuning range compared to Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulations and improving experimental feasibility. By tailoring the construction to the geometric connectivity of Rydberg atom arrays, the framework bypasses the all-to-all physical couplings often assumed in logical encodings. This enables embeddings compatible with planar layouts and avoids highly connected arrangements. We develop scalable implementations that reduce atom count and connectivity overhead while avoiding extensive classical preprocessing, making them compatible with near-term neutral-atom hardware. As illustrations, we apply our framework to the gate-assignment and $N$-queens problems, highlighting its practicality, resource efficiency, and hardware compatibility. In these examples, we observe reductions in detuning range of up to $99\%$ and savings in atom count and connectivity overhead of up to $54\%$ compared to the QUBO method. These results establish a route toward implementing large-scale combinatorial optimization on Rydberg platforms beyond the limits of existing encodings.
one_liner: A hardware-native Rydberg encoding cuts detuning, atom count, and connectivity overhead for exactly-one constrained optimization.
why_it_matters: It makes larger combinatorial optimization instances more realistic on near-term neutral-atom hardware by replacing heavy QUBO penalties with native blockade gadgets.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a compact hardware-native `xor_1` gadget that enforces exactly-one constraints directly through Rydberg blockade geometry rather than through large logical penalty terms. This keeps the required detuning scale fixed with problem size and allows planar embeddings better matched to neutral-atom arrays. The authors instantiate the framework on gate-assignment and N-queens problems, reporting up to 99% detuning-range reduction and up to 54% savings in atom count and connectivity overhead relative to QUBO mappings.
main_result: The work demonstrates scalable native embeddings of constrained optimization problems on Rydberg platforms with sharply reduced detuning and hardware overhead versus QUBO encodings.
application_angle: This improves the practicality of deploying neutral-atom processors for real optimization workloads where control range and connectivity are major experimental bottlenecks.

title: Optical squeezing mediated by levitated oscillators at their quantum ground state
abstract: We demonstrate optical squeezing below the shot-noise level generated through the interaction of an optical cavity field with two center-of-mass modes of a levitated nanoparticle, simultaneously cooled to occupation numbers well below unity. By analyzing the quadrature fluctuations of the cavity output through heterodyne detection, we resolve the full spectral covariance matrix of the optical field and map regions of sub-shot-noise squeezing as a function of detection phase and frequency. Operating in the resolved sideband and strong coupling regime where mechanical modes hybridize with the optical mode, we observe consistent squeezing in the band 70-95 kHz with a lowest variance of 0.98 (2$\%$ below vacuum fluctuations). We thus demonstrate optical squeezing mediated by multiple mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground state, bridging mechanical quantum control with non-classical light and establishing levitated optomechanics as a platform for multimode quantum interactions.
one_liner: A levitated nanoparticle in a cavity generates measurable optical squeezing while two motional modes are cooled below one phonon.
why_it_matters: It links ground-state levitated optomechanics with nonclassical light generation, strengthening the case for levitated systems in quantum sensing and multimode photonics.
detailed_summary: Using a cavity-coupled levitated nanoparticle, the authors cool two center-of-mass modes to sub-unity occupation and analyze the output field through heterodyne-resolved quadrature covariance measurements. In the resolved-sideband, strong-coupling regime, optical and mechanical modes hybridize and produce sub-shot-noise output fluctuations. They observe squeezing in the 70-95 kHz band, with a minimum variance of 0.98, corresponding to 2% below vacuum noise.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates optical squeezing below shot noise mediated by two ground-state-cooled mechanical modes of a levitated nanoparticle.
application_angle: Nonclassical light sourced from levitated oscillators could be useful for force sensing, precision metrology, and multimode quantum transduction experiments.

title: Chip-to-chip entanglement distribution over 80-km multicore fiber link
abstract: Long-range quantum entanglement is essential for building large-scale quantum networks and unconditionally secure cryptographic systems based on quantum key distribution (QKD). While photonic integrated circuits offer a highly scalable platform, the fragility of phase coherence between spatial modes has prevented the distribution of path-encoded entanglement over long distances. Here, we report chip-to-chip distribution of path-encoded entangled states over 80 km between fully integrated silicon photonic transmitter and receiver chips. Telecom-band entangled photon pairs are generated via spontaneous four-wave mixing in on-chip spiral waveguides and distributed between chips over a dual-core, actively stabilized fiber link. Upon distribution, we measure a Bell state fidelity of $85.7 \pm 0.2 \%$. Implementing the BBM92 protocol with the same source, we obtain a secure key rate of 2.03 bit/s in the infinite-key regime. These results establish silicon photonic chips as a viable platform for long-distance path-encoded entanglement-based quantum key distribution, paving the way toward scalable, device-independent quantum networks.
one_liner: Integrated silicon photonic chips distribute path-entangled photons over 80 km of multicore fiber and support secure BBM92 key exchange.
why_it_matters: It shows a scalable chip-based route to long-distance entanglement distribution and entanglement-based QKD without abandoning path encoding on photonic integrated circuits.
detailed_summary: The system uses silicon photonic transmitter and receiver chips, with telecom entangled photon pairs generated on chip by spontaneous four-wave mixing in spiral waveguides. Path-encoded entanglement is sent chip-to-chip over an 80 km dual-core fiber link with active phase stabilization preserving spatial-mode coherence. After transmission, the experiment measures a Bell-state fidelity of 85.7 +/- 0.2% and demonstrates BBM92 operation with a 2.03 bit/s secure key rate in the infinite-key regime.
platform_or_system: Silicon photonic chips over multicore fiber
main_result: The work achieves 80 km chip-to-chip distribution of path-encoded entanglement with 85.7% fidelity and secure entanglement-based key generation.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to deployable quantum networks and integrated QKD systems that need manufacturable photonic hardware and long-haul fiber compatibility.

title: Fault-Tolerant Resource Comparison of Qudit and Qubit Encodings for Diagonal Quadratic Operators
abstract: Finite local Hilbert-space truncations arise naturally in quantum simulations of lattice field theories and motivate qudit encodings, but their fault-tolerant advantage over qubit encodings remains unclear. We compare the non-Clifford cost of implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions, exemplified by $U=e^{-itφ_x^2}$ in a uniform field-amplitude discretization of a real scalar field, using either one logical $d$-level qudit or $n_b=\lceil \log_2 d\rceil$ logical qubits. We analyze two standard settings: product-formula simulation and LCU/block encoding, taking the resource metric to be the number of non-Clifford gates after synthesis into a discrete logical gate set. Because tight synthesis bounds for general single-qudit rotations are not known, we express the qudit constructions in terms of embedded two-level $SU(2)$ rotations and derive explicit finite-$d$ break-even conditions for their synthesis cost; these serve as compiler targets for when qudit encodings can outperform the qubit baseline. Within the constructive models studied here, product-formula implementations would require an exponentially stronger per-primitive synthesis advantage for qudits to win asymptotically, while in the LCU setting the qubit encoding is asymptotically cheaper in $d$. Nevertheless, the finite-$d$ threshold analysis identifies low dimensional regions in which qudits can yield meaningful constant-factor savings, particularly for LCU-based implementations. As a secondary analysis of the LCU construction, we use an idealized negligible-overhead qubit-qudit code-switching model to give an absolute $T$-count comparison, and reinterpret the savings as an allowable per-switch overhead budget.
one_liner: A fault-tolerant resource study identifies when qudit encodings can beat qubits for diagonal quadratic operators and when they cannot.
why_it_matters: It gives concrete compiler-level break-even targets for qudit hardware, clarifying where higher-dimensional logical encodings may actually deliver savings.
detailed_summary: The authors compare implementing quadratic diagonal evolutions such as exp(-it phi_x^2) using either one logical d-level qudit or n_b logical qubits under product-formula and LCU/block-encoding approaches. They decompose qudit primitives into embedded two-level SU(2) rotations and derive finite-dimensional synthesis break-even conditions relative to qubit baselines. Their analysis finds qubits asymptotically cheaper in the studied constructions, but identifies low-dimensional finite-d regimes where qudits can provide meaningful constant-factor savings, especially for LCU.
main_result: The paper establishes explicit finite-d thresholds for when qudit implementations of diagonal quadratic operators can outperform qubit encodings in non-Clifford cost.
application_angle: These thresholds can guide compiler and architecture decisions for emerging qudit hardware in quantum simulation and fault-tolerant processing.

title: HyPulse: A Pulse Synthesis Framework for Hybrid Qubit-Oscillator Gates on Trapped-Ion Platform
abstract: As hybrid qubit-oscillator algorithm development and trapped-ion hardware demonstrations advance in parallel, there is a lack of a compilation layer connecting the two at the pulse level in the vertical software stack. While qubit gate control and pulse synthesis are well-established, the translation of hybrid qubit-oscillator primitives to the pulse level has not been systematically addressed. This gap is further compounded by the inherently continuous parametric nature of such gates. Each distinct parameter value defines a physically unique operation requiring independent pulse optimization, making static pre-compilation strategies inapplicable. To fill this gap, we present HyPulse, a hardware-aware pulse synthesis and generation framework, which contributes a two-phase architecture decoupling pulse discovery from circuit assembly. An offline optimization engine populates a content-addressed cache of high-fidelity primitives: If a pulse for a given gate, parameter, and device specification already exists in the library, it is retrieved instantly; otherwise the optimizer synthesizes, hashes, and caches it automatically. An online assembler then constructs circuit-specific pulse programs ready to drive trapped-ion hardware control systems via DAX/ARTIQ (Duke) and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT (Sandia), trapped-ion pulse execution backends.
one_liner: HyPulse provides a hardware-aware compilation layer that synthesizes and caches hybrid qubit-oscillator pulses for trapped-ion systems.
why_it_matters: It fills a missing software-stack layer for analog-rich trapped-ion workflows, making parameterized hybrid gates more reusable and executable on real control backends.
detailed_summary: HyPulse targets the pulse-level realization of hybrid qubit-oscillator primitives whose continuous parameters make static compilation impractical. The framework separates offline pulse discovery from online circuit assembly: optimized high-fidelity primitives are cached by gate, parameter, and device specification, then reused when available. It interfaces with DAX/ARTIQ and JaqalPaw/QSCOUT backends, connecting algorithmic hybrid gate descriptions to executable trapped-ion pulse programs.
main_result: The framework demonstrates a two-phase pulse synthesis and caching architecture for compiling parameterized hybrid trapped-ion gates into hardware-ready control programs.
application_angle: This can accelerate experimental deployment of bosonic and hybrid ion algorithms by reducing per-instance pulse-optimization overhead and improving software-hardware integration.

title: Gouy phase engineering of self-splitting quantum correlations
abstract: In this work, we demonstrate the effect of self-splitting spatial quantum correlations induced by Gouy phase engineering. In the process of spontaneous parametric down conversion the pump beam is structured with a mode superposition that produces a dynamical splitting and recombination of the light beam. This structure is transferred to the quantum correlations between signal and idler photons. As a result the joint two-photon probability distribution propagates like a self-splitting and recombining light beam, implementing a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometer. We observe heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON state interference. These results open new avenues for applications in quantum metrology.
