one_liner: Structured-pump SPDC with Gouy-phase engineering creates self-splitting spatial quantum correlations and interferometric single- and two-photon effects.
why_it_matters: It introduces a new way to encode and manipulate quantum spatial correlations for metrology without conventional beam-splitter interferometer layouts.
detailed_summary: The authors shape the pump in spontaneous parametric down-conversion with a mode superposition whose Gouy-phase evolution causes dynamical beam splitting and recombination. This propagation structure transfers to the signal-idler joint probability distribution, effectively producing a Mach-Zehnder-like interferometric evolution in the two-photon correlations. They observe both heralded single-photon interference and two-photon NOON-state interference arising from this self-splitting correlation dynamics.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates Gouy-phase-controlled self-splitting quantum correlations that realize interferometric behavior at the single- and two-photon level.
application_angle: The approach could support compact quantum metrology and imaging schemes that exploit engineered spatial correlations as an interferometric resource.

title: Protein folding on a 64 qubit trapped-ion hardware via counterdiabatic quantum optimization
abstract: We report the largest trapped-ion hardware demonstration of lattice protein-folding optimization to date, using bias-field digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization (BF-DCQO) on a fully connected 64-qubit Barium development system similar to the forthcoming IonQ Tempo line. Six peptide sequences with 14-16 amino-acid residues are encoded using a coarse-grained tetrahedral lattice model, yielding higher-order spin-glass Hamiltonians with long-range interactions involving up to five-body terms and mapped to 46-61 qubits. The resulting instances are demanding for near-term quantum hardware because low-energy configurations must satisfy backbone-geometry constraints while optimizing dense residue-contact interactions. BF-DCQO uses a non-variational bias-feedback mechanism, where low-energy samples from each round define longitudinal fields that guide subsequent quantum evolutions. Across the studied instances, BF-DCQO shifts raw sampled energy distributions toward lower energies than uniform random sampling, with the strongest improvements appearing in residue-contact variables. To preserve this signal, we introduce a consensus-based post-processing pipeline that combines quantum-learned contact information with feasible backbone geometries. The resulting hybrid workflow reaches the classical reference energy in multiple instances and improves over the corresponding random-seeded pipeline. These results show that BF-DCQO can generate structured samples for dense protein-folding Hamiltonians at previously unexplored trapped-ion scales.
one_liner: A 64-qubit trapped-ion processor runs counterdiabatic optimization for lattice protein-folding instances up to 61 qubits.
why_it_matters: It pushes quantum optimization into larger, constraint-heavy molecular instances and shows a practical hybrid path for extracting useful structure from noisy samples.
detailed_summary: The work implements bias-field digitized counterdiabatic quantum optimization on a fully connected 64-qubit barium trapped-ion system for coarse-grained lattice protein-folding problems. Six 14-16 residue peptide instances are mapped to dense higher-order Hamiltonians with long-range couplings and up to five-body terms, requiring 46-61 qubits. A non-variational feedback loop uses low-energy samples to update longitudinal bias fields and steer later evolutions toward improved energy distributions. A consensus post-processing stage combines quantum-learned residue-contact information with feasible backbone geometries, reaching classical reference energies on multiple instances.
main_result: The hybrid BF-DCQO workflow shifted sampled energies downward and matched classical reference energies for multiple protein-folding instances on up to 61 encoded qubits.
application_angle: This suggests a near-term route to using trapped-ion optimizers as structured samplers for hard molecular and combinatorial design problems.

title: Convex combinations of bosonic pure-loss channels
abstract: The pure-loss channel is a fundamental model for describing noise in bosonic quantum platforms. It is characterised by a single parameter, the transmissivity, which quantifies the fraction of the input energy that reaches the output of the channel. In realistic scenarios, however, such as free-space quantum communication, the transmissivity is not fixed but fluctuates from one channel use to another. In this setting, the overall channel is effectively described as a convex combination of pure-loss channels, known as a fading channel. Despite its practical relevance, the quantum Shannon theory of the fading channel has remained largely unexplored. Here, we address this gap, specifically investigating degradability, anti-degradability, entanglement breakingness, and capacities of the fading channel. Of particular relevance to practical quantum-internet applications, we prove that entanglement distribution and quantum key distribution can always be achieved at a strictly positive rate over any fading channel, no matter how noisy it is or how strongly the transmissivity fluctuates, provided the channel is not completely noisy. Moreover, we prove that thermal states, which are optimal for a broad class of static bosonic Gaussian channels, fail to achieve the entanglement-assisted classical capacity of fading channels: non-Gaussian Fock-diagonal states strictly outperform all Gaussian encodings. Most strikingly, we identify regimes where the coherent information of thermal inputs vanishes, while optimized non-Gaussian states achieve strictly positive values, thereby activating the channel for quantum communication. For a paradigmatic binary fading model we establish this result analytically, deriving the exact capacity-achieving state in closed form. For general fading distributions, we design an iterative variational algorithm to optimize the coherent and mutual information.
one_liner: This paper establishes core capacity properties of bosonic fading channels and shows non-Gaussian inputs can unlock quantum communication.
why_it_matters: Free-space links fluctuate in transmissivity, so proving positive QKD and entanglement-distribution rates under fading is directly relevant to deployable quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze convex mixtures of bosonic pure-loss channels, focusing on degradability, anti-degradability, entanglement breaking, and communication capacities. They prove that any fading channel that is not completely noisy still supports strictly positive entanglement-distribution and QKD rates, regardless of fluctuation strength. They also show Gaussian thermal encodings are not generally optimal for entanglement-assisted classical capacity, with Fock-diagonal non-Gaussian states performing strictly better. For a binary fading model they derive the exact optimal state analytically, and for general fading they provide an iterative variational optimizer for coherent and mutual information.
main_result: The paper proves positive-rate quantum networking tasks remain possible over any nontrivial bosonic fading channel and identifies regimes where non-Gaussian inputs activate quantum capacity.
application_angle: It gives a stronger theory foundation for free-space and fluctuating optical links, indicating that tailored non-Gaussian encodings can materially improve real quantum communication systems.

title: Cavity-mediated coherence protection and one-axis twisting for spins in solids
abstract: Long-range interactions between emitters give rise to collective phenomena, including superradiance, spin squeezing, and coherence protection, that are important to both fundamental physics and quantum technologies. Despite progress in cold atoms, coherent cavity-mediated all-to-all interactions have not yet been realized in a solid-state ensemble. Here we demonstrate such interactions in a $^{171}$Yb$^{3+}$:CaWO$_4$ crystal coupled to a microwave resonator, observing superradiant emission on resonance and unitary one-axis twisting dynamics in the dispersive regime. The same interaction also opens a many-body energy gap that suppresses inhomogeneous dephasing, extending the ensemble Ramsey coherence time from tens of microseconds to milliseconds without decoupling pulses. These results establish a solid-state platform for collective many-body physics with direct implications for quantum technologies. Specifically, the observed one-axis twisting dynamics opens a path towards spin squeezing for entanglement-enhanced quantum metrology, and the extended coherence due to gap-protection is relevant for both microwave photon storage and precision measurement.
one_liner: A rare-earth solid-state spin ensemble shows cavity-mediated all-to-all interactions, coherence protection, and one-axis twisting.
why_it_matters: It brings collective many-body control and metrologically useful spin squeezing mechanisms into a solid-state hardware platform with long coherence.
detailed_summary: Using a 171Yb3+:CaWO4 crystal coupled to a microwave resonator, the authors realize coherent cavity-mediated long-range interactions in a solid-state ensemble. On resonance they observe superradiant emission, while in the dispersive regime they measure unitary one-axis twisting dynamics. The same collective interaction opens a many-body gap that suppresses inhomogeneous dephasing, extending Ramsey coherence from tens of microseconds to milliseconds without dynamical decoupling pulses. The experiment establishes a cavity-controlled solid-state platform for collective spin physics and entanglement generation.
platform_or_system: 171Yb3+:CaWO4 spin ensemble in a microwave resonator
main_result: The experiment demonstrates superradiance, dispersive one-axis twisting, and millisecond-scale gap-protected Ramsey coherence in a solid-state rare-earth ensemble.
application_angle: The result is directly relevant to entanglement-enhanced sensing, long-lived microwave quantum memories, and scalable solid-state spin hardware.

title: Digital Simulation of Non-Hermitian Knotted Bands on Quantum Hardware
abstract: Knots and links represent a fundamental motif of non-local connectivity that permeates the physical sciences from string theory to protein folds. While spectral braiding has been explored in two-band non-Hermitian models across various platforms, its direct simulation and characterization on programmable quantum hardware, particularly beyond two strands, remains a formidable challenge due to the limitations of variational optimization in these systems. Here, we introduce a family of non-Hermitian multi-band twister models and implement a non-variational protocol to characterize their complex braided band structures on a programmable superconducting quantum processor. By mapping the winding of eigenstates to the spectral topology, we devise an efficient measurement strategy that extracts braid information, including braid words and knot invariants like the Alexander and Jones polynomials, without requiring full spectral tomography or repeated optimization. We experimentally demonstrate the reconstruction of complicated knots and links such as the Hopf chain and Solomon's knot. Our approach provides a general framework for investigating exotic non-Hermitian topology on near-term quantum devices, opening a route to simulate more sophisticated topological structures in knot theory.
one_liner: A superconducting quantum processor digitally reconstructs non-Hermitian braided band topology and associated knot invariants.
why_it_matters: It turns programmable quantum hardware into a practical probe of complex non-Hermitian topological structures without full spectral tomography.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces multi-band non-Hermitian twister models and a non-variational protocol for extracting their braided complex band structure on superconducting quantum hardware. Instead of full spectral reconstruction, the method maps eigenstate winding to spectral topology and measures braid words together with knot invariants such as Alexander and Jones polynomials. The experiment reconstructs nontrivial structures including the Hopf chain and Solomon's knot. This provides an efficient hardware-native route to studying higher-strand non-Hermitian topology.
main_result: The authors experimentally recovered braid structure and knot invariants of multi-band non-Hermitian models on superconducting quantum hardware without full tomography.
application_angle: The protocol offers a scalable way to benchmark and explore exotic topological simulators on near-term quantum processors.

title: Onset of superactivation of quantum capacity
abstract: Superactivation of quantum capacity is the phenomenon whereby two quantum channels, each with zero quantum capacity, can exhibit a strictly positive capacity when used in tandem. In this work, we explore superactivation in the previously unexplored non-asymptotic regime of finitely many channel uses. We give a definition of finite-blocklength superactivation and propose numerical methods that can certify it. Then, focusing on the 50% erasure and positive-partial-transpose channels considered in the original work on superactivation, we show that as few as 17 uses of the joint channel already enable qubit transmission with a fidelity unattainable by any number of uses of either channel alone, demonstrating a strong finite-blocklength form of superactivation and opening the door to experimental demonstration.
one_liner: Finite-blocklength superactivation appears after only 17 joint uses of two individually zero-capacity quantum channels.
why_it_matters: Reducing superactivation from an asymptotic curiosity to a small-use regime makes experimental tests and practical coding studies more realistic.
detailed_summary: The authors define finite-blocklength superactivation and develop numerical certification methods for it. They study the canonical pairing of a 50% erasure channel with a positive-partial-transpose channel, both of which individually have zero quantum capacity. Their analysis shows that 17 uses of the joint channel suffice to transmit a qubit with fidelity impossible for any number of uses of either constituent channel alone. This sharpens superactivation into a concrete non-asymptotic phenomenon with experimental relevance.
platform_or_system: joint use of erasure and PPT quantum channels
main_result: The work certifies a strong finite-blocklength form of superactivation, with positive transmission capability emerging after as few as 17 joint channel uses.
application_angle: It provides actionable targets for laboratory demonstrations of channel-combination advantages and for designing short-block quantum communication protocols.

title: Large quantum dot energy level shifts in anomalous photon-assisted tunneling
abstract: Orbital energy splittings are important quantum dot parameters for the operation of hole spin qubits. They are known to depend on the lateral confinement of the quantum dots. However, when changing top, plunger gate voltages, which are the typical control parameter for qubit applications, such energy splitting changes are typically negligible, both as measured in experiment and as assumed in effective theories. Here, we study the singlet-triplet (ST) splittings, which depend on the orbital splittings, of a double quantum dot (DQD) in a Ge/SiGe heterostructure using photon-assisted tunneling (PAT) and pulsed-gate spectroscopy. We find that the ST splittings have a surprising, strong dependence on the top gate voltages, leading to anomalous PAT measurements. We combine data from both measurements in a model that well describes the linear gate-voltage dependence of the ST splittings. Finally, we show that the ST splittings of the two dots exhibit similar linear gate-voltage dependences when the device is retuned such that their ratio is significantly different.
one_liner: Ge/SiGe double quantum dots show unexpectedly large gate-dependent singlet-triplet shifts seen through anomalous photon-assisted tunneling.
why_it_matters: Orbital and singlet-triplet energies are core qubit parameters, so strong hidden gate dependence affects calibration, control, and device modeling.
detailed_summary: The study measures singlet-triplet splittings in a Ge/SiGe double quantum dot using photon-assisted tunneling and pulsed-gate spectroscopy. Contrary to standard assumptions, the splittings vary strongly and approximately linearly with top-gate voltage, producing anomalous PAT features. A joint model of both measurement modalities captures the voltage dependence and remains consistent after retuning the device to significantly change the splitting ratio between the two dots. The results indicate that top-gate control can reshape orbital structure more strongly than commonly assumed in effective qubit models.
platform_or_system: Ge/SiGe double quantum dot hole-spin device
main_result: The experiment reveals large, nearly linear top-gate-induced shifts of singlet-triplet splittings in both dots, explaining anomalous PAT spectra.
application_angle: This informs qubit tuning strategies and compact models for semiconductor spin hardware where gate-voltage cross-couplings can impact fidelity and reproducibility.

title: Non-Local Magic Resources for Fermionic Gaussian States
abstract: Entanglement and magic are fundamental resources that capture the complexity of quantum many-body systems. Non-local magic isolates the irreducible nonstabilizerness intrinsically tied to entanglement. However, evaluating this quantity generally requires a prohibitive minimization over the full Hilbert space, making it computationally inaccessible beyond a few qubits. Here, we overcome this bottleneck by suggesting a closed-form expression for the non-local stabilizer entropies of fermionic Gaussian states over local Gaussian unitaries, which can be evaluated in polynomial time directly from the eigenvalues of the reduced Majorana covariance matrix. We apply this framework to characterize fermionic non-local magic across diverse physical regimes: we derive an exact Page-like curve for typical random states, reveal logarithmic scaling at the quantum critical point of the XY model, and establish a quasiparticle picture for magic generation during out-of-equilibrium quantum quenches. Crucially, because our result relies solely on two-point correlation functions, it provides a scalable route for the experimental estimation of fermionic non-local magic in large-scale quantum processors via fermionic shadow tomography.
one_liner: Closed-form non-local magic for fermionic Gaussian states makes this many-body resource efficiently computable from two-point correlators.
why_it_matters: It turns a previously intractable resource measure into a scalable observable, opening a practical route to benchmarking nonstabilizerness in fermionic processors.
detailed_summary: The paper derives a closed-form expression for non-local stabilizer entropies of fermionic Gaussian states after optimization over local Gaussian unitaries. The quantity can be computed in polynomial time directly from eigenvalues of the reduced Majorana covariance matrix, avoiding Hilbert-space minimization. Using this framework, the authors obtain an exact Page-like curve for random states, logarithmic critical scaling in the XY model, and a quasiparticle picture for magic growth after quenches. Because the estimator depends only on two-point correlators, it is compatible with scalable experimental extraction via fermionic shadow tomography.
main_result: A polynomial-time, correlation-function-based formula is given for fermionic non-local magic, with exact and dynamical applications across random, critical, and quenched regimes.
application_angle: The method provides a realistic benchmark for many-body quantum hardware where fermionic correlations are measurable but full state reconstruction is impossible.

title: High-fidelity iSWAP gate with Double Transmon Coupler
abstract: Entangling operations are at the heart of all approaches to quantum information processing. Parametric gates, in particular, offer a versatile solution to strongly couple off-resonant superconducting qubits with suppressed parasitic crosstalk to spectator qubits due to frequency-selective activation. In this work, we demonstrate a parametric iSWAP gate between two transmon qubits using the recently developed double transmon coupler (DTC). The DTC supports robust internally-defined cancellation point (``off'' state) for static interactions, while simultaneously mediating a fast parametric coupling between data qubits that can be deployed for high-fidelity two-qubit operations. We use robust phase estimation to calibrate non-commuting error terms in the parametric iSWAP gate, and achieve a 99.827% gate fidelity in 40ns without any numerical optimization. The circuit architecture and calibration techniques developed here are extensible to other gate implementations and qubit modalities, paving the way towards resource-efficient quantum information processing.
one_liner: A double transmon coupler enables a 40 ns parametric iSWAP gate with 99.827% fidelity between superconducting transmons.
why_it_matters: Fast, high-fidelity entangling gates with an intrinsic off-state directly improve scalability and crosstalk control in superconducting processors.
detailed_summary: This work implements a parametric iSWAP gate between two off-resonant transmon qubits mediated by a double transmon coupler. The coupler provides a robust static-interaction cancellation point while still allowing strong frequency-selective parametric activation. Using robust phase estimation to calibrate non-commuting error terms, the experiment reaches 99.827% gate fidelity in 40 ns without numerical pulse optimization. The architecture is presented as extensible to other gate types and qubit modalities.
platform_or_system: superconducting transmons with double transmon coupler
main_result: The authors demonstrate a 40 ns parametric iSWAP gate with 99.827% fidelity and suppressed static coupling using a double transmon coupler.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to building denser superconducting quantum processors with lower spectator crosstalk and lower calibration overhead.

title: Quantum Noise Fraction and the Thermal Frontier in High-Frequency Gravitational Wave Detection
abstract: We introduce a diagnostic -- the quantum noise fraction $β$ -- that determines the maximum sensitivity improvement achievable through quantum enhancement for any gravitational wave detector. Applied to the landscape of proposed high-frequency (kHz-GHz) detectors, this diagnostic reveals that resonant mass detectors operating through tidal coupling are thermally dominated ($β\approx 0$) at all frequencies below ~230 MHz at dilution temperatures, rendering squeezing and entanglement limited in effectiveness. Only above this thermal frontier, defined by $\hbar ω= k_B T \ln 3$, does the quantum regime become accessible. We identify a single concrete realization: a bulk acoustic wave resonator at 1 GHz and 10 mK ($β= 0.98$), and propose a gravitational wave detector employing squeezed phononic states via circuit QED readout. An array of $10^4$ such resonators with 10 dB mechanical squeezing reaches $\sqrt{S_h} = 7.6 \times 10^{-26}/\sqrt{\rm Hz}$ -- still a factor ~$10^9$ above the BBN bound on stochastic backgrounds at 1 GHz, indicating that the sensitivity gap remains predominantly classical in origin and that concurrent advances in classical detector parameters will be required.
one_liner: A quantum-noise-fraction metric identifies when squeezing can actually help high-frequency gravitational-wave detectors.
why_it_matters: It sharply separates thermal and quantum-limited regimes, guiding where quantum photonic or phononic enhancement is worth deploying.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce the quantum noise fraction beta as a diagnostic for the maximum sensitivity gain available from quantum enhancement in high-frequency gravitational-wave detectors. Applying it across proposed kHz-GHz concepts, they find resonant-mass tidal detectors remain thermally dominated below about 230 MHz at dilution temperatures, so squeezing and entanglement offer little benefit there. They identify a viable quantum-regime case in a 1 GHz bulk acoustic wave resonator at 10 mK with beta = 0.98 and outline a squeezed-phonon detector read out via circuit QED. Even with 10^4 resonators and 10 dB squeezing, the projected strain sensitivity remains far above the BBN bound, implying the main bottleneck is still classical detector performance.
platform_or_system: bulk acoustic wave resonators with circuit QED readout
main_result: The work defines a thermal frontier for quantum enhancement and pinpoints 1 GHz, 10 mK bulk acoustic wave resonators as a concrete regime where squeezing can matter.
application_angle: It offers a practical screening tool for designing quantum-enhanced gravitational-wave sensors and avoiding architectures where thermal noise dominates.

title: Beyond Project-Based Learning: Conference-Style Writing as Authentic Assessment in Interdisciplinary Quantum Engineering Education
abstract: Project-based learning is recognized as an effective approach for improving engagement and applied understanding in STEM education. In quantum engineering courses, however, the question is no longer only whether students benefit from projects but how those projects should culminate if the goal is authentic disciplinary preparation. This paper examines the educational role of a conference-style paper requirement embedded within a project-based learning implementation for an introductory quantum mechanics course for engineers. We use post-course survey responses from students in a pilot run of the course. We evaluate perceived effects on conceptual understanding, scientific communication, research readiness, and attitudes toward the writing requirement itself. The results suggest that students viewed the project as beneficial for engagement, confidence, and technical skill development, while the conference-style paper emerged as a demanding but meaningful component of the experience. We argue that once PBL has been established in quantum mechanics education, conference-style writing can serve as an extension of that model, especially for graduate students. The findings support retaining the conference-paper requirement with improved scaffolding.
one_liner: Conference-style paper writing is evaluated as an authentic assessment layer on top of project-based quantum engineering instruction.
why_it_matters: It addresses workforce preparation in quantum engineering by testing a course design that better mirrors research communication practice.
detailed_summary: Based on post-course surveys from a pilot introductory quantum mechanics course for engineers, the paper studies a conference-style paper requirement embedded within project-based learning. Students reported benefits in engagement, confidence, conceptual understanding, and technical skill development, while also describing the writing component as demanding but meaningful. The authors argue that conference-style writing extends project-based learning toward more authentic disciplinary preparation, especially for graduate-level training. They recommend keeping the requirement with better scaffolding.
main_result: The study finds that a conference-paper deliverable is perceived as a meaningful and skill-building assessment in a project-based quantum engineering course.
application_angle: While not hardware-focused, it is relevant to training the interdisciplinary workforce needed for practical quantum technology development.

title: Light-Cone Structure of Propagation of Entanglement
abstract: For a wide class of bipartite systems with localized couplings, we establish existence of an effective light-cone for propagation of entanglement. This result yields a hard lower bound on the time it takes, under ideal conditions (no loss, no decoherence), to transport entanglement to a distant location (say, a node of a graph-structured quantum network), or to maintain it there.
one_liner: An effective light-cone is proven for entanglement transport in bipartite systems with localized couplings.
why_it_matters: It sets hard timing limits for distributing or sustaining entanglement across quantum networks even under idealized conditions.
detailed_summary: The paper establishes the existence of an effective light-cone governing propagation of entanglement in a broad class of bipartite systems with localized interactions. From this structure, the authors derive rigorous lower bounds on the time required to move entanglement to distant nodes in graph-like quantum networks or maintain it there. The result is notable because it applies even in ideal settings without loss or decoherence, isolating a fundamental dynamical constraint. This provides a clean theoretical limit for entanglement distribution protocols.
platform_or_system: graph-structured quantum networks with localized couplings
main_result: A general lower-bound framework is derived showing that entanglement distribution obeys an effective light-cone and cannot be made arbitrarily fast.
application_angle: These bounds inform latency expectations and protocol design for long-distance quantum communication and repeater-network architectures.

title: Enhanced stability from co-resonant cavities in a monolithic array
abstract: We demonstrate a micro-Fabry-Pérot cavity array through laser etching of high-surface-quality mirrors onto a single fused silica substrate. A cavity finesse of $4750\pm 200$ was achieved with a simple array design with $500~μm$ cavity length, $100~μm$ diameter micromirrors and $300~μm$ transverse separation. Arrays with up to 12 cavities were simultaneously tested for single mode operation, and absolute frequency measurements correlated strongly with the etched depth as measured by profilometry. Simultaneous measurements of the absolute resonant frequency for neighboring cavities showed a factor of 5 common-mode cavity drift reduction. Arrays of such cavities can be employed in chip-scale cavity QED networks (current cooperativity estimates are at the border of strong coupling for $^{87}$Rb atoms, $C=1$) as well as for precise laser stabilization at nearby wavelengths on a chip.
one_liner: A monolithic micro-Fabry-Perot cavity array shows shared-mode stability and chip-scale cavity-QED compatibility.
why_it_matters: Co-resonant cavity arrays with reduced common-mode drift are useful for scalable cavity-QED networks and compact frequency references.
detailed_summary: The authors fabricate a micro-Fabry-Perot cavity array by laser etching high-quality mirrors into a single fused-silica substrate. For a simple 500 um cavity geometry with 100 um micromirrors and 300 um spacing, they measure a finesse of 4750 +/- 200 and verify simultaneous single-mode operation across arrays up to 12 cavities. Absolute resonance frequencies track etched depth, and neighboring cavities exhibit a fivefold reduction in common-mode drift during simultaneous measurements. The reported parameters place chip-scale cavity-QED with 87Rb near the strong-coupling border with estimated cooperativity around C = 1.
main_result: The work demonstrates multi-cavity monolithic arrays with finesse near 4750 and a fivefold reduction in common-mode resonance drift between neighboring cavities.
application_angle: The platform is promising for compact cavity-QED networks, integrated atom-photon interfaces, and on-chip laser stabilization.

title: Measurement of complex scattering matrix in a nano-cavity array for boundary scattering tomography
abstract: On-chip silicon photonic coupled cavity arrays (CCA) are a promising platform for quantum simulators, with access to high Quality (Q) factor resonators, tunability, and foundry compatibility. Furthermore, scalable two-dimensional (2D) silicon photonic CCAs allow for simulation of rich physical phenomena via Hamiltonian engineering. However, complete reconstruction of the Hamiltonian is limited by access to cavities in the bulk, with current approaches relying on imaging scattered light from bulk resonators. These approaches often require additional scatterers to be built in, limiting scalability, while also being hampered by imaging technology in the near-infrared range. Instead of these approaches, Hamiltonian tomography algorithms that require homodyne boundary measurements have been demonstrated in literature, however measurements of complex scattering measurements along a CCA boundary have not been shown. Here, we experimentally demonstrate an on-chip homodyne measurement setup along a single boundary of a $3\times 3$ silicon photonic racetrack resonator array and reconstruct the system's edge scattering matrix.
one_liner: An on-chip homodyne setup reconstructs the complex edge scattering matrix of a 3x3 silicon photonic cavity array from boundary-only measurements.
why_it_matters: Boundary-only complex scattering readout removes the need for built-in bulk scatterers, making Hamiltonian tomography of scalable photonic simulator chips more practical.
detailed_summary: The work experimentally implements homodyne measurements along a single boundary of a 3x3 silicon photonic racetrack resonator array. From these measurements, the authors reconstruct the complex edge scattering matrix, which is the key input for boundary-scattering tomography of the array Hamiltonian. The approach avoids direct optical access to bulk resonators and sidesteps added scatterers that can limit scalability and complicate near-IR imaging. It is directly relevant to characterization of foundry-compatible coupled-cavity photonic quantum simulators.
main_result: The authors measured and reconstructed the complex boundary scattering matrix of an on-chip 3x3 coupled-cavity array using homodyne detection.
application_angle: This enables scalable characterization and calibration of photonic simulator chips without invasive bulk probes or custom imaging structures.

title: Quantum Anonymous Secret Sharing with Permutation Invariant Codes
abstract: Quantum secret sharing schemes are a family of quantum cryptographic protocols which provide secure quantum encodings, mapping one secret to multiple shares of information such that the original secret cannot be accessed without an authorized set of shares present for decoding. In this work, we describe a protocol that enables sender-anonymity during the secret decoding process. By using permutation-invariant QEC codes along with a set of anonymous quantum transmission algorithms, we construct a quantum anonymous secret sharing scheme that achieves sender-anonymity. We quantify information leakage in ramp quantum secret sharing schemes via the quantum conditional min-entropy, justifying it as a valid measure of leaked information by relating it to the Knill-Laflamme quantum error correction conditions. Finally, we evaluate several permutation-invariant codes using this measure to make observations on the information leakage of intermediate shares for each quantum anonymous secret sharing scheme.
one_liner: A quantum secret-sharing protocol uses permutation-invariant codes and anonymous quantum transmission to preserve sender anonymity during decoding.
why_it_matters: It extends quantum secret sharing with an anonymity property and quantifies intermediate-share leakage using a coding-theoretic security measure.
detailed_summary: The paper constructs a quantum anonymous secret sharing scheme by combining permutation-invariant quantum error-correcting codes with anonymous quantum transmission primitives. It formalizes leakage in ramp secret-sharing schemes via the quantum conditional min-entropy and connects that metric to Knill-Laflamme error-correction conditions. Several permutation-invariant codes are then evaluated to compare how much information intermediate share sets reveal. The contribution is protocol and code-design oriented rather than a device demonstration, but it is relevant to secure quantum networking architectures.
main_result: The work proposes an anonymous quantum secret-sharing protocol and evaluates code families for leakage of intermediate shares using conditional min-entropy.
application_angle: The scheme targets privacy-preserving quantum network services where both secret recovery and sender anonymity matter for distributed cryptographic applications.

title: Stable thin-film lithium tantalate modulators operating at high temperature for uncooled operation
abstract: We demonstrate stable operation of a thin-film lithium tantalate (TFLT) modulator at very high operating temperatures. We show that the electro-optic modulation and bandwidth of the TFLT modulators are not affected by high-temperature operation, and both waveguide and resonant modulators are DC-bias stable even at 120°C. At higher temperatures, we even observe 10% reduction of the Vπ of the modulator. Our results position TFLT modulators as a strong candidate for uncooled operation in co-packaged optics.
one_liner: Thin-film lithium tantalate modulators remain bias-stable and high-bandwidth up to 120 C, supporting uncooled photonic operation.
why_it_matters: Thermally robust electro-optic modulators can reduce cooling requirements in co-packaged optics while preserving modulation efficiency and stability.
detailed_summary: The authors demonstrate stable high-temperature operation of thin-film lithium tantalate modulators, including both waveguide and resonant devices. Electro-optic response and bandwidth are maintained at elevated temperature, and DC bias stability persists up to 120 C. At still higher temperatures they observe about a 10% reduction in Vpi, indicating no penalty in drive efficiency. The result strengthens thin-film lithium tantalate as a practical integrated photonics platform for thermally demanding environments.
main_result: Waveguide and resonant TFLT modulators operated stably up to 120 C without bandwidth degradation, with roughly 10% lower Vpi at higher temperature.
application_angle: Uncooled modulators are attractive for co-packaged optical interconnects and other deployed photonic systems where thermal management dominates cost and power.

title: High-Rate Free-Space Continuous-Variable QKD with Self-Referenced Passive State Preparation
abstract: Continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CVQKD) using passive state preparation (PSP) offers low-cost, high-rate secure communication. However, the existing PSP-CVQKD scheme with a transmitted local oscillator has high photon leakage noise and poor stability, making it unsuitable for high-loss transmission. In this work, for the first time, we propose and implement a local local oscillator (LLO) CVQKD system using a self-referenced (SR) PSP scheme, and give a theoretical proof of the equivalence of the PSP and GMCS protocol using temporal-mode theory. By employing the novel self-referenced pilot scheme to achieve high-precision time-varying frequency and phase compensation algorithms, we significantly improve the system' s signal-to-noise ratio and stability. The system achieves a record-high asymptotic secret key rate of 10.34 Mbps over a free-space channel with up to 23.5 dB loss, while maintaining low excess noise and robust performance under turbulent conditions. This work establishes the feasibility of SR-LLO CVQKD, providing a practical pathway toward secure, high-rate quantum communication in realistic environments.
one_liner: A self-referenced local-LO free-space CV-QKD system reaches 10.34 Mbps secret key rate through 23.5 dB loss under turbulence.
why_it_matters: It shows passive-state-prepared CV-QKD can be both high-rate and robust in realistic free-space links, addressing stability and leakage issues of transmitted-LO designs.
detailed_summary: This work proposes and experimentally demonstrates a local-local-oscillator CV-QKD architecture with self-referenced passive state preparation. Using temporal-mode theory, it proves equivalence between the passive-state-prepared scheme and GMCS CV-QKD, then implements a self-referenced pilot for time-varying frequency and phase compensation. The improved phase tracking raises SNR and stability relative to transmitted-LO approaches. Experimentally, the system achieves an asymptotic secret key rate of 10.34 Mbps over a free-space channel with losses up to 23.5 dB while maintaining low excess noise in turbulence.
main_result: The demonstrated SR-LLO CV-QKD link delivered a record 10.34 Mbps asymptotic secret key rate at up to 23.5 dB channel loss.
application_angle: The architecture is a practical fit for high-rate secure optical links in mobile, atmospheric, and satellite-adjacent free-space environments.

title: High-key-rate Fully-Passive Quantum Access Network with Thermal Source
abstract: To accommodate classical communication systems with progressively increasing transmission rates, quantum access networks (QAN) have undergone systematic and protocol-level optimizations in recent years, where quantum passive optical network (QPON) architectures are gaining significant attention due to their simple structure. It is challenging for the previous QAN based on active protocols or Stokes operator coding protocols to achieve high-speed linear modulation with high extinction ratio and stability under practical conditions. In this work, we propose and experimentally demonstrate a downstream fully passive quantum access network protocol using passive state preparation (PSP) with free-space and single-mode fiber hybrid channels, and the final key generation rate is up to a record-breaking 19.48 Mbps per quantum network unit. The proposed PSP-QPON scheme extends the scope of PSP-CVQKD from point-to-point to point-to-multi-point networks, which enables high-key-rate, high-stability, and low-resource-consumption implementation. Moreover, the network channel in this experiment is fully compatible with access networks in classical optical communications, which allows integration with existing optical infrastructure without the need for additional modifications, providing a promising solution for local area network quantum access network at home or a mobile terminal.
one_liner: A fully passive quantum access network using a thermal source delivers 19.48 Mbps per user over hybrid free-space and fiber channels.
why_it_matters: It pushes passive-state-prepared CV-QKD from point-to-point links into practical point-to-multipoint access networks compatible with existing optical infrastructure.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces and experimentally validates a downstream fully passive quantum access network protocol based on passive state preparation with a thermal source. The network uses hybrid free-space and single-mode fiber channels and achieves a reported 19.48 Mbps final key rate per quantum network unit. By eliminating demanding active high-speed modulation requirements, the design aims for higher stability and lower resource consumption. The protocol is explicitly framed as compatible with classical access-network infrastructure, making it relevant for local-area and terminal-side deployment.
main_result: The experiment demonstrated a point-to-multipoint passive quantum access network with up to 19.48 Mbps key rate per user node.
application_angle: Its compatibility with existing access-network hardware makes metropolitan, home, and mobile-terminal quantum-secure connectivity more deployable.

title: Nonlinear exceptional points in an integrated acoustic-wave oscillator for longwave infrared sensing
abstract: Exceptional points (EP) featuring enhanced responsivity and rich dynamics have attracted extensive attentions in device developments and sensing applications. However, it remains debated whether employing EP systems is beneficial in practical sensing applications. Here, we demonstrate that a nonlinear EP in our microwave-frequency acoustic-wave oscillator improves longwave infrared (LWIR) detection under practical conditions. By phase tuning the nonlinear gain, our detector can be operated at different conditions with respect to the nonlinear EP. Compared with operation away from EP, our detector at EP shows a 33-fold improvement in responsivity and an 8.75-fold extension of 3-dB bandwidth. We observe a 6-fold enhancement in signal-to-noise ratio at an input modulation frequency of 6.2 kHz. At the incident LWIR wavelength of 9.6 um, our detector at EP exhibits a noise equivalent power (NEP) of 310 pW*Hz^-1/2 at input frequency of 10 kHz, yielding a figure of merit, product of NEP and time constant, of 9.87*10^-3 pW*Hz^-3/2, a 10-fold improvement over operation away from EP. Our integrated acoustic devices offer a versatile platform for exploring noise dynamics and developing practical sensors that exploit non-Hermitian nonlinearities.
one_liner: A nonlinear exceptional point in an integrated acoustic-wave oscillator boosts longwave-IR sensing responsivity, bandwidth, and SNR.
why_it_matters: It provides a concrete device-level case where exceptional-point physics improves practical sensor metrics rather than only idealized eigenvalue sensitivity.
detailed_summary: The authors realize a microwave-frequency integrated acoustic-wave oscillator whose nonlinear gain can be tuned to operate at or away from a nonlinear exceptional point. At the exceptional point, the LWIR detector shows a 33x responsivity increase, an 8.75x larger 3 dB bandwidth, and a 6x SNR enhancement at 6.2 kHz modulation compared with off-EP operation. For 9.6 um incident radiation, the detector reaches a noise-equivalent power of 310 pW Hz^-1/2 at 10 kHz and a NEP-time-constant figure of merit improved by 10x. The study directly addresses the practical usefulness of EP-enhanced sensing under realistic noise and bandwidth constraints.
main_result: Operating the acoustic-wave LWIR detector at a nonlinear exceptional point substantially improved responsivity, bandwidth, SNR, and NEP-based figure of merit.
application_angle: This points to compact infrared sensors that exploit non-Hermitian dynamics for faster and more sensitive detection in real operating conditions.

title: Demonstration of Exponential Quantum Speedup with Constant-Depth Compiled Circuits for Simon's Problem
abstract: We demonstrate exponential quantum speedup for a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem on present-day superconducting quantum processors by introducing a hardware-aware compilation strategy that compiles the quantum part of each Simon query circuit to constant depth. The resulting compiled circuits have $O(1)$ depth and linear connectivity, map directly onto common device layouts, and avoid additional routing and SWAP overhead. Implemented on IBM's $156$-qubit Boston and $120$-qubit Miami processors, the resulting circuits achieve sufficiently high fidelity to exhibit algorithmic quantum speedup without error suppression. Using the number-of-queries-to-solution metric, we observe exponential speedup over the classical lower bound across the full Hamming-weight range studied on Boston and across low-to-intermediate Hamming weights on Miami; at higher Hamming weights on Miami, we still observe polynomial speedup. The same construction also reaches a regime where the original Simon problem is recovered for the problem sizes studied. These results show that careful hardware-aware compilation can make exponential quantum speedup experimentally accessible for a canonical hidden-subgroup problem in the NISQ regime.
one_liner: Hardware-aware constant-depth compilation lets superconducting processors show Simon-problem quantum speedup in the NISQ regime.
why_it_matters: It turns a canonical exponential-separation problem into an experimentally accessible benchmark on real superconducting hardware without error suppression.
detailed_summary: The work compiles the quantum subroutine for a restricted-Hamming-weight version of Simon's problem into O(1)-depth circuits with linear connectivity, avoiding routing and SWAP overhead on standard superconducting layouts. These circuits were executed on IBM's 156-qubit Boston and 120-qubit Miami processors, with performance evaluated using a number-of-queries-to-solution metric. The experiment shows exponential speedup over the classical lower bound across the studied Hamming-weight range on Boston and for low-to-intermediate weights on Miami, with polynomial speedup at higher Miami weights. The same construction also reaches the regime corresponding to the original Simon problem for the tested sizes.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrated algorithmic quantum speedup, including exponential scaling regimes, using constant-depth compiled Simon-query circuits on present-day superconducting devices.
application_angle: The compilation strategy is directly relevant for mapping useful quantum algorithms onto sparse, noisy hardware with minimal overhead.

title: Galilean boost invariance does not survive the trace: symmetry breaking in open quantum systems
abstract: Tracing out a Galilean-invariant Caldeira-Leggett environment breaks Galilean boost covariance of the reduced dynamics, while spatial translations and rotations survive intact. An operator-level analysis of the exact Hu-Paz-Zhang master equation localizes the violation entirely in the dissipative anticommutator term, scaling with the damping coefficient $Γ(t)f(t)$. The fluctuation-dissipation theorem ties this coefficient to the absorptive bath response that drives equilibrium momentum diffusion, so for any non-trivial bath spectral density bilinear-coupled Galilean invariance, the fluctuation-dissipation theorem, and reduced boost covariance cannot hold simultaneously. The stochastic decomposition of the influence functional extends the mechanism beyond the quadratic regime. The dimensionless ratio $\hbarγ/k_\mathrm{B} T$ delineates the crossover: cold atoms in dissipative optical lattices and ultracold molecules sit at its edge. Parametric driving offers a one-directional escape: the squeezing rate that protects nonequilibrium entanglement above the standard quantum limit also suppresses boost-breaking over a driving cycle.
one_liner: Open-system tracing breaks Galilean boost covariance, identifying a symmetry limit relevant to dissipative optical-lattice and molecule platforms.
why_it_matters: It pinpoints a fundamental symmetry tradeoff in reduced quantum dynamics that matters for modeling precision photonic and cold-atom experiments under dissipation.
detailed_summary: Using an operator-level analysis of the exact Hu-Paz-Zhang master equation, the paper shows that tracing out a Galilean-invariant Caldeira-Leggett bath preserves translations and rotations but breaks boost covariance in the reduced dynamics. The violation is localized to the dissipative anticommutator term and scales with the coefficient Γ(t)f(t), which is tied by fluctuation-dissipation relations to absorptive bath response and equilibrium momentum diffusion. A stochastic influence-functional treatment extends the mechanism beyond quadratic models. The dimensionless parameter hbar*gamma/kBT marks the crossover regime, with dissipative optical lattices and ultracold molecules near the edge, while parametric driving can suppress boost breaking over a cycle.
platform_or_system: dissipative optical lattices and ultracold molecules
main_result: The main result is a proof that reduced Galilean boost invariance cannot generally coexist with nontrivial bilinear bath coupling and fluctuation-dissipation-consistent open dynamics.
application_angle: This gives a sharper theoretical basis for designing and interpreting dissipative photonic and cold-atom platforms where symmetry assumptions affect control and sensing performance.

title: Observation antibunching with classical light in a linear interferometer
abstract: Understanding the boundary between classical and nonclassical phenomena is important for both fundamental researches in quantum optics and applications in quantum information. One of the most interesting research directions in this field is exploring nonclassical effects with classical light. In this paper, we will show that it is possible to observe antibunching with thermal light in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer by treating single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving detectors to perform photon-number projection measurements. Both temporal and spatial antibunching is observed via the correlation of two detectors detecting one and zero photon, respectively. By comparing the measured results of thermal and laser light, it is found that the observed antibunching arises from the combined effect of photon statistics of thermal light and photon-number projection measurement.The classical and nonclassical nature of the observed antibunching is analyzed. The results are helpful to understand the connection between classical and nonclassical correlation and may find applications in multiphoton interference and quantum imaging.
one_liner: A linear HBT interferometer shows apparent antibunching with thermal light by using photon-number projection at single-photon detectors.
why_it_matters: It clarifies how detector modeling and measurement backaction can create antibunching signatures without genuinely nonclassical light sources.
detailed_summary: The experiment treats nominal single-photon detectors as photon-number-resolving devices performing projections onto one-photon and zero-photon outcomes in a Hanbury Brown-Twiss interferometer. Under this measurement model, both temporal and spatial antibunching are observed for thermal light, while comparisons with laser light isolate the role of the source photon statistics. The analysis argues that the effect arises from the combination of thermal statistics and projection measurement rather than from intrinsically nonclassical input fields. This sharpens the boundary between classical-field behavior and nonclassical correlation signatures in linear optics.
platform_or_system: thermal light in an HBT interferometer
main_result: The authors observed temporal and spatial antibunching signatures using thermal light and linear optics through photon-number projection measurements.
application_angle: The result is useful for multiphoton interference metrology and quantum imaging schemes that rely on correlation signatures but may use classically generated light.

title: Magnonic Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill states
abstract: Bosonic quantum error correction encodes a logical qubit in an oscillator, avoiding the hardware overhead of large qubit arrays. Among such encodings, Gottesman-Kitaev-Preskill (GKP) states are paticularly powerful because their phase-space grid structure protects against small displacement errors simultaneously in both conjugate quadratures. Here we provide the first protocol for preparing magnonic GKP states, which involves an ellipsoidal magnetic crystal effectively coupled to a superconducting qubit via a microwave cavity. The geometric anisotropy intrinsically squeezes the magnon mode, while the cavity-mediated qubit control realizes an effective conditional-displacement interaction. We show that two rounds of a conditional-displacement interaction and a qubit projective measurement yield three- and four-component magnonic GKP-like states. We also show how to realize single logical qubit gate operations, such as Pauli, Hadamard and phase gates, completing the logical Pauli basis of the approximate GKP code. Our results establish hybrid magnon-qubit systems as a promising platform for preparing bosonic code states, with applications in magnonic fault-tolerant quantum computation and quantum sensing.
one_liner: A hybrid magnon-qubit protocol generates GKP-like bosonic code states and logical gates in a cavity-mediated magnonic platform.
why_it_matters: It opens a new bosonic error-correction route in spin-wave hardware that could reduce qubit overhead and link computation with sensing.
detailed_summary: The proposal uses an ellipsoidal magnetic crystal whose geometric anisotropy intrinsically squeezes a magnon mode, while a microwave cavity mediates an effective conditional-displacement interaction with a superconducting qubit. Two rounds of conditional displacement followed by qubit projective measurement prepare three- and four-component magnonic GKP-like states. The scheme also implements logical Pauli, Hadamard, and phase gates within the approximate GKP code space. Together, these ingredients establish a complete hybrid route for preparing and controlling bosonic code states in magnonic hardware.
main_result: The paper presents the first concrete protocol for preparing magnonic GKP-like states and performing key logical single-qubit gates in a hybrid magnon-qubit system.
application_angle: If realized, the scheme could enable lower-overhead fault-tolerant hardware and quantum-enhanced sensing based on protected magnon oscillator states.

title: Experimental detection of entanglement in multimode Gaussian states from high-order intensity correlation moments
abstract: Quantum universal invariants of a Gaussian state's covariance matrix, which can be derived from intensity correlation moments, have been adopted to characterize the entanglement properties of Gaussian states via the positive partial transpose criterion, also known as the Peres-Horodecki separability criterion. Such intensity correlation moments enable the extraction of information about the covariance matrix without the need for a coherent local oscillator. Here, we experimentally detect the entanglement properties of multimode Gaussian states using high-order\,(up to sixth-order) intensity correlation moments. These multimode Gaussian states are prepared via spontaneous and cascaded parametric down-conversion pumped by a high-peak-energy pulsed laser. Their intensity correlation moments are measured using a pseudo-photon-number-resolving detector constructed through spatial multiplexing of 32 threshold superconducting nanowire single photon detectors. This method is successfully demonstrated for two-mode and three-mode Gaussian states and can be extended to $N$-mode Gaussian states with $N>3$.
one_liner: High-order intensity moments measured with multiplexed SNSPDs detect entanglement in multimode Gaussian states without a local oscillator.
why_it_matters: It provides a scalable, experimentally practical entanglement-detection method for multimode photonic Gaussian resources used in quantum networks and processors.
detailed_summary: The experiment infers quantum universal invariants of the covariance matrix from intensity-correlation moments up to sixth order, avoiding homodyne detection and coherent local oscillators. Multimode Gaussian states are generated using spontaneous and cascaded parametric down-conversion driven by a high-peak-energy pulsed laser. Their correlation moments are recorded with a pseudo-photon-number-resolving detector built from spatial multiplexing of 32 threshold superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors. The method is demonstrated on two-mode and three-mode states and is designed to extend to higher-mode Gaussian systems.
platform_or_system: PDC Gaussian states with multiplexed SNSPD detection
main_result: The authors experimentally detected entanglement in two- and three-mode Gaussian photonic states using only high-order intensity-correlation measurements.
application_angle: This approach is attractive for characterizing complex photonic entanglement in platforms where stable local-oscillator phase control is difficult or costly.

title: Quantum Magnetometry with Orientation beyond Steady-State Limits in Cavity-Magnon Systems
abstract: We present a transient quantum sensing framework for cavity-magnon systems that circumvents the inevitable loss of initial-state quantum properties plaguing conventional steady-state protocols. Explicitly incorporating finite-time dynamics and adopting an engineered steady state as the initial condition, we derive the exact transient noise spectrum. We show that residual initial quantum correlations alone can drastically enhance the short-time signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) beyond that achievable with unsqueezed steady-state schemes. Through analysis of the transient spectral density and joint measurements of orthogonal cavity quadratures, we realize crosstalk-free reconstruction of all three magnetic field components, enabling orientation of magnetic signals. In the long-time limit, our theory yields a closed-form stationary noise spectrum and uncovers a resonance condition $g_{am}=\sqrt{κ_aκ_m}/2$, where cavity field quantum noise is fully canceled without requiring strong coherent coupling. Away from this resonance, injected squeezing further suppresses cavity induced noise and broadens the detection bandwidth. Extending the framework to an array of $N$ yttrium iron garnet (YIG) spheres generates a collective bright mode, with magnon-probe noise scaling as $1/N$. Our results establish a unified route to scalable, high precision, multidimensional quantum magnetometry using cavity-magnon platforms.
one_liner: Transient cavity-magnon sensing with residual quantum correlations enables vector magnetometry beyond steady-state limits.
why_it_matters: It shows a practical path to higher-SNR, multidimensional quantum magnetometers and favorable scaling with YIG arrays.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a finite-time sensing theory for cavity-magnon systems that uses an engineered steady state as the initial condition and derives the exact transient noise spectrum. Residual initial quantum correlations enhance short-time signal-to-noise ratio beyond unsqueezed steady-state protocols, while joint measurements of orthogonal cavity quadratures reconstruct all three magnetic-field components without crosstalk. In the stationary limit, the analysis identifies a resonance condition g_am = sqrt(kappa_a kappa_m)/2 that cancels cavity-field quantum noise even without strong coherent coupling. Extending to an array of N YIG spheres produces a collective bright mode with magnon-probe noise scaling as 1/N.
main_result: The main result is a transient quantum magnetometry framework that surpasses steady-state limits, enables full vector-field reconstruction, and scales favorably in YIG arrays.
application_angle: This directly targets compact, high-precision magnetic-field sensing for navigation, materials probing, and cryogenic quantum-device diagnostics.

title: Hyperfine-resolved laser excitation and detection of nuclear isomer in trapped $^{229}$Th$^{3+}$ ions
abstract: We present a comprehensive theoretical investigation of hyperfine-resolved excitation and detection of the low-energy isomeric state of $^{229}$Th in trapped $^{229}\mathrm{Th}^{3+}$ ions. Using a quantum master equation approach, we analyze the dependence of the isomeric population on laser linewidth, detuning, and irradiation time, showing that their proper matching is essential for efficient excitation. We further propose two nuclear-state detection schemes based on three hyperfine-resolved electronic fluorescence channels at 690, 984, and 1088 nm. Our analysis shows that the 690-nm and 984-nm scheme yields detectable photon rates on the order of $10^4~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion for each wavelength, whereas the 1088-nm scheme achieves a higher rate on the order of $10^5~\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ per ion. By quantifying the trade-off between irradiation time and scan-step size, we show that the nuclear transition can be located within one month for a 100-MHz uncertainty using currently available vacuum-ultraviolet laser technology. These results provide practical guidance for trapped-ion $^{229}\mathrm{Th}$ spectroscopy and the development of nuclear clocks.
one_liner: A theory-guided roadmap shows how to excite and read out the 229Th nuclear isomer in trapped 229Th3+ with practical VUV scan times.
why_it_matters: It turns a difficult nuclear-clock search problem into an experimentally parameterized protocol with detectable fluorescence rates and realistic timelines.
detailed_summary: The paper models hyperfine-resolved excitation of the low-energy 229Th isomer in trapped 229Th3+ using a quantum master equation, explicitly tracking how laser linewidth, detuning, and irradiation time set the achievable isomer population. It proposes nuclear-state detection through three electronic fluorescence channels at 690, 984, and 1088 nm, with predicted count rates around 10^4 s^-1 per ion for the 690/984 nm scheme and 10^5 s^-1 per ion for 1088 nm. By quantifying the trade-off between dwell time and spectral scan-step size, the authors estimate that a 100 MHz transition uncertainty can be resolved within about one month using existing VUV laser technology. The analysis is directly framed as operational guidance for spectroscopy and clock development rather than only as a conceptual proposal.
main_result: The study identifies excitation and fluorescence-readout conditions that make locating and detecting the 229Th nuclear isomer transition feasible with current trapped-ion and VUV-laser capabilities.
application_angle: This provides an actionable route toward a nuclear clock and ultra-precise frequency metrology based on 229Th.

title: An Analytical Approach to Design Space Exploration for Cavity-Mediated Quantum State Transfer in Multi-core Architectures
abstract: In multi-core quantum computing architectures, waveguide-mediated interconnects are essential for facilitating fast, high-fidelity quantum state transfer between qubits located in different chips. However, optimizing these systems typically relies on computationally expensive numerical simulations that offer limited physical insight. In this work, we derive exact analytical expressions for the state transfer dynamics of a two-qubit system coupled via a waveguide, modeled through a Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian and the Lindblad master equation. We apply the Monte Carlo wave-function method and obtain a closed-form solution for qubit occupation probabilities that accounts for both detuning and dissipative losses. Our analytical framework provides a significant computational speedup compared to standard numerical solvers, enabling large-scale parameter sweeps while maintaining high precision in both fidelity and latency predictions. Furthermore, the model reveals and explains systematic low-fidelity regions arising from destructive interference between internal oscillations and detuning-induced envelopes, which are phenomena that are difficult to characterize through numerical means alone. Finally, we propose a simplified latency model and an efficiency-based function to enable rapid identification of optimal operating points. This analytical approach provides a robust foundation for the design and optimization of interconnects in multi-core quantum processors.
one_liner: An exact analytic model accelerates design-space exploration for cavity- or waveguide-mediated quantum state transfer between chips.
why_it_matters: Fast closed-form fidelity and latency estimates can shorten interconnect optimization cycles for modular and multi-core quantum processors.
detailed_summary: The authors derive exact analytical expressions for the dynamics of two qubits coupled through a waveguide within a Jaynes-Cummings plus Lindblad framework, using a Monte Carlo wave-function treatment to obtain closed-form occupation probabilities. The solution captures both detuning and dissipative loss, allowing high-precision prediction of transfer fidelity and latency without repeated heavy numerical simulation. The model also explains low-fidelity regions as interference between internal oscillations and detuning-induced envelopes, exposing a failure mode that standard parameter sweeps can obscure. A simplified latency model and an efficiency metric are then proposed for rapid operating-point selection.
main_result: The paper delivers a closed-form state-transfer model that reproduces fidelity and latency trends while enabling much faster parameter sweeps for inter-chip quantum links.
application_angle: It is directly useful for engineering photonic or cavity-mediated interconnects in scalable modular quantum computing hardware.

title: Parametrically Driven iSWAP Gate Using a Capacitively Shunted Double-Transmon Coupler at the Zero-Flux Sweet Spot
abstract: A double-transmon coupler (DTC) enables a fast, high-fidelity CZ gate between two highly detuned, fixed-frequency transmon qubits. Moreover, a recently proposed capacitively shunted DTC (CSDTC) realizes a small residual ZZ interaction over a wide flux-bias range around zero flux, eliminating the necessity of static flux biasing while maintaining high CZ-gate fidelity. However, CZ gates with the DTC and CSDTC require baseband flux pulses with large amplitudes, which are vulnerable to pulse distortion and decoherence due to large qubit-coupler hybridization. To address these issues, we experimentally demonstrate a parametrically driven iSWAP gate operated at zero flux bias between highly detuned, fixed-frequency transmon qubits coupled through a CSDTC. Using a simple flux-drive waveform without predistortion, we realize an average gate fidelity of 99.92(2)% at a total gate time of 112 ns. The observed high-fidelity performance is consistent with small qubit-coupler hybridization and small effective ZZ interaction during the gate. Our numerical simulations reproduce the experimentally observed iSWAP interaction rate and effective ZZ interaction, demonstrating the applicability of the theoretical model not only to spectral information but also to time-domain dynamics such as gate operations. These results boost further progress in the research of superconducting quantum computers.
one_liner: A zero-flux parametric iSWAP gate with a capacitively shunted double-transmon coupler reaches 99.92% fidelity in 112 ns.
why_it_matters: It shows a high-fidelity entangling gate that avoids large static flux bias and heavy pulse predistortion, easing superconducting control engineering.
detailed_summary: This experiment implements a parametrically driven iSWAP between highly detuned fixed-frequency transmons linked by a capacitively shunted double-transmon coupler operated at the zero-flux sweet spot. Using a simple flux-drive waveform without predistortion, the device achieves an average gate fidelity of 99.92(2)% with a total duration of 112 ns. The reported performance is consistent with low qubit-coupler hybridization and small effective ZZ interaction during the gate, addressing limitations of large-amplitude baseband-flux CZ implementations. Numerical simulations reproduce both the measured interaction rate and effective ZZ behavior, supporting the time-domain gate model.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate a 112 ns zero-flux parametric iSWAP gate with 99.92(2)% average fidelity between highly detuned superconducting qubits.
application_angle: This gate scheme improves robustness and control simplicity for scalable superconducting processors using fixed-frequency qubits.

title: Size-Limited Room Temperature Single-Photon Emission from Sidewall-Treated Fractional Dimension InGaN Quantum Dots: Determined by Density-of-States-Corrected Ultrafast Carrier Dynamics and Improved Signal-to-Noise Ratio
abstract: Room-temperature single-photon emission (SPE) resulting from a biexciton-exciton cascaded decay is demonstrated for the first time from chemically and photoelectrochemically etched site-controlled In0.14Ga0.86N quantum dots (QDs) embedded in vertical GaN nanowires. Diameter-dependent biexciton-exciton dynamics are analysed to determine the eligibility of QD as a single-photon emitter. The signal-to-noise ratio degrades with increasing QD diameter. Background noise photons pose a bottleneck to achieving SPE. This is also explained from a carrier dynamics perspective. Surface recombination contributes to inhomogeneous broadening at QD diameters larger than 35 nm. Below 35 nm, density-of-states-corrected Auger gradually becomes the principal biexciton-decay route with further reduction in QD diameter, thereby quenching the possibility of thermal broadening and setting a threshold for SPE. Below 9 nm, the Auger recombination rate becomes manyfold of other decay rates, causing multi-photon suppression via single Auger decay to form an exciton. Surface recombination probability of this exciton is minimized while biexciton state filling probability is maximized by reducing sidewall surface states through wet-treatment. These improve biexciton state preparation and enhance the single-photon purity of the exciton towards the exciton Bohr radius (3 nm) regime. Far away from this regime, higher-order autocorrelations to characterize quantum emission involving multi-photon events are discussed. This study establishes a generalized physical framework for predetermining SPE probability as a function of QD surface and geometry down to the exciton Bohr radius regime, with practical implementations. This work shows the pathway to design and develop next-generation semiconductor QDs for high-purity room-temperature SPE.
one_liner: Room-temperature single-photon emission is realized in sidewall-treated InGaN quantum dots by tuning dot size and suppressing surface states.
why_it_matters: It gives a practical geometry-and-surface design rule for high-purity semiconductor single-photon sources that work at room temperature.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates room-temperature single-photon emission from site-controlled In0.14Ga0.86N quantum dots embedded in vertical GaN nanowires, with biexciton-exciton cascade dynamics analyzed as a function of dot diameter. The authors show that above roughly 35 nm, surface recombination broadens the emission, while below that scale density-of-states-corrected Auger processes increasingly dominate biexciton decay and help suppress multiphoton events. For diameters below about 9 nm, Auger recombination strongly outpaces competing channels, enabling single-photon behavior, while wet sidewall treatment reduces surface states to improve biexciton preparation and exciton purity. The paper frames these results as a generalized predictive model linking surface chemistry and geometry to SPE probability down toward the exciton Bohr-radius regime.
platform_or_system: InGaN quantum dots in GaN nanowires
main_result: The study reports room-temperature single-photon emission and identifies a size-dependent crossover where Auger-assisted biexciton dynamics and sidewall passivation enable high-purity operation.
application_angle: This is relevant for manufacturable on-chip quantum light sources for quantum photonics and integrated optical communication hardware.

title: Macroscopic photon counting beating the Poisson noise limit
abstract: Photon counting is a cornerstone of quantum optics. Here, we demonstrate precisely counting from 0 to over 9000 photons, beating the Poisson noise limit by at least $4.1~\mathrm{dB}$ across this range. We achieve sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse. To do so, we multiplex eight intrinsically photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors across 128 temporal modes. We use a model-informed characterization of each of the 1024 detection bins, for optimal precision. We perform quantum detector tomography to reconstruct the positive operator valued measures (POVMs) of the complete device, which consists of $1.38\cdot10^8$ matrix elements. At the repetition rate of our experiment of $80~\mathrm{kHz}$, we can precisely count photons corresponding to an optical power of approximately $71~\mathrm{pW}$, bridging the gap from single-photon measurements to high-sensitivity optical power meters. A photon-number-resolving detector of this size, and the tools used to analyze it, will become increasingly important to characterize large quantum states, as well as tasks in precision metrology and optical power standards.
one_liner: A large multiplexed superconducting detector counts up to 9000+ photons with precision beyond the Poisson noise limit.
why_it_matters: It bridges single-photon metrology and sensitive optical power measurement, expanding detector utility for quantum optics and standards.
detailed_summary: The authors build a photon-number-resolving system by multiplexing eight intrinsically PNR superconducting nanowire detectors across 128 temporal modes, yielding 1024 characterized detection bins. With model-informed calibration and full detector tomography, they reconstruct the device POVM comprising 1.38 x 10^8 matrix elements. Experimentally, the setup counts from 0 to more than 9000 photons while beating the Poisson noise limit by at least 4.1 dB, and it maintains sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse. At 80 kHz repetition rate, the achieved range corresponds to roughly 71 pW optical power.
main_result: The detector achieves precise macroscopic photon counting above 9000 photons with noise performance better than the Poisson limit across the full operating range.
application_angle: Such calibrated large-dynamic-range PNR detection is valuable for quantum-state characterization, precision metrology, and optical power standards.

title: YOSO: single-frame Gerchberg-Saxton phase retrieval with AI-based data augmentation for in-line holography
abstract: We present YOSO (You Only Shot Once), a single-frame phase retrieval framework for digital in-line holographic microscopy (DIHM) in which supervised deep learning is used to numerically generate an additional hologram corresponding to different defocus distance, creating a so-called multi-height dataset, which is then conventionally processed with a well-established Gerchberg-Saxton (GS) algorithm. YOSO is trained on computer-generated data derived from natural images, enabling strong generalization. The selected multi-scale ResNet architecture enables rapid training in under two hours on a mid-range workstation, which is done only once, enabling efficient inference thereafter. We further show that YOSO network can process inputs of varying spatial dimensions, allowing training on small inputs and direct inference on full-sized holograms while bypassing patch-and-stitch procedure. A further advantage of YOSO is its physics-consistent hologram padding, which replaces conventional zero or edge-value padding with a physically grounded approach compatible with the GS framework. The YOSO framework is tested on various systems (lens-based and lensless DIHM) and diverse samples: a resolution test target, adherent and suspended biological cells, and a mouse brain slice. The results show that YOSO is compatible with 3D objects and correctly recovers defocused object wave features, enabling holographic postprocessing such as numerical refocusing. The results of this work are available publicly as software for end-to-end implementation.
one_liner: YOSO turns single-shot in-line holograms into multi-height phase retrieval inputs using a learned defocus synthesis step.
why_it_matters: It reduces acquisition burden in holographic microscopy while keeping physics-based reconstruction and refocusing capabilities.
detailed_summary: YOSO uses a supervised neural network to infer an additional defocused hologram from a single measured in-line hologram, effectively creating a synthetic multi-height dataset for Gerchberg-Saxton phase retrieval.
