The network is trained on computer-generated data from natural images, uses a multi-scale ResNet, and generalizes across varying image sizes so full-resolution holograms can be processed without patch stitching. The framework also introduces physics-consistent hologram padding rather than standard zero or edge padding, improving compatibility with wave-propagation reconstruction. Tests on lens-based and lensless DIHM with resolution targets, cells, and brain tissue show recovery of defocused wave features and support downstream numerical refocusing.
main_result: The method enables single-frame phase retrieval by synthesizing a second hologram for conventional GS reconstruction, while preserving 3D and refocusing information across diverse samples.
application_angle: It can simplify high-throughput holographic imaging systems by cutting acquisition complexity while retaining quantitative phase-reconstruction workflows.

title: Second harmonic generation and third harmonic generation in topological insulator-based van der Waals metamaterials
abstract: High-order harmonic generation (HHG) in solids - the frequency up-conversion of an optical signal - is governed by symmetries. At terahertz (THz) frequencies, HHG is a key technology to access high frequency spectral windows that are usually difficult to cover using conventional solid state laser technologies. This effect has been recently exploited in graphene where HHG has been demonstrated, albeit only at odd multiples of the driving frequency owing to its inherent centro-symmetry. In topological insulators (TIs), the combination of spin-orbit interaction and time-reversal symmetry create an insulating bulk state with an inverted band order, inseparably connected with conducting surface states. TIs have been predicted to support unconventional high harmonic generation from the bulk and topological surface, which are usually difficult to be distinguished. However, no experimental results have been provided, so far. Here, we exploit the strong optical field amplification provided by an array of single or double split ring resonators, with embedded Bi2Se3 or (InxBi(1-x))2Se3/ Bi2Se3 van der Waals heterostructures, to achieve up-conversion in the 6.4 (even) - 9.7 (odd) THz frequency range. This results from bulk centro-symmetry (odd states) and symmetry breaking in the topological surface states (odd and even).
one_liner: Topological-insulator van der Waals metamaterials generate both second- and third-harmonic THz signals by combining resonant field enhancement with surface-state symmetry breaking.
why_it_matters: It demonstrates even and odd THz harmonic generation in a solid-state topological platform, opening a route to compact nonlinear frequency converters in hard-to-reach spectral bands.
detailed_summary: The authors embed Bi2Se3 and (InxBi1-x)2Se3/Bi2Se3 van der Waals heterostructures inside single and double split-ring resonator arrays to amplify incident THz fields. They observe up-converted emission at 6.4 THz and 9.7 THz, corresponding to second- and third-harmonic generation. The measurements are interpreted as a coexistence of odd-order response from the centrosymmetric bulk and even-plus-odd response from symmetry-broken topological surface states. This provides an experimental route to disentangle bulk and surface nonlinearities in topological materials.
platform_or_system: TI van der Waals metamaterial with split-ring resonators
main_result: The device experimentally produces both second- and third-harmonic THz radiation from topological-insulator-based metamaterials.
application_angle: Such resonantly enhanced TI nonlinear metastructures could serve as compact THz frequency up-converters and nonlinear sources for spectroscopy and photonic integration.

title: Adaptable Continuous Variable Quantum Network with Finite Size Security
abstract: In recent years, continuous-variable quantum key distribution (CV-QKD) has become a promising paradigm for enabling secure communication among multiple end users sharing the same telecommunication backbone. CV-QKD with reverse reconciliation naturally enables scalability from conventional point-to-point links to quantum access networks based on passive quantum broadcasting channels. Here, we report an experimental demonstration on an active $1:4$ multi-user CV quantum network (QN) in the finite-size regime. With $1.25\cdot10^9$ coherent states exchanged on each $11\text{km}$ quantum channel, the highest performance for secret key generation totaling $1.9\cdot10^{-1}$ bits/channel use. Furthermore, we investigate adaptable CV-QN protocols that comprehensively allow network operation in various security and key rates requirements of individual users. The results establish the practical security of CV-QN compatible with existing telecommunication for broad deployment, and allowing additional degree of freedom for connected end users in existing infrastructures.
one_liner: An active 1:4 continuous-variable quantum network is experimentally demonstrated with finite-size security over four 11 km links.
why_it_matters: It moves CV-QKD from point links toward deployable multi-user telecom quantum networks with explicit finite-size security and configurable user tradeoffs.
detailed_summary: This work reports an active 1:4 continuous-variable quantum network in which each user channel spans 11 km and exchanges 1.25x10^9 coherent states. Under finite-size analysis, the network achieves total secret-key performance up to 1.9x10^-1 bits per channel use. The authors also study adaptable protocol settings that let different users trade security level against key rate within the same network architecture. The experiment is framed as compatible with existing telecommunication infrastructure and passive-broadcast style scaling concepts.
main_result: The experiment demonstrates finite-size-secure secret-key generation across a four-user CV quantum network over metropolitan-scale fiber channels.
application_angle: The result supports practical secure multi-user quantum access networks that can be layered onto current telecom backbones without requiring discrete-variable hardware stacks.

title: Spatially Resolved Temperature Measurement Using Rydberg Doppler Broadening Thermometry
abstract: We demonstrate a technique for spatially resolved temperature measurement utilizing Rydberg Doppler broadening thermometry. This method employs two focused laser beams arranged perpendicularly to excite laser-cooled atoms from the ground state to a Rydberg state via two photon absorption process. Temperature is obtained through the Doppler broadening of the spectral line. The perpendicular configuration allows for selective probing of a specific position within the atomic cloud, enabling localized temperature measurement. This technique, in principle, offers a temperature resolution on the order of \SI{}{\nano\kelvin}, attributed to the exceptionally narrow natural linewidth of the involved rubidium Rydberg transition line. Furthermore, the setup enables the measurement of position-velocity correlations within the cold atom ensemble. The velocity information is extracted through the Doppler shift, whereas the spatial information is inferred from the arrival time of ions detected by a channel electron multiplier detector. Using this technique, for the first time, we observed the spatial temperature gradient within the atomic cloud, with higher temperature in the wings and lower temperature in the center.
one_liner: Perpendicular two-photon Rydberg excitation enables spatially resolved thermometry inside a cold-atom cloud via Doppler-broadened spectra.
why_it_matters: It adds localized, potentially ultrahigh-resolution thermal diagnostics to neutral-atom platforms where temperature inhomogeneity directly affects coherence and control fidelity.
detailed_summary: The method uses two focused perpendicular laser beams to drive a two-photon transition from ground-state rubidium atoms to a Rydberg state, so only a localized region is probed. Temperature is extracted from Doppler broadening of the narrow Rydberg spectral line, while ion arrival times on a channel electron multiplier provide spatial and velocity information. This configuration also gives access to position-velocity correlations within the ensemble. Using the technique, the authors directly observe a temperature gradient across the cloud, with hotter wings and a colder center.
main_result: The authors demonstrate localized temperature readout and resolve an internal spatial temperature gradient in a cold-atom ensemble.
application_angle: This offers an in situ diagnostic for optimizing atom-trap loading, cooling uniformity, and neutral-atom quantum hardware performance at the sub-cloud level.

title: Branch-Resolved Characterization of Feed-Forward Error in Dynamic Teleportation via Classical Choi Shadows
abstract: Mid-circuit measurement and classical feed-forward are essential primitives for dynamic-circuit teleportation on superconducting quantum processors. However, the error associated with measurement-conditioned corrective operations remains poorly understood when evaluated with respect to individual measurement branches. In this paper, we present a framework for characterizing feed-forward error in dynamic circuit teleportation without losing valuable information related to its behavior across separate branches. We analyze three approaches to applying measurement-conditioned corrections: (i) physical application, (ii) post-processing adjustments, and (iii) a mitigated physical application which utilizes Bit-Flip Averaging (BFA)-based Probabilistic Readout Error Mitigation (PROM). We experimentally reconstruct branch Choi operators via an entangled reference qubit, and validate our physical-application and post-processing Choi-shadow estimators against full tomography of the branch Choi operators. We perform experiments on two physical qubit layouts which differ greatly in mid-circuit measurement readout error, and observe a reversal in the relative order in branch qualities obtained from the post-processing and PROM mitigation strategies. In one physical layout with higher measurement readout error, the operational feed-forward penalty is relatively modest (approximately 0.02-0.03) and PROM produces higher branch qualities than post-processing for every branch. In a separate layout with lower readout error, the operational feed-forward penalty increases to roughly 0.07, and post-processing exceeds PROM for all branch qualities. Our characterization framework can reveal branch-specific error structure and mitigation behavior that state-of-the-art outcome-averaged analyses fail to expose.
one_liner: Branch-resolved Choi-shadow tomography exposes how feed-forward errors behave across individual teleportation branches in superconducting dynamic circuits.
why_it_matters: It gives hardware teams a sharper diagnostic for mid-circuit measurement and conditional-control errors that averaged teleportation metrics can hide.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a framework to reconstruct branch-specific Choi operators for dynamic-circuit teleportation using an entangled reference qubit and classical Choi shadows. Three correction modes are compared experimentally: physical feed-forward, post-processing corrections, and physically applied corrections mitigated by bit-flip averaging based probabilistic readout error mitigation. On two superconducting-qubit layouts with different readout characteristics, the branch ordering reverses between post-processing and mitigation strategies, showing that averaged analyses miss important structure. Measured feed-forward penalties are modest on the noisier layout, around 0.02-0.03, but rise to about 0.07 on the lower-readout-error layout.
platform_or_system: superconducting quantum processor with mid-circuit feed-forward
main_result: The study experimentally validates branch-resolved Choi-shadow characterization and reveals layout-dependent reversals in the best feed-forward mitigation strategy.
application_angle: This can improve calibration and mitigation of dynamic-circuit primitives needed for teleportation, modular networking, and fault-tolerant superconducting processors.

title: Multimode grating couplers via foundry-compliant inverse design
abstract: We apply a systematic inverse design approach to discover foundry-compliant, multilayer grating couplers that can efficiently couple a number of independent waves from free space to on-chip propagating modes. For visible- and near-infrared couplers, we find that minimum feature sizes are by far the most important constraint to tailor the design algorithms around. If, additionally, one forces the optimization to be robust to over- and under-etch errors, the resulting designs exhibit stable optimal efficiencies in the presence of other imperfections (critical dimension variations, overlay mismatch, and sidewall angle variation). The foundry-compliant designs exhibit moderate efficiency penalties as feature sizes increase, but no change to simple underlying scaling laws with respect to requisite numbers of layers and layer thicknesses. These results establish a practical, generalizable framework for high-efficiency multimode coupling within the constraints of modern semiconductor foundries.
one_liner: Foundry-compliant inverse design yields multilayer multimode grating couplers that stay efficient under realistic fabrication constraints.
why_it_matters: It bridges inverse-designed couplers with semiconductor foundry rules, which is critical for scalable photonic packaging and repeatable quantum-photonic manufacturing.
detailed_summary: The authors develop a systematic inverse-design workflow for multilayer grating couplers that connect multiple independent free-space waves to on-chip modes in visible and near-infrared platforms. They identify minimum feature size as the dominant fabrication constraint and show that enforcing over- and under-etch robustness also improves resilience to critical-dimension variation, overlay mismatch, and sidewall-angle errors. Efficiency degrades moderately as allowed feature sizes grow, but the underlying scaling with number of layers and thickness remains unchanged. The result is a practical design framework aligned with modern photonic foundry process limits.
main_result: The work demonstrates robust, high-efficiency multimode grating-coupler designs that satisfy realistic foundry fabrication rules.
application_angle: These couplers are relevant for manufacturable free-space-to-chip interfaces in quantum photonic links, multiplexed transceivers, and packaged integrated optics.

title: Many-mode grating couplers by avoiding undesired couplings
abstract: To couple many independent modes from free space to on chip, the key challenge is not enhancing the many necessary coupling rates (scattering-matrix elements) between targeted mode pairs. Instead, the key is to avoid additional cross-couplings to undesired modes, due to the presence of multiple simultaneously satisfied phase-matching conditions. With this principle, we identify scaling laws for the maximum number of high-efficiency multi-mode couplings that may be achievable for a given refractive index and design region, which are strongly supported by extensive numerical inverse-design experiments in 2D (one-dimensional coupler patterns, scattering in 2D). For such couplers, typical mode counts of 5--10 appear achievable. Three-dimensional couplers (patterned across two dimensions) can be markedly better, with tens of Fourier components in a single-layer device offering the possibility of high-efficiency coupling of hundreds to thousands of modes in relatively compact form factors. Numerical simulations of such a device, without any parameter optimization, predict efficiencies on the order of 5\% for 100 modes -- a collective order-of-magnitude improvement over previous designs.
one_liner: A many-mode grating-coupler theory shows that suppressing undesired phase-matched cross-couplings is the main route to dense free-space/on-chip mode conversion.
why_it_matters: It provides scaling laws and design intuition for dramatically higher mode-count optical interfaces, a bottleneck for multiplexed quantum and classical photonic systems.
detailed_summary: Rather than focusing on boosting desired coupling elements, the paper argues that multimode coupler performance is limited by unwanted cross-couplings arising from simultaneously satisfied phase-matching conditions. Supported by extensive 2D inverse-design studies, the authors derive scaling laws suggesting roughly 5-10 high-efficiency modes are realistic in typical 2D couplers. They then argue 3D patterned couplers can perform much better, with enough Fourier components to address hundreds to thousands of modes in compact devices. A non-optimized 3D example is simulated to achieve about 5% efficiency for 100 modes, representing an order-of-magnitude collective improvement over earlier designs.
main_result: The study establishes mode-count scaling laws and predicts compact 3D grating couplers capable of coupling on the order of 100 modes with percent-level efficiency.
application_angle: High-mode-count couplers could enable dense spatial or modal multiplexing for quantum communication links, photonic interconnects, and large-channel optical I/O.

title: g-tensor Optimization in Ge/SiGe Quantum Dots
abstract: Planar germanium heterostructures hosting hole-spin qubits are among the leading platforms for scalable semiconductor-based quantum computing. Yet, device performance is hindered by significant quantum dot variability, which leads to uncertainty in qubit energy levels and random orientations of the spin quantization axis. Tailored control of the g-tensor offers a strategy to overcome these limitations and achieve more reliable qubit operations. Here, we introduce a flexible optimization framework for engineering g-tensor properties. As a benchmark, we numerically obtain the optimal reshaping of the out-of-plane potential in a SiGe-Ge-SiGe quantum well to suppress the in-plane g-tensor components and realize the recently proposed gapless single-spin qubit encoding. This reshaping is achieved through heterostructure engineering, specifically by adjusting the silicon concentration within the quantum well, though the framework remains readily adaptable to alternative design objectives. Our results provide practical design principles for improving the tunability of the spin response, paving the way towards large-scale germanium-based quantum computers.
one_liner: A numerical design framework tunes the hole-spin g-tensor in Ge/SiGe dots to suppress unwanted in-plane components.
why_it_matters: It gives actionable heterostructure design rules to reduce qubit variability and enable more robust germanium spin-qubit operation at scale.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a flexible optimization framework for engineering g-tensor properties in planar Ge/SiGe hole-spin quantum dots. As a benchmark, it numerically reshapes the out-of-plane confinement potential in a SiGe-Ge-SiGe quantum well to suppress in-plane g-tensor terms. The optimized profile can be realized by adjusting the silicon concentration inside the quantum well, linking abstract control targets to practical materials engineering. This is used to target the gapless single-spin qubit encoding and to mitigate device-to-device variability in spin quantization axes and energy splittings.
main_result: The authors show that heterostructure engineering can optimize the g-tensor to strongly suppress in-plane components and support a gapless single-spin qubit encoding.
application_angle: The framework provides concrete device-design knobs for more uniform, tunable, and scalable germanium spin-qubit hardware.

title: Source-independent quantum key distribution without pre-sending entanglement
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) theoretically offers information-theoretic security. The prevailing approach is the prepare-and-measure BB84 protocol, which implements QKD using conventional laser rather than single-photon source via the decoy-state method. However, side-channel attacks targeting sources severely threaten system security. Despite extensive efforts, including fully passive scheme, this vulnerability persists even with perfect single-photon source. Here, we propose a source-independent (SI) QKD protocol that resolves all known and unknown source-side attacks without pre-sending entanglement source. Aligning with advances in quantum light sources, our protocol simultaneously doubles the transmission distance while remaining robustness against imperfection of source. Theoretical analysis shows that non-classical light source provides practical security advantages unattainable with conventional laser.
one_liner: A source-independent QKD protocol removes source-side trust assumptions without requiring pre-distributed entanglement.
why_it_matters: It targets a major practical QKD vulnerability, promising longer-distance secure links even with imperfect real-world quantum light sources.
detailed_summary: This work proposes a source-independent quantum key distribution protocol that does not rely on pre-sending an entangled source. The scheme is designed to close both known and unknown source-side attack channels that remain problematic for BB84-style prepare-and-measure systems. Theoretical analysis indicates that the protocol can roughly double transmission distance while remaining robust to source imperfections. It also argues that non-classical light sources yield practical security benefits beyond what conventional laser sources can provide.
main_result: The protocol achieves source-independent security without pre-shared entanglement and is predicted to extend transmission distance by about a factor of two.
application_angle: It points toward deployable QKD systems with stronger source-side security and better range using realistic photonic hardware.

title: Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Solutions for Transport under 3D Spatially Varying Advection on Trapped Ion Hardware
abstract: The Quantum Lattice Boltzmann Method (QLBM) has emerged as one of the most promising quantum computing approaches for the numerical simulation of problems in computational fluid dynamics (CFD). The dynamics is formulated in terms of mesoscopic particle distribution functions governed by a discrete Boltzmann transport equation, comprising local streaming and collision operations. In this work, the resulting macroscopic behavior corresponds to the advection-diffusion equation, which we adopt as a canonical model problem for transport phenomena. Building upon recent progress in QLBM implementations, we advance towards more realistic problem settings that better reflect conventional CFD requirements. We address, for the first time, transport under the action of non uniform velocity fields on quantum hardware. We implement our demonstration using IonQ's trapped-ion systems including Forte generation systems and a 64-qubit Barium development system similar to the forthcoming IonQ Tempo line. We identify the density readout and subsequent reloading of the fluid density as a potential bottleneck of the current algorithm and discuss several approaches to mitigate this bottleneck. We identify the use of MPS shadow tomography as a promising method to efficiently scale the readout to large system with complex density distributions. Lastly, we introduce and simulate a novel method to implement wall boundaries for advection-diffusion in QLBM, and discuss the prospects of scaling to higher-complexity problems.
one_liner: Quantum lattice Boltzmann transport with spatially varying 3D advection is demonstrated on trapped-ion quantum hardware.
why_it_matters: It pushes hardware-level quantum CFD toward more realistic transport problems and identifies concrete scaling bottlenecks in readout and state reloading.
detailed_summary: The paper extends quantum lattice Boltzmann methods to transport under nonuniform three-dimensional velocity fields, a more realistic regime than prior uniform-flow demonstrations. The implementation runs on IonQ trapped-ion systems, including Forte-generation hardware and a 64-qubit barium development platform. The authors identify density readout and reloading as key algorithmic bottlenecks and discuss mitigation strategies, especially MPS shadow tomography for scalable density reconstruction. They also introduce and simulate a new wall-boundary implementation for advection-diffusion within QLBM.
main_result: The work demonstrates QLBM advection-diffusion with spatially varying 3D flow fields on trapped-ion hardware and proposes scalable readout and boundary-handling methods.
application_angle: It advances quantum hardware toward practical simulation of transport and fluid problems relevant to engineering and sensing environments.

title: Nodal algebraic curves and entropy diagnostics in degenerate two-dimensional harmonic-oscillator shells
abstract: Degenerate quantum eigenspaces can support substantial changes in nodal geometry at fixed energy. We show that, for the two-dimensional isotropic harmonic oscillator, this restructuring is organized by the Hermite-constrained algebraic curve $P_N(x,y)=0$ appearing in every real shell state, $ψ_N=e^{-αr^2/2}P_N(x,y)$. Finite singularities, $P_N=\nabla P_N=0$, and projective degeneracies of the leading homogeneous part identify the strata where topology-changing events can occur. We combine these criteria with entropy diagnostics: the nodal-domain entropy $S_{\mathrm{dom}}$, Cartesian mutual information $I(x;y)$, and the entropic uncertainty sum $S_r+S_p$. The first three shells reveal a hierarchy: $N=1$ only rotates a nodal line; $N=2$ has a conic transition at $b^2=2ac$, sharply detected by $S_{\mathrm{dom}}$ but not by global entropies; and $N=3$ supports cubic close-branch regimes organized by the projective discriminant, with enhanced responses in $S_{\mathrm{dom}}$ and $I(x;y)$. Thus algebraic stratification, rather than spectral ordering, organizes nodal geometry inside a degenerate eigenspace, while entropy diagnostics quantify probability redistribution and correlation. The framework suggests experimentally reconstructible signatures for real-phase Hermite--Gaussian structured light and approximately isotropic trapped motional systems.
one_liner: Algebraic-curve structure and entropy metrics reveal topology changes in nodal patterns of degenerate 2D oscillator shells.
why_it_matters: It offers experimentally accessible diagnostics for structured-light and trapped-mode systems where nodal geometry encodes useful physical information.
detailed_summary: The authors analyze real shell states of the degenerate two-dimensional isotropic harmonic oscillator through the algebraic curve defined by the Hermite polynomial factor. They show that finite singularities and projective degeneracies organize topology-changing nodal events within a fixed-energy shell. Entropy-based observables, especially nodal-domain entropy and Cartesian mutual information, track these transitions with shell-dependent sensitivity. The framework is explicitly connected to reconstructible signatures in real-phase Hermite-Gaussian optical modes and approximately isotropic trapped motional systems.
main_result: The study shows that algebraic stratification, rather than energy ordering, governs nodal-geometry transitions, and that specific entropy diagnostics can detect them.
application_angle: The results provide analysis tools for photonic mode engineering and experimental reconstruction of structured-light field topology.

title: Optimal current-based sensing of phonon temperature using a finite reservoir
abstract: In realistic nanoscale transport set-ups, electron-phonon coupling leads to the exchange of heat between phonon baths and electronic reservoirs with finite heat capacities. Such exchange affects the finite reservoir's temperature. However, this sensitivity of the finite reservoir temperature to the exchange of heat with the finite reservoir has remained unexplored for thermometry. Here, we fill this gap by combining current metrology techniques with a thermodynamic framework encompassing finite reservoirs. We focus on an experimentally realizable set-up with a quantum dot coupled to a finite reservoir and consider two distinct current-based strategies in the long time limit, namely monitoring quanta exchanged between the quantum dot and finite reservoir and the measurement of the total current flowing from the quantum dot into an infinite reservoir. A third strategy involves measurements of the quantum dot occupation. For a large but finite reservoir, we show that the Fisher information for all three strategies captures the finite reservoir's contribution to sensitivity through common factors. We also demonstrate that monitoring quanta exchanged between the system and finite reservoir in the long time limit achieves optimal precision. Finally, we provide an optimization analysis that explores how maximal precision can be achieved within each of the current-based strategies by tuning the gate voltage.
one_liner: Current measurements can optimally estimate phonon-bath temperature when the coupled electronic reservoir has finite heat capacity.
why_it_matters: It turns finite-reservoir effects from a nuisance into a thermometry resource for nanoscale devices with electron-phonon heat exchange.
detailed_summary: This work studies a quantum dot coupled to a finite reservoir and evaluates three long-time thermometry strategies based on exchanged quanta, output current, and dot occupation. Within a finite-reservoir thermodynamic framework, the Fisher information for all strategies contains common sensitivity factors arising from the reservoir's finite heat capacity. The authors show that directly monitoring quanta exchanged with the finite reservoir achieves optimal precision in the long-time limit. They also optimize sensitivity within each strategy by tuning the gate voltage.
main_result: The paper proves that counting quanta exchanged with a finite reservoir is the optimal current-based strategy for estimating phonon temperature in the long-time regime.
application_angle: It guides the design of nanoscale temperature sensors and transport experiments where finite-reservoir effects cannot be neglected.

title: Observation of Vinen turbulence during far-from-equilibrium Bose-Einstein condensation
abstract: Relaxation of far-from-equilibrium quantum fluids, intimately related to the emergence of long-range order, is theoretically associated with the decay of a turbulent isotropic tangle of vortex lines. We observe and study such decaying quantum turbulence in a homogeneous 3D atomic Bose gas. Using matter-wave techniques to magnify the gas density distribution, and then imaging a thin slice of the magnified cloud, we observe imprints of randomly oriented vortex lines and measure the vortex line-length density $\mathcal{L}$. The observed decay of $\mathcal{L}$ agrees with the prediction for Vinen `ultraquantum' turbulence. Although our weakly interacting gases are highly compressible, their large-scale dynamics are consistent with the behavior of an incompressible hydrodynamic fluid, with the decay of $\mathcal{L}$ not depending on the strength of the interatomic interactions and being similar to that in the strongly interacting superfluid helium.
one_liner: A homogeneous 3D atomic Bose gas directly reveals Vinen turbulence during far-from-equilibrium condensation.
why_it_matters: It provides a clean experimental window into universal quantum-turbulence decay, informing quantum-fluid control and nonequilibrium matter studies.
detailed_summary: The experiment observes decaying quantum turbulence during far-from-equilibrium Bose-Einstein condensation in a homogeneous three-dimensional atomic Bose gas. Using matter-wave magnification and thin-slice imaging, the authors resolve signatures of randomly oriented vortex lines and extract the vortex line-length density. The measured decay of this density matches the prediction for Vinen ultraquantum turbulence. Despite the gas being weakly interacting and highly compressible, its large-scale decay dynamics resemble incompressible hydrodynamics and are largely interaction-independent.
main_result: The authors directly observe vortex-tangle decay consistent with Vinen turbulence during nonequilibrium Bose-Einstein condensation.
application_angle: The measurement techniques and universal decay behavior are relevant for diagnosing and controlling turbulent quantum-fluid platforms used in precision experiments.

title: On-chip levitation of ferromagnetic microparticles
abstract: Levitation of microscopic objects in vacuum combines exceptional environmental isolation with precise control of their dynamics, pushing the limits of sensing and macroscopic quantum physics. In particular, magnetic levitation allows a large range of particle sizes, while avoiding detrimental effects from high-intensity optical trapping beams and electric field noise. However, existing diamagnetic and Meissner levitation approaches are typically constrained by low mechanical eigenfrequencies, limited integrability with other systems due to bulky coils or magnets, and, for Meissner levitation, the need for cryogenic operation. Here, we demonstrate a room-temperature on-chip magnetic levitation platform capable of stably levitating a nanogram (6.5 micrometer radius) ferromagnetic microsphere. The platform is scalable and tunable, and supports librational modes with eigenfrequencies exceeding 10 kHz. Further miniaturization and coupling to solid-state spin qubits could enable cooling to the quantum ground state. Beyond quantum experiments, this architecture enables integrated precision sensing and studies of isolated ferromagnet thermodynamics.
one_liner: A room-temperature chip platform magnetically levitates a ferromagnetic microsphere with >10 kHz librational modes.
why_it_matters: It offers an integrated, cryogen-free route to isolated mechanical devices for precision sensing and future spin-mechanics quantum experiments.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates stable on-chip magnetic levitation of a nanogram-scale ferromagnetic sphere with 6.5 micrometer radius at room temperature. Unlike optical or Meissner-based trapping, the platform avoids trapping-light heating and cryogenic requirements while maintaining tunability and scalability. The reported librational eigenfrequencies exceed 10 kHz, which is favorable for control and sensing. The authors argue that further miniaturization and coupling to solid-state spins could enable ground-state cooling and hybrid quantum devices.
main_result: The authors stably levitate a 6.5 micrometer-radius ferromagnetic microsphere on chip at room temperature with librational modes above 10 kHz.
application_angle: The architecture is directly relevant to integrated inertial or magnetic sensing and could evolve into chip-scale hybrid quantum transducers.

title: Scalable spin-nematic squeezing in multi-level dipole-interacting Rydberg atom arrays
abstract: We study the generation of metrologically useful entanglement in a three-level (spin-1) system naturally realized in arrays of dipole-interacting Rydberg atoms confined in optical tweezers. In the spin-quadrupolar operator basis, the interaction Hamiltonian decomposes into effective SU(2) subspaces, within which quench dynamics from product initial states generate scalable spin-nematic squeezing. For symmetric interactions, we identify a mapping to effective one-axis twisting within bright and dark manifolds and demonstrate that the squeezing parameter scales as $ξ^{2}\propto N^{-2/3}$ ($ξ^{2}\propto N^{-0.5}$) with system size for all-to-all (two-dimensional dipolar) couplings. In both cases the quantum Fisher information reaches $F_Q\propto N^{2}$. For antisymmetric interactions supplemented by a microwave drive we find a distinct two-axis countertwisting mechanism. This results in squeezing $ξ^{2}\propto N^{-0.7}$ for all-to-all interactions and moderate squeezing for dipolar interactions in 2D. Our results constitute a first theoretical step beyond the well-studied qubit setting toward scalable entanglement generation in qudit systems with dipolar interactions, directly relevant to current Rydberg tweezer experiments.
one_liner: Rydberg spin-1 atom arrays can generate scalable spin-nematic squeezing with near-Heisenberg metrological scaling.
why_it_matters: It extends entanglement generation beyond qubits to experimentally relevant qudit Rydberg platforms with strong metrology potential.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes three-level Rydberg atom arrays in the spin-quadrupolar basis and shows that their dipolar interactions decompose into effective SU(2) sectors that support squeezing dynamics. For symmetric couplings, the dynamics map to one-axis twisting in bright and dark manifolds, yielding squeezing that scales as N^-2/3 for all-to-all interactions and N^-0.5 for 2D dipolar couplings, with F_Q proportional to N^2. For antisymmetric interactions plus microwave driving, a distinct two-axis-countertwisting-like mechanism gives N^-0.7 squeezing under all-to-all coupling. These results provide a concrete many-body route to scalable entanglement in multilevel Rydberg hardware.
main_result: The authors show that realistic multilevel Rydberg arrays can produce scalable spin-nematic squeezing and quadratic quantum Fisher information scaling.
application_angle: The protocol is relevant for Rydberg-based quantum metrology and for using qudit atomic arrays as entanglement-enhanced quantum processors.

title: Provable and scalable quantum Gaussian processes for quantum learning
abstract: Despite rapid recent advances in quantum machine learning, the field is in many ways stuck. Existing approaches can exhibit serious limitations, and we still lack learning frameworks that are simple, interpretable, scalable, and naturally suited to quantum data. To address this, here we introduce quantum Gaussian processes, a Bayesian framework for learning from quantum systems through priors over unknown quantum transformations. We show that, under suitable conditions, unitary quantum stochastic processes define Gaussian processes, thereby enabling regression, classification, and Bayesian optimization directly on quantum data. The key ingredient in this framework is sufficient knowledge of a quantum process's structure and symmetries to define an informative prior through its corresponding quantum kernel, effectively injecting a strong, physics-informed inductive bias into the learning model. We then prove that matchgate, or free-fermionic, evolutions give rise to provable and scalable quantum Gaussian processes, providing the first family in our framework where the unknown unitary acts non-trivially on all qubits. Finally, we demonstrate accurate long-range extrapolation, phase-diagram learning in many-body systems, and sample-efficient Bayesian optimization in a quantum sensing task. Our results identify quantum Gaussian processes as a promising route toward simpler and more structured forms of quantum learning.
one_liner: Quantum Gaussian processes provide a Bayesian learning framework for structured quantum data with provable scalable cases.
why_it_matters: It gives quantum experiments a more interpretable and sample-efficient learning tool, including for sensing and many-body characterization.
detailed_summary: The authors define quantum Gaussian processes by placing Gaussian-process priors over unknown quantum transformations, using symmetry-aware quantum kernels to encode physics-informed inductive bias. They prove that matchgate or free-fermionic evolutions generate a family of such models that remains scalable even when the unknown unitary acts nontrivially across all qubits. Numerically and analytically, they demonstrate regression, classification, long-range extrapolation, phase-diagram learning, and Bayesian optimization. A highlighted use case is sample-efficient optimization in a quantum sensing setting, showing practical utility beyond abstract QML proposals.
main_result: The paper establishes provable scalable quantum Gaussian processes for free-fermionic dynamics and demonstrates accurate learning and optimization on quantum tasks.
application_angle: This framework could reduce measurement overhead in sensor tuning, experiment calibration, and many-body system identification by exploiting structured priors.

title: From Tensor Networks to Tractable Circuits, and back
abstract: Tensor networks and circuits are widely used data structures to represent pseudo-Boolean functions. These two formalisms have been studied primarily in separate communities, and this paper aims to establish equivalences between them. We show that some classes of tensor networks that are appealing in practice correspond to classes of circuits with specific properties that have been studied in knowledge compilation as \emph{tractable circuits}. In particular, we prove that matrix product states (tensor trains) coincide with nondeterministic edge-valued decision diagrams and that tree tensor networks exactly correspond to structured-decomposable circuits. These correspondences enable direct transfer of structural and algorithmic results; for example, canonicity and tractability guarantees known for circuits yield analogous guarantees for the associated tensor networks, and vice versa.
one_liner: This work proves exact correspondences between common tensor-network classes and tractable circuit families.
why_it_matters: The mapping transfers canonicity and efficient inference results between two major representations used in quantum and photonic computation.
detailed_summary: The paper establishes formal equivalences between tensor-network representations of pseudo-Boolean functions and tractable circuits from knowledge compilation. In particular, matrix product states are shown to coincide with nondeterministic edge-valued decision diagrams, while tree tensor networks exactly match structured-decomposable circuits. These identifications let algorithmic guarantees move in both directions, including canonicity and tractable evaluation properties. The result is primarily theoretical but directly relevant to simulation and representation choices in quantum information workflows.
main_result: The authors prove exact class-by-class correspondences linking matrix product states and tree tensor networks to specific tractable circuit families.
application_angle: The equivalence can improve classical tooling for quantum-circuit simulation, verification, and compact modeling of photonic or many-body states.

title: Cryogenic Graphene-Based Phase Modulators for Quantum Information Processing
abstract: Electro-optic modulators are key components for photonic quantum computing, particularly in fully cryovenic integrated platforms where low loss and compactness are critical. We present a systematic theoretical investigation of compact dual-layer graphene (DSLG) electro-optic phase modulators integrated on silicon nitride waveguides, with emphasis on cryogenic operation. By combining electromagnetic simulations with a physically consistent description of graphene conductivity based on the Kybo formalism, we analyze the interplay between electrostatic tuning, optical mode confinement, and material-dependent losses. We show that cryogenic operation enhances device performance by sharpening the Fermi-Dirac distribution, enabling access to the Pauli-blocking regime at lower Fermi levels and reducing the required modulation length. Through optimization of the waveguide geometry, dielectric spacer thickness and permittivity, and graphene quality, we identify regimes that simultaneously minimize insertion loss and device footprint under realistic voltage constraints. The optimized designs achieve near-pure phase modulation with insertion losses below 0.3 dB and modulation lengths below 50 um at 10 K, while maintaining GHz-scale bandwidths. These results provide quantitative design guidelines for low-loss, compact, cryogenic graphene phase modulators for scalable integrated quantum photonics.
one_liner: Cryogenic dual-layer graphene modulators on SiN can deliver sub-0.3 dB loss and sub-50 micrometer phase shifting.
why_it_matters: Compact low-loss phase control is a key bottleneck for scalable cryogenic integrated quantum photonics and photonic computing.
detailed_summary: This theoretical study models dual-layer graphene electro-optic phase modulators integrated with silicon nitride waveguides under cryogenic conditions using electromagnetic simulation and a conductivity model based on the Kybo formalism. At 10 K, sharper Fermi-Dirac occupation improves access to the Pauli-blocking regime, lowering the required Fermi level and shortening the modulation length. By optimizing waveguide geometry, dielectric spacer parameters, and graphene quality, the authors identify designs with near-pure phase modulation, insertion loss below 0.3 dB, lengths below 50 micrometers, and GHz-scale bandwidth. The analysis provides quantitative design rules for cryogenic photonic integrated circuits.
main_result: Optimized cryogenic graphene phase modulators are predicted to achieve low-loss GHz-scale phase modulation in footprints under 50 micrometers at 10 K.
application_angle: These devices could enable dense cryogenic reconfigurable photonic circuits for quantum interconnects, feedforward, and photonic quantum processors.

title: Comment on "Quantum teleportation, entanglement, LQU and LQFI in $e^{+} e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y} \overline{\mathrm{Y}}$ processes at BESIII through noisy channels''
abstract: We provide a critical assessment of a recent study applying quantum information concepts, including noisy channels and teleportation fidelity, to hyperon-antihyperon pairs produced in $e^{+}e^{-} \to Y\bar Y$ reactions at BESIII. While the spin density matrix reconstructed from experimental data provides a physically meaningful description of production correlations, we argue that its subsequent interpretation in terms of standard decoherence models-such as amplitude damping, phase damping, and phase flip-lacks a clear physical correspondence for these systems. The produced particles emerge from a single scattering event and propagate as free, unstable relativistic states, without a well-defined system-environment interaction acting on their spin degrees of freedom. As a result, the variation of quantum correlations with an abstract noise parameter does not describe a genuine physical evolution. We further contend that the reported teleportation fidelity should not be interpreted as evidence for operational quantum communication, since hyperon states cannot be prepared, controlled, or measured in a way that would enable a realizable teleportation protocol. More generally, quantities such as logarithmic negativity, local quantum uncertainty, and local quantum Fisher information primarily characterize static production correlations rather than directly usable quantum resources. Our analysis highlights the importance of distinguishing between formal quantum-information measures and their physical interpretation in high-energy particle systems.
one_liner: A critical comment argues that noisy-channel and teleportation interpretations for BESIII hyperon pairs are physically unjustified.
why_it_matters: It clarifies the boundary between formal quantum-correlation metrics and realizable quantum communication resources in particle systems.
detailed_summary: The authors reassess a prior analysis of hyperon-antihyperon pairs from e+e- collisions and argue that standard noisy-channel models such as amplitude damping or phase damping do not correspond to a real system-environment process for these free unstable relativistic spins. They emphasize that the reconstructed spin density matrix is meaningful for production correlations, but varying an abstract noise parameter does not describe physical evolution. The paper also disputes interpreting teleportation fidelity as evidence of operational quantum communication, since preparation, control, and measurement requirements for a teleportation protocol are absent. The comment is a methodological correction about how quantum-information language should be used in high-energy contexts.
main_result: The paper concludes that prior noisy-channel and teleportation-resource claims for BESIII hyperon pairs lack a defensible physical operational basis.
application_angle: For quantum communication research, it helps prevent overstating static correlation measures as deployable communication resources or protocols.

title: Optically detected nuclear magnetic resonance of carbon-13 in bulk diamond
abstract: Precision measurements based on optically detected nuclear magnetic resonance offer exquisite sensitivity to absolute shifts in spin transition frequencies, with potential applications in fundamental physics experiments and inertial sensing. We investigate 13C nuclear spins in diamond as a candidate system for solid-state implementations, which hold the promise for high-fidelity readout of large numbers of coherent nuclear spins in millitesla or lower magnetic fields. We demonstrate a technique that allows for both optical polarization and readout of large ensembles of ~10^{16} polarized nuclear spins. Our method takes advantage of state-selective Landau-Zener transitions under microwave frequency sweeping, which bidirectionally transfer spin polarization between Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) electron spins and remote 13C nuclear spins. Using natural isotopic abundance diamonds with nitrogen densities of ~0.5-10 ppm, we perform optically-detected 13C Ramsey spectroscopy and realize a nuclear-spin-dependent fluorescence contrast exceeding 0.5% peak-to-peak. We observe nuclear spin dephasing times T2*~2 ms that only modestly improve with homonuclear dipolar decoupling, indicating that they are limited by the longitudinal spin relaxation of nearby NV electron spins. We study the magnetic field dependence of the optical readout and find comparable contrast and dephasing times for magnetic fields in the range 8-20 mT. Our method can be interpreted as a type of repetitive readout, where each NV center optically reads out the spin state of ~100 nuclei before nuclear spins depolarize.
one_liner: Bulk diamond OD-NMR uses NV centers to polarize and optically read out about 10^16 carbon-13 nuclear spins at low magnetic field.
why_it_matters: It shows ensemble nuclear-spin readout in a solid-state platform at 8-20 mT, relevant to compact precision sensors and fundamental-physics experiments.
detailed_summary: The work demonstrates optically detected nuclear magnetic resonance of natural-abundance 13C spins in bulk diamond by using microwave-swept, state-selective Landau-Zener transfer between NV electron spins and remote nuclei. The authors polarize and read out ensembles of roughly 10^16 nuclear spins and perform 13C Ramsey spectroscopy with fluorescence contrast above 0.5% peak-to-peak. They measure T2* around 2 ms, with limited improvement under homonuclear decoupling, indicating dephasing set mainly by nearby NV longitudinal relaxation. Comparable readout contrast and coherence are maintained over 8-20 mT fields, consistent with repetitive optical readout of about 100 nuclei per NV before depolarization.
main_result: The main result is optical polarization, Ramsey interrogation, and repetitive fluorescence readout of large 13C ensembles in bulk diamond at millitesla-scale fields.
application_angle: This platform is a practical route to solid-state low-field NMR-based precision sensing, including inertial sensing and frequency-shift measurements in deployable devices.

title: Asymptotic Replacement for Quantum Channel Products with Applications to Inhomogeneous Matrix Product States
abstract: We develop a product-level trace-Dobrushin theory for finite-dimensional quantum channel products and apply it to deterministic and stationary random inhomogeneous matrix product states in left-canonical CPTP gauge. For a product of channels, the centered trace-Dobrushin coefficient quantifies the residual dependence on the input state, and its decay is the criterion for trace-norm forgetting. In the deterministic setting, this decay is equivalent to asymptotic replacement by a moving replacement channel. For two-sided products, pullback forgetting produces a unique boundary state, which determines the canonical replacement family. For stationary random CPTP cocycles, submultiplicativity of the product coefficient yields a trace-Dobrushin Lyapunov exponent. We prove that the almost sure negativity of this exponent is equivalent to quenched trace-norm memory loss and gives exponential forward and pullback convergence to a unique dynamically stationary random replacement channel. When the \(\varrho\)-mixing profile of the channel environment tends to zero, we obtain annealed super-polynomial estimates, while independence gives annealed exponential estimates. Finally, we transfer these estimates to inhomogeneous matrix product states whose auxiliary transfer maps are CPTP. These channel estimates transfer to deterministic and stationary random inhomogeneous MPS, giving infinite-volume limits of trace-closed finite-volume states, quantitative boundary stability, and correlation bounds governed by the same auxiliary product coefficients.
one_liner: A trace-Dobrushin theory for quantum-channel products gives rigorous memory-loss criteria and convergence results for inhomogeneous MPS.
why_it_matters: It provides quantitative stability and correlation bounds for noisy or random tensor-network channels, useful for analyzing realistic quantum many-body and photonic state-generation models.
detailed_summary: The paper develops a product-level trace-Dobrushin framework for finite-dimensional quantum channel products, where decay of a centered coefficient characterizes trace-norm forgetting of the input state. In deterministic settings this is shown equivalent to asymptotic replacement by a moving replacement channel, while for stationary random CPTP cocycles a negative trace-Dobrushin Lyapunov exponent is proved equivalent to quenched memory loss. The authors further derive annealed convergence estimates under mixing or independent environments and transfer these bounds directly to deterministic and random inhomogeneous matrix product states in left-canonical CPTP gauge. This yields infinite-volume limits, boundary-state uniqueness, boundary stability, and correlation bounds controlled by auxiliary transfer-channel products.
main_result: The main result is a rigorous equivalence between decay of channel-product dependence and asymptotic replacement dynamics, extended to random CPTP cocycles and inhomogeneous MPS.
application_angle: These results help certify stability and memory loss in engineered quantum-state preparation pipelines where spatially varying channels or disorder matter.

title: Generation of Tunable Entanglement from Thin-Film Lithium Niobate
abstract: Entangled photon pairs play a major role in various modern technologies such as quantum imaging, communication, and computing. Conventional photon-pair sources are often based on spontaneous parametric down-conversion in bulk nonlinear crystals. Recent advances have also shown entangled photon-pairs from transition metal dichalcogenide thin-films, however, these materials are not widely available and are not compatible with existing fabrication capabilities. We present a new thin-film lithium niobate source of polarization-entangled photon pairs at the telecom wavelength that requires no additional optical elements for entanglement generation and allows for easy application using the existing lithium niobate fabrication technologies. We demonstrate tunable entanglement generation using the three-fold rotational crystal symmetry of lithium niobate, allowing the generation of different maximally entangled Bell states or completely separable states depending on the polarization of the pump beam.
one_liner: Thin-film lithium niobate directly generates telecom polarization entanglement with pump-controlled switching between Bell and separable states.
why_it_matters: It turns a fabrication-friendly integrated photonics material into a tunable on-chip entangled-photon source without extra entangling optics.
detailed_summary: The authors propose and demonstrate a polarization-entangled photon-pair source in thin-film lithium niobate operating at telecom wavelengths. Rather than relying on added interferometric or polarization optics, the scheme uses the three-fold rotational symmetry of lithium niobate so that the pump polarization sets the generated two-photon state. By tuning the pump, the device can emit different maximally entangled Bell states or fully separable states. The approach is naturally compatible with established lithium-niobate fabrication workflows, making it suitable for integrated implementations.
main_result: The main result is direct generation of tunable telecom-band polarization entanglement in thin-film lithium niobate without auxiliary entanglement-forming elements.
application_angle: This is attractive for scalable quantum photonic chips for communication, imaging, and processing because it combines entanglement generation with a mature integrated material platform.

title: Sequential Measurements as a Resource for Quantum Metrology
abstract: We present a protocol in which sequential weak measurements of a quantum harmonic oscillator enable simultaneous estimation of both quadratures of a displacement channel. Calculations of the quantum Fisher information show that the measurement backaction can increase the information gained for a range of measurement strengths. The protocol distributes information over a $N$-bit string after $N$ weak measurements. Thus we find that post-processing can be used to avoid information loss due to phase wrapping, increasing the effective dynamic range. Finally, the periodic information extraction makes the protocol robust to decoherence. Our results establish mid-sensing measurement as a resource for single- and multi-parameter quantum metrology.
one_liner: Sequential weak measurements of an oscillator can improve joint displacement estimation, extend dynamic range, and add decoherence robustness.
why_it_matters: It identifies mid-sensing measurement backaction and time-resolved post-processing as usable resources for practical multi-parameter quantum metrology.
detailed_summary: This work studies repeated weak measurements on a quantum harmonic oscillator for simultaneous estimation of both displacement quadratures. Quantum Fisher information analysis shows that for a finite range of measurement strengths, the backaction itself can increase the recoverable information rather than only degrading it. Information is spread across an N-bit record from N sequential measurements, enabling post-processing that mitigates phase wrapping and therefore enlarges effective dynamic range. Because the sensing information is extracted periodically, the protocol is also more resilient to decoherence during the interrogation window.
main_result: The main result is a metrology protocol where sequential weak measurements improve joint quadrature estimation and preserve information over a larger dynamic range.
application_angle: The method is relevant to sensors that need continuous or long-duration readout under decoherence, such as force or displacement sensing with bosonic modes.

title: Toward Heisenberg-Limited Interferometry with Dual Squeezers
abstract: The canonical Mach-Zehnder interferometer fed with a coherent state and a squeezed-vacuum state of equal intensities is theoretically predicted to achieve Heisenberg scaling in phase sensitivity. However, this ultimate performance is unattainable using direct photon-number-difference detection due to a divergence arising precisely at the optimal equal-intensity regime. In this work, we introduce a dual-squeezing approach that overcomes this fundamental limitation. Our scheme employs an additional single-mode squeezer before detection, forming a paired configuration with the input squeezer used to generate the squeezed-vacuum state. We analytically demonstrate that the resulting dual-squeezing Mach-Zehnder interferometer enables Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity with di rect photon-number-difference detection, while remaining robust against detection noise. Our work provides a feasible and robust route toward quantum-limited interferometric phase measurements
one_liner: A dual-squeezer Mach-Zehnder design restores Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity with direct photon-number-difference detection.
why_it_matters: It removes a key readout pathology at the equal-intensity optimum and makes quantum-enhanced interferometry more experimentally viable.
detailed_summary: The paper addresses the divergence that prevents standard coherent-plus-squeezed-vacuum Mach-Zehnder interferometers from reaching their predicted optimum under direct number-difference detection. The proposed solution adds a second single-mode squeezer before detection, paired with the input squeezer that generates the squeezed vacuum. Analytical treatment shows that this dual-squeezing architecture recovers Heisenberg-limited phase sensitivity at the equal-intensity operating point. The scheme is also shown to remain robust against detection noise, which is critical for realistic photonic interferometers.
main_result: The main result is that an added output squeezer enables Heisenberg-limited phase estimation with direct photon-number-difference detection in a practical interferometric setup.
application_angle: This offers a more implementable path to ultra-precise optical phase sensing for metrology platforms constrained by realistic detectors.

title: From quantum storage to amplification: the effect of unwanted couplings and an additional level in cavity-based ensemble quantum memories
abstract: Quantum-memory models often reduce complex level structures to an idealized $Λ$ system, potentially missing nearby levels and unwanted couplings that can qualitatively alter the predicted performance. Here, we study an extension of a cavity-based $Λ$-type ensemble memory, a four-level model with unwanted couplings from both the control field and signal, using a fully quantum treatment. We derive explicit expressions for the single-photon storage efficiency, retrieval efficiency, and fidelity, and on this basis identify three distinct dynamical regimes: stable, threshold, and unstable. Within the stable regime, we additionally discriminate between two qualitatively different sub-regimes. Applying the theory to warm-vapor-inspired parameters, we determine the conditions under which the system can still operate as a high-quality quantum memory. More generally, our results provide a practical framework for distinguishing genuine memory operation from amplification and for optimizing realistic quantum memories beyond idealized models.
one_liner: A four-level cavity-ensemble memory model maps when unwanted couplings preserve storage, drive threshold behavior, or turn the device into an amplifier.
why_it_matters: It gives a realistic design framework for separating true quantum-memory operation from parasitic gain in cavity-assisted ensemble memories.
detailed_summary: The authors extend the usual idealized cavity-based Lambda-memory description to a four-level model that includes an extra level plus unwanted couplings from both the control and signal fields. Using a fully quantum treatment, they derive explicit expressions for single-photon storage efficiency, retrieval efficiency, and fidelity, then classify the dynamics into stable, threshold, and unstable regimes, with two distinct sub-regimes inside the stable region. Applying the model to warm-vapor-inspired parameters, they identify when high-quality memory action survives despite nonideal couplings. The analysis provides a concrete criterion for distinguishing genuine storage from gain-assisted amplification.
main_result: The main result is a quantitative regime map and performance formulas showing how extra levels and parasitic couplings alter single-photon storage, retrieval, and fidelity.
application_angle: This directly supports engineering of practical quantum repeater and networking memories, where imperfect level structures and stray couplings are unavoidable.

title: Suppressing Plasmonic Heating in Aqueous Environments with Hexagonal Boron Nitride
abstract: Optical heating of plasmonic nanostructures is a critical challenge in nanoscale systems. Although plasmonic effects enable enhanced optical functionalities, the associated temperature rise can degrade performance in heat-sensitive applications such as biosensing, nanophotonics, and microelectronics. Conventional cooling strategies fail at these scales due to limited heat transport and high interfacial thermal resistance, motivating the integration of advanced materials for thermal management. Here, we investigate hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) thin flakes as heat spreaders to mitigate plasmonic heating of gold nanospheres immobilized on hBN deposited on glass and surrounded by water. Using finite-element simulations, we quantify the influence of hBN thickness, in-plane thermal conductivity, and interfacial thermal conductance on cooling efficiency. Complementary experiments employ cross-grating wavefront microscopy (CGM) for nanothermometry to map the temperature around optically heated gold nanoparticles and quantify the cooling effect of hBN. We extend the application of CGM for rapid, non-invasive, and all-optical characterization of non-absorbing 2D materials. Our results reveal a strong thickness dependence, where heat dissipation in thin flakes is limited by the heat capacity of hBN and in thick flakes by interfacial thermal conductance. Including hBN, we obtain a reduction in temperature rise by up to 60% compared to glass. In addition, the presence of two main heat dissipation pathways emerges: a direct one from the nanoparticle to the hBN and an indirect one from the particle via water to the hBN. This combined simulation-experiment framework offers a versatile approach to improve thermal management in plasmonic systems and beyond, establishing design guidelines for integrating 2D materials into thermally sensitive platforms such as biosensors and integrated circuits.
one_liner: hBN thin flakes cut optically induced heating of gold plasmonic nanoparticles in water by up to 60% via nanoscale heat spreading.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical thermal-management route for plasmonic biosensors and nanophotonic devices where heating usually limits stability and sensitivity.
detailed_summary: The work combines finite-element thermal simulations with cross-grating wavefront microscopy to study optically heated gold nanospheres immobilized on hBN-coated glass in aqueous surroundings. The authors resolve how cooling depends on hBN thickness, in-plane thermal conductivity, and interfacial thermal conductance, identifying distinct thin-flake heat-capacity and thick-flake interface-limited regimes. Experiments validate up to 60% lower temperature rise versus glass alone and reveal both direct nanoparticle-to-hBN and indirect water-mediated heat-dissipation channels. The study also extends CGM as a non-contact optical method for characterizing non-absorbing 2D materials.
platform_or_system: gold nanospheres on hBN in water
main_result: Integrating hBN beneath plasmonic gold nanoparticles reduces optical temperature rise by as much as 60% compared with glass substrates.
application_angle: The design rules can be used to build less thermally disruptive plasmonic sensors, nanophotonic elements, and optically active microelectronic interfaces.

title: Suppressing spin qubit decoherence during shuttling via confinement modulation
abstract: Reliable long-range qubit shuttling is a powerful tool for scalable quantum computing architectures. We investigate strategies to improve the coherence of moving spin qubits by performing continuous dynamical decoupling by modulating their confinement potential. Specifically, we introduce temporal and spatial breathing shuttling protocols that leverage spin-orbit interactions in hole-spin systems to electrically drive the qubit while moving. This enables efficient dressed-state shuttling, where the spin is continuously rotated during transport, suppressing the effect of low-frequency noise. Using the filter function formalism, we identify driving regimes that efficiently mitigate both global and local magnetic and electric noise sources. We find that confinement-modulated shuttling can significantly enhance coherence during transport, while revealing distinct limitations depending on the correlation length of the noise. Applying our framework to germanium hole-spin qubits, we show that these protocols provide a practical route toward noise-resilient long-range coherent quantum links.
one_liner: Confinement-modulated shuttling uses spin-orbit-driven dressed rotations to preserve coherence of moving hole-spin qubits during transport.
why_it_matters: Noise-resilient long-range spin shuttling is a key missing ingredient for scalable semiconductor quantum processors with distributed qubit layouts.
detailed_summary: The paper proposes temporal and spatial breathing protocols that modulate the confinement potential of moving hole-spin qubits to implement continuous dynamical decoupling during shuttling. Using filter-function analysis, the authors map operating regimes where dressed-state transport suppresses low-frequency magnetic and electric noise, including both global and local noise sources. Applied to germanium hole-spin qubits, the framework shows substantial coherence gains while clarifying limits set by noise correlation length. The approach is fully electrical and leverages intrinsic spin-orbit coupling rather than requiring separate control hardware during transport.
main_result: Continuous confinement modulation can maintain spin coherence during qubit transport by electrically driving dressed-state shuttling that filters low-frequency noise.
application_angle: This provides a realistic path to coherent long-range links between semiconductor qubit modules, improving routing and architecture flexibility in large-scale processors.

title: Learning Lindblad Dynamics of a Superconducting Quantum Processor
abstract: Accurate models of quantum processors are essential for understanding, calibrating, and improving their performance. In practice, model construction must balance physical detail against the experimental and computational effort required to reliably learn parameters. Compact descriptions therefore often rely on assumptions about which interactions, noise processes, or hidden degrees of freedom are relevant. Here we introduce LIMINAL, a data-driven framework for testing such assumptions and selecting minimal adequate Lindblad models. LIMINAL fits nested candidate models to time-resolved tomographic data and uses likelihood-ratio tests to decide when added physical mechanisms are warranted. We apply LIMINAL to a five-qubit superconducting processor, identifying an idling model with three-local Hamiltonian terms and two-local dissipation, while finding no support for three-local dissipation. We further apply it to recover driven single-qubit Hamiltonians, reconstruct a shaped-pulse Hamiltonian without assuming an analytic pulse model, and test hidden-qubit extensions in coupler-mediated dynamics, demonstrating the applicability of the framework for a wide range of tasks.
one_liner: LIMINAL learns minimal adequate Lindblad models for a five-qubit superconducting processor directly from time-resolved tomography.
why_it_matters: Better compact processor models improve calibration, control, and diagnosis without overfitting nonexistent physics or hidden degrees of freedom.
detailed_summary: LIMINAL is a nested-model inference framework that fits candidate Lindblad descriptions to time-resolved tomographic data and uses likelihood-ratio tests to decide which physical mechanisms are justified. On a five-qubit superconducting device, it identifies an idling model requiring three-local Hamiltonian terms and two-local dissipation, but finds no evidence for three-local dissipation. The method also recovers driven single-qubit Hamiltonians, reconstructs shaped-pulse Hamiltonians without assuming analytic pulse forms, and tests hidden-qubit extensions in coupler-mediated dynamics. This makes it a broadly useful system-identification tool for processor characterization.
main_result: The framework extracts statistically justified Lindblad models from experimental data, including an idling model with three-local Hamiltonian structure and two-local dissipation.
application_angle: It can reduce calibration overhead and improve predictive hardware models used for pulse design, diagnostics, and control-stack optimization in superconducting quantum chips.

title: An Error-aware and Adaptive Method for the Estimation of Quantum Observables on Qudit-Based Quantum Computers
abstract: The accurate estimation of observables is a crucial task in quantum computing. Recent advances have highlighted the need for (a) specialized protocols for qudit-based devices, that include (b) error-aware strategies. Here, we present AQUIRE, the first protocol that can (a) accurately estimate both the mean and the error of an observable on qudit-based quantum computers. AQUIRE achieves this by constructing a Bayesian model to accommodate generalized Pauli operators. It is designed to continuously monitor the estimated average and the associated error of the observable, adjusting the subsequent measurements in real-time. Additionally, AQUIRE is (b) device- and experiment-specific error-aware, and accounts for hardware imperfections and experimental noise during the estimation process. We demonstrate AQUIRE's advantage via numerical simulations and showcase its ability to quantify the noise affecting the estimation by implementing it on a trapped-ion qudit quantum processor. By exploiting general commutation relations and overlap grouping measurements, our protocol is state-of-the-art when restricted to qubit-based quantum computers and extends this advantage to the qudit case.
one_liner: AQUIRE adaptively estimates qudit observables and uncertainties while accounting for hardware-specific noise in real time.
why_it_matters: Qudit processors need native measurement protocols; this gives them an error-aware observable-estimation method rather than qubit-only approximations.
detailed_summary: AQUIRE builds a Bayesian estimation framework for generalized Pauli observables on qudit quantum computers, tracking both the mean value and uncertainty during measurement acquisition. It adaptively chooses subsequent measurements in real time and incorporates device- and experiment-specific noise into the estimation loop. The authors benchmark the protocol in simulation and implement it on a trapped-ion qudit processor to quantify noise affecting observable estimation. By exploiting commutation relations and overlap-based grouping, it extends state-of-the-art adaptive estimation methods beyond qubits.
main_result: AQUIRE provides adaptive, noise-aware estimation of both observable expectation values and error bars on qudit hardware.
application_angle: The method can improve resource efficiency and trustworthiness of measurements in qudit algorithms, variational workloads, and hardware benchmarking experiments.

title: All-optical saddle trap as a platform for mesoscopic quantum experiments
abstract: We investigate the quantum dynamics of a levitated nanoparticle in a structured light rotating saddle-like optical potential consisting of a superposition of Gaussian and Laguerre-Gauss modes with detuned frequencies. This rotating saddle trap offers unique opportunities for quantum experiments, such as reduced decoherence due to photon recoil and absorption, the possibility of large delocalization of the particle's center-of-mass motion, particle recovery protocols, the generation of motional entanglement and momentum squeezing. As an application, we show that this saddle-trap architecture enables force detection with sensitivity in the zepto-Newton regime.
one_liner: A rotating all-optical saddle trap for levitated nanoparticles enables low-decoherence mesoscopic quantum control and zeptonewton force sensing.
why_it_matters: Levitated platforms are promising for macroscopic quantum tests and precision metrology, and this trap geometry improves both coherence and sensing reach.
detailed_summary: The paper analyzes a levitated nanoparticle confined in a rotating saddle potential formed by superposing Gaussian and Laguerre-Gauss optical modes with detuned frequencies. This structured-light architecture reduces decoherence from photon recoil and absorption while allowing large center-of-mass delocalization, recovery protocols, motional entanglement, and momentum squeezing. The authors identify it as a favorable platform for mesoscopic quantum experiments because the trap geometry simultaneously supports control and isolation. As a concrete application, they predict force detection sensitivity in the zeptonewton regime.
platform_or_system: levitated nanoparticle in rotating optical saddle trap
main_result: The proposed all-optical rotating saddle trap supports low-decoherence mesoscopic dynamics and enables predicted force sensitivities at the zeptonewton scale.
application_angle: It could serve as a practical architecture for ultraprecise inertial or force sensors and for experiments probing quantum behavior of massive objects.

title: Combined spatially and temporally multiplexed photonic reservoir computer with a diffractively coupled VCSEL-array
abstract: We report and analyse the classification performance of an experimental hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computer based upon a free-space VCSEL array. We demonstrate experimentally the enhancement of spatial-only reservoir operation, featuring the diffractive coupling of lasers in an external cavity, by exploiting up to 88 virtual nodes with time multiplexing. We analyse the dependance of performance on the spatial and virtual node number, and achieve an improvement for both spatial- and temporal-only reservoirs with a reduced test error of 0.026 in a classification task. Further, given the high bandwidth of the non-linear laser transformation, we demonstrate the expansion of a 12 spatial node network to a 968 node network, operating at an input time of 17.6ns, maintaining high processing speed and improving network scalability and performance.
one_liner: A VCSEL-array reservoir computer combines spatial and temporal multiplexing to scale experimental photonic processing up to 968 effective nodes.
why_it_matters: Scalable high-speed photonic reservoirs are relevant for low-latency optical information processing and edge inference with compact laser hardware.
detailed_summary: The authors experimentally demonstrate a hybrid spatio-temporal photonic reservoir computer built from a free-space diffractively coupled VCSEL array in an external cavity. Time multiplexing adds up to 88 virtual nodes to improve on purely spatial operation, reducing classification test error to 0.026 and outperforming spatial-only and temporal-only configurations.
